Describe the derived features that distinguish primates from other animals

knowledge_units: Vertebrates are among the most recognizable organisms of the animal kingdom.
More than 62,000 vertebrate species have been identified.
The vertebrate species now living represent only a small portion of the vertebrates that have existed.
Dinosaurs were a unique group of reptiles that were the dominant terrestrial animals for 150 million years.
Modern fishes include an estimated 31,000 species.
Fishes were the earliest vertebrates, and jawless fishes were the earliest of these.
Jawless fishes have a distinct cranium and complex sense organs including eyes, distinguishing them from invertebrate chordates.
Jawed fishes evolved later and are extraordinarily diverse today.
Jawless fishes are craniates that represent an ancient vertebrate lineage.
Hagfishes are eel-like scavengers that live on the ocean floor and feed on dead organisms.
Hagfishes have slime glands that release mucus to help them escape predators.
The skeleton of a hagfish is composed of cartilage, including a cartilaginous notochord and a skull.
Hagfishes are not vertebrates because they do not replace the notochord with a vertebral column during development.
Lampreys have a brain case and incomplete vertebrae, unlike hagfishes.
Lampreys are characterized by a toothed, funnel-like sucking mouth as adults.
Some lamprey species are parasitic as adults, attaching to and feeding on the body fluids of fish.
Gnathostomes are vertebrates that have jaws, which are hinged structures attached to the cranium that allow grasping and tearing of food.
The evolution of jaws allowed early gnathostomes to exploit food resources unavailable to jawless fishes.
Chondrichthyes, the cartilaginous fishes, include sharks, rays, skates, sawfishes, and chimaeras.
Chondrichthyes have paired fins and a skeleton made of cartilage, which is a later development from an ancestral bony skeleton.
Most cartilaginous fishes are marine, and sharks are typically carnivores that use their jaws and teeth to tear prey.
Sharks have well-developed sense organs including keen smell, electroreception via ampullae of Lorenzini, and a lateral line for detecting movement and vibration.
Sharks reproduce sexually with internal fertilization, and most species are ovoviviparous, retaining eggs in the oviduct until hatching.
Rays and skates are closely related to sharks but have flattened bodies, enlarged pectoral fins fused to the head, and ventral gill slits.
Bony fishes are characterized by a bony skeleton.
Bony fishes have an ossified skeleton with specialized bone cells that produce and maintain a calcium phosphate matrix.
The skin of bony fishes is often covered in overlapping scales, and glands in the skin secrete mucus that reduces drag when swimming and aids in osmoregulation.
Bony fishes have a lateral line system that detects vibrations in water.
Bony fishes use gills to breathe, with water drawn over gills located in chambers covered by an operculum.
Bony fishes possess a swim bladder, a gas-filled organ that helps control buoyancy.
Bony fishes are divided into two clades: Actinopterygii (ray-finned fishes) and Sarcopterygii (lobe-finned fishes).
Ray-finned fishes have fins supported by bony spines called rays.
Lobe-finned fishes have fleshy fins supported by bone.
Amphibians are vertebrate tetrapods that include frogs, salamanders, and caecilians.
Amphibians are characterized by a moist, permeable skin that allows cutaneous respiration.
Amphibians can be divided into three clades: Urodela (salamanders and newts), Anura (frogs and toads), and Apoda (caecilians).
Amniotes (reptiles, birds, and mammals) are distinguished from amphibians by their terrestrially adapted shelled egg and embryo protected by amniotic membranes.
The evolution of amniotic membranes allowed embryos to develop within an aquatic environment inside the egg, reducing dependence on water for development.
Bird eggs have hard, brittle shells composed of calcium carbonate with pores for gas and water exchange.
Reptile eggs have more leathery and pliable shells compared to bird eggs.
Reptiles are tetrapods, including limbless species like snakes that descended from four-limbed ancestors.
Reptiles lay shelled eggs on land, with some species exhibiting ovoviviparity or viviparity.
Scaly skin with keratin and waxy lipids prevents water loss, allowing reptiles to live on land.
Reptiles breathe with lungs and excrete nitrogen as uric acid paste to conserve water.
Reptiles are ectotherms that regulate body temperature through behavioral maneuvers.
Class Reptilia includes four living clades: Crocodilia, Sphenodontia, Squamata, and Testudines.
Squamata, the largest reptile clade, includes lizards and snakes, with lizards having limbs, eyelids, and external ears.
Turtles are characterized by a bony or cartilaginous shell composed of a carapace and plastron.
Birds are endothermic, generating their own body heat through metabolic processes.
Feathers are modified reptilian scales and are the most distinctive characteristic of birds.
Feathers serve multiple functions including flight, insulation, and streamlining.
Birds have hollow or pneumatic bones to reduce body weight for flight.
Birds possess a unidirectional airflow system through their lungs for efficient gas exchange.
Mammals are vertebrates characterized by hair and mammary glands.
Hair provides insulation, sensory input, and protective coloration in mammals.
Mammalian skin includes sebaceous glands, sudoriferous glands, and mammary glands.
Most mammals have heterodont teeth and are diphyodonts.
Monotremes are egg-laying mammals that lack teeth and have a single cloaca.
Marsupials give birth to underdeveloped young that typically develop in a pouch.
Eutherians, or placental mammals, have a complex placenta for extended gestation.
Order Primates includes lemurs, tarsiers, monkeys, and apes, which include humans.
All primates have adaptations for climbing trees due to their arboreal heritage.
Primate adaptations for brachiation include a rotating shoulder joint, separated big toes and thumbs for gripping, and stereoscopic vision for depth perception.
Other primate characteristics include relatively large brains, flattened nails instead of claws, typically one offspring per pregnancy, and a trend toward upright posture.
Order Primates is divided into prosimians and anthropoids.
Prosimians include bush babies, lemurs, lorises, pottos, and tarsiers.
Anthropoids include monkeys, lesser apes, and great apes.
Prosimians tend to be nocturnal, smaller in size, and have relatively smaller brains compared to anthropoids.

learning_objectives: Define scarcity and explain its economic impact
Define opportunity cost
Define productive resources
Explain why trade and markets exist
Distinguish between macroeconomics and microeconomics

knowledge_units: Scarcity means that there are never enough resources to satisfy all human wants.
Economics is the study of the trade-offs and choices that we make, given the fact of scarcity.
Opportunity cost is what we give up when we choose one thing over another.
Scarcity is the condition where limited resources cannot meet all human needs and desires.
Economics is the study of how humans make choices under conditions of scarcity.
Scarcity forces individuals and societies to make trade-offs when allocating resources.
Opportunity cost is what must be given up to obtain something that is desired.
Every choice has an opportunity cost because resources are limited.
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative.
Recognizing opportunity cost can alter individual decisions, such as choosing between buying lunch or bringing it from home.
Societal decisions, like implementing universal health care, involve opportunity costs such as reduced spending on other public goods.
Government policies, such as enhanced airline security, have opportunity costs that include both monetary expenditures and the value of time spent by travelers.
Scarcity forces individuals to make choices that involve opportunity costs.
The division of labor divides the production of a good or service into separate tasks performed by different workers.
Specialization allows workers to focus on tasks where they have an advantage, increasing productivity.
Specialization enables workers to learn their tasks more quickly and produce higher quality output.
Economies of scale mean that as production increases, the average cost per unit decreases.
The division and specialization of labor helps society produce and consume more, countering scarcity.
Specialization requires trade, as workers use income from their specialized work to purchase other goods and services.
Markets allow individuals to specialize in a set of skills and trade for the goods and services they need.
Microeconomics focuses on the actions of individual agents within the economy, like households, workers, and businesses.
Macroeconomics looks at the economy as a whole, focusing on broad issues such as growth, unemployment, inflation, and trade balance.
Microeconomics and macroeconomics are complementary perspectives on the overall subject of the economy.
Microeconomic decisions of individual businesses are influenced by the health of the macroeconomy.
The performance of the macroeconomy ultimately depends on the microeconomic decisions made by individual households and businesses.
Macroeconomic policy pursues goals like growth in the standard of living, low unemployment, and low inflation.
Monetary policy involves policies that affect bank lending, interest rates, and financial capital markets, conducted by a nation's central bank.
Fiscal policy involves government spending and taxes, determined by a nation's legislative body.

learning_objectives: Define an economic model
Explain why economic models are useful to economists
Identify common economic models

knowledge_units: Economists use economic models as their principal tool to analyze choices about scarce resources.
Economic models help analyze choices at both the individual level and the broader societal level.
Economists use models as the primary tool for explaining or making predictions about economic issues and problems.
Economic models can be represented using words or using mathematics.
Math is one tool for working with economic models, but it is not the only or always the best tool.
Economic models offer a way to get a complete view of an economic situation and understand how economic factors fit together.
The circular flow diagram is a basic economic model showing households and firms interacting in goods-and-services and labor markets.
Economists use theories and models to gain insights about economic issues and problems.
An economic model is a simplified version of reality that allows us to observe, understand, and make predictions about economic behavior.
The purpose of an economic model is to pare down a complex, real-world situation to its essentials.
A good economic model is simple enough to be understood while complex enough to capture key information.

learning_objectives: Use an equation to understand the impact of changing economic variables
Explain how functions describe cause and effect

knowledge_units: Economists use mathematics as a tool for manipulating and exploring economic models.
Many economic models use mathematics to explain cause and effect relationships.
Developing mathematical proficiency in economics is primarily a matter of practice and training the brain, not innate talent.
A function describes a relationship involving one or more variables.
In economics, functions frequently describe cause and effect, where the left-hand side variable is the effect and the right-hand side variables are the causes.
Functions are useful for making predictions about outcomes based on explanatory variables.
Economic models express relationships using economic variables, often with simplifying assumptions.

The order of operations requires simplifying parentheses and brackets first, then exponents, followed by multiplication and division from left to right, and finally addition and subtraction from left to right.
A variable is a quantity that can assume a range of values, represented by a letter or symbol in an equation.
Economic models use variables to represent cause and effect, allowing consideration of different options and outcomes.
To solve an equation for a variable, isolate the variable by performing the same operation on both sides of the equation.

learning_objectives: Explain how a graph shows the relationship between two variables
Differentiate between a positive relationship and a negative relationship
Interpret economic information on a graph

knowledge_units: A graph is a visual representation of numerical information that condenses detailed data to reveal patterns.
Economists use graphs as compact presentations of data and as models to visually represent relationships between variables.
Graphs can be used to answer economic questions by illustrating how changes in one variable affect another.
The x-axis is the horizontal line on a graph and the y-axis is the vertical line.
An intercept is where a line crosses the x-axis or y-axis.
Slope is the change in the vertical axis divided by the change in the horizontal axis, often called 'rise over run'.
In the equation for a line y = b + mx, the b term is the y-intercept, which is where the line crosses the y-axis when x = 0.
The m term in the equation for a line is the slope, indicating how much y changes for each unit increase in x.
Specifying a y-intercept and a slope identifies a specific straight line on a graph.
A positive slope indicates that two variables are positively related, meaning as one increases, the other also increases.
A negative slope indicates that two variables are negatively related, meaning as one increases, the other decreases.
A slope of zero indicates a constant relationship between two variables, resulting in a flat line on a graph.
The slope of a line measures the relationship between two variables and is calculated as the rise over run between two points.
A steeper slope indicates a larger absolute value of the slope, while a flatter slope indicates a smaller absolute value.
A change in the intercept of a line causes a parallel shift of the line on a graph.
Line graphs show the relationship between two variables, with one variable on the horizontal axis and the other on the vertical axis.
A time-series graph is a line graph where the horizontal axis represents time and the vertical axis shows how another variable changes over time.
Pie graphs are used to show how an overall total is divided into parts, with each slice representing a share or percentage of the whole.
Bar graphs use the height of bars to compare quantities across different categories.
Line graphs are effective for illustrating relationships between two variables that are both changing, such as in time-series data.
Pie graphs are often better than line graphs at showing how an overall group is divided into subgroups.
Bar graphs are especially useful for comparing quantities across multiple categories and can show breakdowns within groups.

chapter_title: Choice in a World of Scarcity

learning_objectives: Explain how budget constraints impact choices
Calculate the opportunity cost of an action

knowledge_units: Budget constraints limit the choices available to individuals and firms.
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that must be given up when making a choice.
A budget constraint refers to all possible combinations of goods that someone can afford given prices and income.
The slope of a budget constraint is determined by the relative prices of the goods.
Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that must be given up when making a choice.
Sunk costs are costs incurred in the past that cannot be recovered and should not affect current decisions.
Choices involve trade-offs because resources like money and time are limited.
Points inside a budget constraint represent affordable combinations where not all income is spent.
Points outside a budget constraint are not affordable because they exceed available income.
A budget constraint equation shows that total spending on goods equals the available budget.
The budget constraint equation is Budget = P1 × Q1 + P2 × Q2 + ... + Pn × Qn.
Solving the budget constraint equation for one quantity shows how much of that good can be purchased given the quantity of another good.
The slope of a budget constraint line is negative, indicating an inverse relationship between the quantities of two goods.
The slope of a budget constraint line represents the opportunity cost of the good on the horizontal axis.
A change in the price of one good alters the slope of the budget constraint, changing the opportunity cost.

learning_objectives: Explain the production possibilities frontier
Identify the impact of society’s production choices

knowledge_units: The production possibilities frontier illustrates society's trade-offs by showing possible combinations of two products or services that can be produced with available resources.
A society's production choices involve trade-offs, where producing more of one good or service requires producing less of another.
The production possibilities curve represents points where a society maximizes resource use by choosing combinations of goods or devoting all resources to a single good.
The production possibilities frontier (PPF) is an economic model showing possible combinations of two products or services a society could produce with its limited resources.
The PPF illustrates trade-offs between producing different goods, such as health care and education.
Moving along the PPF to produce more of one good requires giving up some of the other good, representing opportunity cost.
The slope of the PPF represents the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of a good.
Society can produce any combination on or inside the PPF but cannot produce outside it due to resource constraints.
The PPF is curved outward due to the law of diminishing returns.
The law of diminishing returns states that as additional resources are devoted to a purpose, the marginal benefit from those increments declines.
When few resources are allocated to a good, adding more yields large gains; when many resources are already allocated, adding more yields smaller gains.
Productive efficiency means that given available inputs and technology, it is impossible to produce more of one good without decreasing the quantity of another good produced.
All choices along the production possibilities frontier display productive efficiency.
Any choice inside the production possibilities frontier is productively inefficient because it is possible to produce more of one good, the other good, or some combination of both goods.
Allocative efficiency means that the particular mix of goods a society produces represents the combination that society most desires.
Only one of the productively efficient choices will be the allocative efficient choice for society as a whole.
A society must choose between trade-offs in the present because improvements in productive efficiency take time and economic growth happens gradually.
When a country can produce a good at a lower opportunity cost than another country, it has a comparative advantage in that good.
Countries tend to have different opportunity costs of producing a specific good due to differences in climate, geography, technology, or skills.
The slope of a country's production possibilities frontier gives the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of a good in terms of the other good.

learning_objectives: Define rationality in an economic context
Provide examples of rational decision-making

knowledge_units: Economists assume that human decision-making is predictable and rational.
Rational decision-making involves trying to avoid costs and maximize benefits to oneself.
In economics, rationality refers to the assumption that people make choices by balancing costs against benefits to maximize their personal utility.
Economists assume that individuals make decisions in their own self-interest, choosing options that provide the greatest personal benefit.
The assumption of rational self-interest is a simplification used to model human decision-making and does not imply that people are purely selfish or greedy.
In economic rationality, consumers aim to maximize benefit and minimize cost.
A consumer's rational decision involves comparing the benefits of a purchase against its costs.
As the cost of a product increases, it becomes less likely that a consumer will find the benefits outweigh the costs.
Businesses are assumed to act rationally by seeking to maximize profits.
Economists predict business behavior based on the assumption that decisions are made to maximize profit.

learning_objectives: Define marginal cost
Define marginal benefit

knowledge_units: Marginal analysis describes how people make choices by comparing the benefits and costs of doing a bit more or a bit less of an activity.
The term 'marginal' means 'additional' or 'extra' in economics.
Marginal decision-making involves considering a little more or a little less than what we already have.
Marginal analysis compares the costs and benefits of a little more or a little less.
Marginal cost is the change in total cost from one option to another.
From a consumer's point of view, marginal cost is the additional cost of one more item purchased.
From a business's point of view, marginal cost is the additional cost of one more item produced.
Marginal benefit is the difference in what you receive from a different choice.
From a consumer's point of view, marginal benefit is the additional satisfaction of one more item purchased.
From a business's point of view, marginal benefit is the additional revenues received from selling one more item.
An economically rational decision is one in which the marginal benefits of a choice are greater than the marginal costs of the choice.

learning_objectives: Define positive and normative statements
Provide examples of positive and normative statements

knowledge_units: Positive statements describe 'what is possible' and are based on facts or cause-and-effect relationships.
Normative statements describe 'what should be chosen' and are based on value judgments or opinions.
Positive statements describe the world as it is and can be tested or verified.
Normative statements describe how the world should be and involve value judgments that cannot be proven true or false.
Disagreements over positive statements can be resolved through investigation, while disagreements over normative statements often persist due to differing values.

learning_objectives: Define and give an example of a market economy
Define and give an example of a planned economy
Define and give an example of a command economy
Define and explain the characteristics of a competitive market

knowledge_units: The answer to questions about organizing and coordinating an economy depends on the kind of economic system a society uses.
What you’ll learn to do: describe and differentiate between major economic systems Think about what a complex system a modern economy is.
A market economy is an economic system where decision-making occurs through markets based on private enterprise and supply and demand.
In a market economy, businesses supply goods and services based on consumer demand, and income is based on the value society places on a person's output.
A competitive market is one with many buyers and sellers, preventing any single entity from controlling the market price.
A free market is one where the government does not intervene in economic decisions.
A command economy is an economic system where the government owns resources and businesses and makes decisions about production, prices, and wages.
In a command economy, economic effort is devoted to goals set by a ruler or ruling class, as seen in historical examples like ancient Egypt.
The primary distinction between free and command economies is the degree of government control over production and pricing.
Most real-world economies are mixed, combining elements of both market and command systems.
Economic systems determine what to produce, how to produce it, and who gets the produced goods and services.

learning_objectives: Explain the law of demand
Explain a demand curve
Create a demand curve using a data set
Describe the differences between changes in demand and changes in the quantity demanded
Explain the impact of factors that change demand

knowledge_units: The law of demand states that, all else equal, a decrease in price leads to an increase in the quantity demanded, and an increase in price leads to a decrease in the quantity demanded.
The law of demand is a fundamental concept essential for understanding economics.
Demand refers to the amount of a good or service consumers are willing and able to purchase at each price.
The law of demand states that, all else equal, a rise in price decreases the quantity demanded and a fall in price increases the quantity demanded.
A demand schedule is a table showing the quantity demanded at each price.
A demand curve slopes downward from left to right, illustrating the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded.
Demand refers to the entire relationship between price and quantity demanded, while quantity demanded refers to a specific point on the demand curve.
Demand is defined as the amount of a product a consumer is willing and able to purchase at each price.
Factors other than price that affect demand include tastes and preferences, income, prices of related goods, and population size or composition.
The ceteris paribus assumption holds all other factors constant when analyzing the relationship between price and quantity demanded.
A demand curve shows the relationship between price and quantity demanded when all other factors are held constant.
An increase in income typically shifts the demand curve for a normal good to the right, indicating increased demand at every price.
A decrease in income shifts the demand curve for a normal good to the left, indicating decreased demand at every price.
A normal good is a product whose demand rises when income rises and falls when income falls.
An inferior good is a product whose demand falls when income rises and rises when income falls.
Changes in tastes or preferences shift the demand curve for a good.
Changes in the composition or size of the population shift the demand curve for related goods.
A substitute is a good that can be used in place of another; a lower price for a substitute decreases demand for the other product.
Complements are goods often used together; a higher price for one complement decreases demand for the other.
Expectations about future prices or other factors can shift the current demand curve.
A shift in demand occurs when a change in an economic factor other than the current price causes a different quantity to be demanded at every price.
Six factors can shift demand curves, and these shifts represent either an increase or a decrease in demand.
A change in the price of a good or service itself does not shift the demand curve but causes a movement along it, leading to a change in the quantity demanded.

learning_objectives: Explain the law of supply
Explain a supply curve
Create a supply curve using a data set
Describe the differences between changes in supply and changes in quantity supplied
Explain the impact of factors that change supply

knowledge_units: Supply refers to the producer side of economics, focusing on factors that impact the amount of goods supplied in a market.
The law of supply describes the relationship between the price of a good and the quantity supplied.
Understanding supply is essential as a counterpart to demand in economics.
Supply refers to the amount of a good or service a producer is willing to supply at each price.
The law of supply states that a higher price leads to a higher quantity supplied and a lower price leads to a lower quantity supplied, assuming all other variables are held equal.
Supply refers to the entire relationship between price and quantity supplied, which can be represented by a supply curve or schedule.
Quantity supplied refers to a specific point on the supply curve, representing a particular quantity at a given price.
A supply schedule is a table showing the quantity supplied at a range of different prices.
A supply curve is a graphic illustration of the relationship between price and quantity supplied.
Supply curves typically slope upward from left to right, illustrating the law of supply.
A shift in supply means a change in the quantity supplied at every price, caused by factors other than the good's own price.
When costs of production fall, a firm will tend to supply a larger quantity at any given price, shifting the supply curve to the right.
When costs of production rise, a firm will tend to supply a smaller quantity at any given price, shifting the supply curve to the left.
Changes in the prices of inputs, such as materials or labor, affect the cost of production and thus shift the supply curve.
Changes in natural conditions, like weather, can affect the cost of production for agricultural products and shift supply.
A technological improvement that reduces production costs will shift the supply curve to the right.
Government taxes and regulations increase production costs and shift the supply curve to the left.
A government subsidy reduces production costs and shifts the supply curve to the right.
Changes in input costs, natural disasters, new technologies, and government decisions affect production costs and thus supply.
A change in the price of the product itself causes a change in quantity supplied, not a shift in the supply curve.
Supply is an umbrella concept covering all factors that affect supply, represented by shifts in the supply curve.

learning_objectives: Define and explain equilibrium price and quantity
Create a graph that illustrates equilibrium price and quantity
Define and explain surpluses and shortages
Create a graph that illustrates surpluses and shortages
Describe how disequilibrium can create surpluses and shortages; explain how markets eliminate them

knowledge_units: Market equilibrium occurs where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.
A surplus exists when the quantity supplied exceeds the quantity demanded at a given price.
A shortage exists when the quantity demanded exceeds the quantity supplied at a given price.
Disequilibrium, such as a price above or below equilibrium, creates surpluses or shortages that are eliminated by market forces.
The law of demand states that as price decreases, consumers demand a higher quantity.
The law of supply states that as price decreases, producers supply a lower quantity.
Equilibrium occurs where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, determining the market price and quantity.
A surplus, or excess supply, occurs when the quantity supplied is greater than the quantity demanded at a given price.
A surplus puts downward pressure on price, which increases quantity demanded and decreases quantity supplied until equilibrium is restored.
A shortage, or excess demand, occurs when the quantity demanded is greater than the quantity supplied at a given price.
A shortage puts upward pressure on price, which decreases quantity demanded and increases quantity supplied until equilibrium is restored.
The equilibrium price is the only price where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied.
The equilibrium quantity is the amount bought and sold at the equilibrium price.
A market not at equilibrium experiences economic pressures that naturally move it toward equilibrium.
The four-step process is used to analyze how an economic event affects equilibrium price and quantity.
Step 1 involves drawing a demand and supply model to find the initial equilibrium values.
Step 2 requires deciding whether the economic event affects demand or supply.
Step 3 involves determining the direction of the curve shift and sketching the new curve.
Step 4 identifies the new equilibrium and compares it to the original equilibrium price and quantity.
A change in tastes, such as a shift to digital news sources, causes a change in demand for traditional news sources.
A leftward shift in the demand curve for traditional news sources leads to a lower equilibrium price and quantity.

learning_objectives: Define elasticity
Provide a practical example of elasticity
Explain common factors that affect elasticity

knowledge_units: Elasticity is an economics concept that measures the responsiveness of one variable to changes in another variable.
Elasticity explains how much one variable, such as sales numbers, will change in response to another variable, like the price of the product.
Elasticity measures how much one variable changes in response to another variable.
Elastic demand occurs when a small change in price leads to a large change in quantity demanded.
Inelastic demand occurs when a large change in price leads to a small change in quantity demanded.
Cigarette demand among certain groups is relatively inelastic because price increases reduce smoking rates less than proportionally.
Understanding elasticity is important for governments to predict the impact of taxes on both revenue and public health.
Firms use elasticity to assess how price changes will affect the quantity demanded of their products.
Price elasticity of demand is fundamentally about the availability of substitutes.
Demand for a product is more elastic if it is easy to find a substitute when its price increases.
Demand for a product is less elastic if there are few or no alternatives.
The greater the necessity of a product, the less elastic its demand tends to be.
The more luxurious a product is, the more elastic its demand tends to be.
The larger the share of an item in a consumer's budget, the more price elastic its demand is likely to be.
The smaller the share of an item in a consumer's budget, the more price inelastic its demand is likely to be.
Demand is usually more price elastic in the long run than in the short run.
Goods produced by a single supplier generally have inelastic demand.
Products in a competitive marketplace generally have elastic demand.

learning_objectives: Define price elasticity of demand
Define price elasticity of supply
Calculate price elasticity using the midpoint method
Mathematically differentiate between elastic, inelastic, and unitary elasticities of demand and supply
Explain perfect elasticity and perfect inelasticity

knowledge_units: Elasticity measures the responsiveness of one variable to changes in another variable.
Price elasticity of demand measures how much a change in price affects the quantity demanded.
Price elasticity of supply measures how much a change in price affects the quantity supplied.
Percentage change, also known as growth rate, is calculated as the change in quantity divided by the original quantity.
Price elasticity of demand is defined as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.
The point elasticity approach uses the initial values to calculate percentage change, while the midpoint approach uses the average of the initial and final values for greater accuracy.
The midpoint method calculates percentage change as the change in quantity divided by the average of the initial and final quantities.
Price elasticity of demand is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price.
Price elasticity of supply is the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price.
The midpoint method calculates percentage changes using the average of the initial and final values as the base.
The midpoint method yields the same elasticity value regardless of the direction of price change.
Price elasticities of demand are negative due to the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded, but are conventionally reported as absolute values.
An elasticity value less than 1 indicates inelastic demand, meaning quantity demanded is relatively unresponsive to price changes.
An elasticity value greater than 1 indicates elastic demand, meaning quantity demanded is relatively responsive to price changes.
Price elasticity of demand varies along a straight-line demand curve, being more elastic at higher prices and lower quantities.
Elasticity is a ratio of percentage changes and is distinct from the slope of a demand or supply curve.
Elastic demand or supply occurs when the elasticity is greater than 1, indicating high responsiveness to price changes.
Inelastic demand or supply occurs when the elasticity is less than 1, indicating low responsiveness to price changes.
Unitary elasticity occurs when the elasticity equals 1, meaning the percentage change in quantity equals the percentage change in price.
Infinite elasticity or perfect elasticity refers to the extreme case where quantity demanded or supplied changes by an infinite amount in response to any price change.
Perfectly elastic supply means any change in price results in an infinite change in quantity supplied, with the supply curve being horizontal.
Perfectly elastic demand means quantity demanded increases to infinity when price decreases and decreases to zero when price increases, with the demand curve being horizontal.
Zero elasticity or perfect inelasticity refers to the extreme case where a percentage change in price results in zero change in quantity supplied or demanded.
Perfectly inelastic supply means quantity supplied remains the same regardless of price changes, as seen with goods having limited inputs like beachfront property.
Perfectly inelastic demand means quantity demanded remains the same regardless of price changes, as seen with necessities like life-saving prescription drugs.

learning_objectives: Explain and calculate elasticity of income and labor
Explain and calculate cross-price elasticity of demand

knowledge_units: Income elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of demand to changes in consumer income.
Cross-price elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of demand for one good to changes in the price of another good, which can be complements or substitutes.
Income elasticity of demand is the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in income.
A positive income elasticity of demand indicates a normal good, while a negative income elasticity indicates an inferior good.
Cross-price elasticity of demand is the percentage change in the quantity demanded of one good divided by the percentage change in the price of another good.
Substitute goods have a positive cross-price elasticity of demand, while complement goods have a negative cross-price elasticity.
Wage elasticity of labor supply is the percentage change in the quantity of labor supplied divided by the percentage change in wages.
Elasticity of savings is the percentage change in the quantity of savings divided by the percentage change in interest rates.
The elasticity concept applies to any market, including labor and financial capital markets, not just goods and services.

learning_objectives: Explain the interaction between price and revenue, given elastic demand
Explain the interaction between price and revenue, given inelastic demand
Explain how changes in production costs affect price

knowledge_units: Price elasticity of demand describes how changes in price relate to changes in demand for goods.
Revenue is calculated as the total number of units sold multiplied by the price per unit.
Changes in price or quantity sold directly impact a firm's total revenue.
Businesses use price as a tool to influence demand and sales to maximize profits.
Total revenue is calculated as price multiplied by quantity sold (TR = P x Qd).
If demand is elastic, a price increase leads to a decrease in total revenue because the percentage drop in quantity sold is greater than the percentage increase in price.
If demand is inelastic, a price increase leads to an increase in total revenue because the percentage drop in quantity sold is smaller than the percentage increase in price.
If demand is unitary elastic, a price change leaves total revenue unchanged because the percentage change in quantity sold equals the percentage change in price.
When demand is elastic, a firm should lower its price to increase total revenue.
When demand is inelastic, a firm should raise its price to increase total revenue.
The price elasticity of demand determines how a price change affects total revenue.
Understanding elasticity helps a firm set a price that maximizes total revenue.
If the cost of a key input rises, a firm may pass along those higher costs to consumers in the form of higher prices.
The price elasticity of demand determines whether a firm can pass along higher costs to consumers or keep benefits from lower costs as higher profits.
With highly inelastic demand, a technological breakthrough that increases supply leads to a substantially lower price with relatively little impact on quantity sold.
With highly elastic demand, a technological breakthrough leads to a much greater quantity sold at very close to the original price.
When demand is inelastic, a surge in production can lead to a severe drop in price that decreases total revenue.
When demand is inelastic, poor conditions that reduce production can sharply raise prices and increase total revenue.
For addictive substances like cigarettes with relatively inelastic demand, increased taxes are mainly passed along to consumers as higher prices.
If demand for cigarettes were more elastic, an increase in taxes would reduce the quantity smoked substantially.
Elasticities are often lower in the short run than in the long run, meaning prices adjust more in the short run while quantities adjust more in the long run.

learning_objectives: Identify the market’s equilibrium price and quantity under a price ceiling
Compute and graph the market shortage resulting from a price ceiling

knowledge_units: A binding price ceiling is a government-imposed maximum price set below the equilibrium price.
A binding price ceiling creates a market shortage because quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied at the ceiling price.
A price ceiling is a legal maximum price set by the government to prevent a price from rising above a certain level.
When a price ceiling is set below the market equilibrium price, it creates a shortage because quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied.
A price ceiling can lead to a reduction in the quantity of goods or services actually exchanged in the market compared to the equilibrium quantity.
Price ceilings can result in non-price rationing mechanisms and a decline in product quality as sellers reduce maintenance or other costs.
Rent control is an example of a price ceiling that limits the amount by which rents can be increased.

learning_objectives: Identify the market’s equilibrium price and quantity for a price floor
Compute and graph the market surplus resulting from a price floor
Explain the outcome of a binding price ceiling or price floor on the price and quantity of a product sold

knowledge_units: A binding price floor forces prices above the equilibrium level.
A price floor can have both positive and negative effects on workers.
A price floor is the lowest legal price that can be paid in markets for goods and services, labor, or financial capital.
Price floors are sometimes called 'price supports' because they support a price by preventing it from falling below a certain level.
The most common way price supports work is that the government enters the market and buys up the product, adding to demand to keep prices higher than they otherwise would be.
When a price floor is set above the equilibrium price, the quantity supplied exceeds the quantity demanded, creating a surplus.
Neither price ceilings nor price floors cause demand or supply to change; they simply set a price that limits what can be legally charged in the market.
Price controls can cause a different choice of quantity demanded along a demand curve, but they do not move the demand curve.
Price controls can cause a different choice of quantity supplied along a supply curve, but they do not shift the supply curve.

learning_objectives: explain how the price elasticities of demand and supply affect the incidence of a sales tax.

knowledge_units: When demand is price inelastic, consumers bear the entire burden of a sales tax because producers can pass the full tax amount to them through higher prices.
When demand is price elastic, producers bear the tax burden because they cannot raise prices without losing significant revenue.
If producers can raise prices by less than the full tax amount, the tax burden is shared between consumers and producers.
Tax incidence is the effect of a tax on producers and consumers, determined by the price elasticities of supply and demand rather than who legally pays the tax.
When supply is inelastic and demand is elastic, the tax burden falls primarily on producers because they cannot pass the tax onto consumers without a large drop in quantity demanded.
When demand is inelastic and supply is elastic, the tax burden falls primarily on consumers because they will continue to buy nearly the same quantity despite a price increase.
When supply and demand have similar elasticities, the tax burden is shared between producers and consumers.
An increase in a tax shifts the supply curve leftward, raising the consumer price and lowering the sellers' price, without changing the elasticities of supply or demand.

learning_objectives: define progressive, proportional, and regressive taxes.

knowledge_units: Describe the incomes earned by the factors of production (land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship), i.e. wages, interest, rents, and profit.
Analyze how perfect/imperfect competition between buyers and sellers of factors can impact wages, interest, and rents.
A regressive tax takes a higher percentage of income as income falls.
A proportional tax takes a fixed percentage of income regardless of the income level.
A progressive tax takes a higher percentage of income as income rises.
The ability-to-pay principle holds that people with more income should pay more taxes.
The benefits-received principle holds that a tax should be based on the benefits received from government services.
User fees apply the benefits-received principle directly by charging for specific government services.
A flat tax with an income exemption can be progressive because lower-income households pay a lower effective tax rate.
Income taxes can be structured to be regressive, proportional, or progressive.
A regressive tax takes a higher percentage of income as income falls.
A proportional tax takes a constant percentage of income regardless of income level.
A progressive tax takes a higher percentage of income as income rises.
The marginal tax rate is the tax rate that applies to an additional dollar of income earned.
Tax incidence analysis seeks to determine who ultimately bears the burden of a tax.
The major types of taxes are income taxes, sales taxes, property taxes, and excise taxes.
Buyers bear most of the burden of an excise tax when supply is relatively elastic and demand is relatively inelastic.
Sellers bear most of the burden of an excise tax when supply is relatively inelastic and demand is relatively elastic.
The federal tax system in the United States is progressive.
A progressive tax is one where tax rates increase as a household's income increases.
A proportional tax is a flat percentage tax applied to all wages earned.
A regressive tax is one where people with higher incomes pay a smaller share of their income in tax.

learning_objectives: define and calculate consumer, producer, and total surplus; graphically illustrate consumer, producer, and total surplus.

knowledge_units: Consumer surplus is the difference between what consumers are willing to pay and the market price they actually pay.
Producer surplus is the difference between the market price and the minimum price producers are willing to accept.
Total surplus is the sum of consumer surplus and producer surplus in a market.
Consumer surplus is the area above the market price and below the demand curve, representing the difference between what consumers are willing to pay and what they actually pay.
Producer surplus is the area between the market price and the supply curve, representing the difference between the price sellers receive and their minimum acceptable price.
Total surplus is the sum of consumer surplus and producer surplus, representing the total gains from trade in a market.
Economic efficiency occurs when it is impossible to improve one party's situation without imposing a cost on another, meaning all possible gains from trade are achieved.
In a demand and supply model, efficiency means the economy is getting as much benefit as possible from scarce resources with the optimal quantity produced and consumed.

learning_objectives: use the concepts of consumer, producer, and total surplus to explain why markets typically lead to efficient outcomes.

knowledge_units: What you’ll learn to do: use the concepts of consumer, producer, and total surplus to explain why markets typically lead to efficient outcomes.
use the concepts of consumer, producer, and total surplus to explain why markets typically lead to efficient outcomes.
Price floors and price ceilings prevent markets from adjusting to equilibrium, creating inefficient outcomes.
Price controls can transfer surplus from producers to consumers or vice versa.
Deadweight loss is the reduction in total surplus that occurs when an economy produces at an inefficient quantity.
When deadweight loss exists, it is possible for both consumer and producer surplus to be higher if the price control is removed.

learning_objectives: Differentiate between marginal utility and total utility

knowledge_units: What you’ll learn to do: define the concept of utility and satisfaction.
In this outcome, you will learn to gauge the measure of satisfaction that a person gets from buying a product.
Consumers aim to maximize total utility, or satisfaction, given their budget constraints.
Consumer choices are influenced by personal tastes, incomes, prices of goods and services, and factors like location.
Economic theory provides a tool to analyze how consumption responds to changes in prices or incomes.
Total utility is the total amount of satisfaction a consumer derives from consuming a certain quantity of a good.
Marginal utility is the additional utility provided by one additional unit of consumption.
Marginal utility is calculated as the change in total utility divided by the change in quantity consumed.
The law of diminishing marginal utility states that the additional utility from each subsequent unit of a good consumed decreases.
Total utility increases as more units of a good are consumed, but at a decreasing rate due to diminishing marginal utility.
A consumer's choice aims to maximize total utility given their budget constraint.
Consumers maximize utility by comparing the marginal utility per dollar spent on different goods.
The step-by-step approach to utility maximization involves reallocating spending from goods with lower marginal utility per dollar to those with higher marginal utility per dollar.
Marginal utility per dollar is calculated as the marginal utility of a good divided by its price.
A consumer will purchase the good with the highest marginal utility per dollar until the marginal utility per dollar is equalized across goods or the budget is exhausted.
The utility-maximizing combination occurs where the marginal utility per dollar is equal for all goods purchased, given the consumer's budget constraint.

learning_objectives: describe and calculate the concept of marginal utility.

knowledge_units: Marginal utility involves examining choices made at the margin, which are decisions to do a little more or a little less of something.
In this section, you will examine choices made at the margin, or the decisions you make to do a little more or a little less of something.
Marginal utility is the change in satisfaction from consuming one additional unit of a good or service.
Choices are made at the margin, meaning decisions involve a little more or a little less of an activity.
The general formula for a marginal concept is the change in outcome divided by the change in inputs.
The law of diminishing marginal utility states that the marginal benefit of a good decreases with each additional unit consumed.

learning_objectives: explain how consumers maximize total utility within a given income using the Utility Maximizing Rule.

knowledge_units: What you’ll learn to do: explain how consumers maximize total utility within a given income using the Utility Maximizing Rule.
explain how consumers maximize total utility within a given income using the Utility Maximizing Rule.
The utility-maximizing choice between consumption goods occurs where the marginal utility per dollar is the same for both goods.
The utility-maximizing condition can be expressed as MU1/P1 = MU2/P2.
If the marginal utility per dollar for one good is higher than for another, a consumer can increase total utility by reallocating spending toward that good.
At the utility-maximizing point, the ratio of the prices of two goods equals the ratio of their marginal utilities.
The step-by-step process of comparing marginal utility gains and losses from different choices along the budget constraint identifies the combination with the highest total utility.
The key assumption for utility maximization is that individuals can identify which of two alternatives they prefer, not that utility can be measured with specific numbers.

learning_objectives: explain how consumer's utility changes when income or prices change.

knowledge_units: Consumer utility is analyzed in relation to a person's budget constraints.
Economists interpret consumer choices to understand how utility changes with income or prices.
A demand curve shows the relationship between price and quantity demanded and typically slopes downward from left to right.
Changes in the price of a good cause the budget constraint to shift, altering the utility-maximizing quantity demanded.
The demand curve is derived from individuals seeking to maximize utility subject to a budget constraint.
When the price of a good rises, the budget constraint shifts left, and the quantity demanded of that good decreases, ceteris paribus.
People react to price or income changes by adjusting consumption across multiple goods, not just the good whose price changed.
A tax on a product increases its price, causing the budget constraint to pivot left and reducing consumption of that product.
The distribution of income within a household can affect consumption patterns, as different members may have different preferences.
A rise in income typically leads to increased consumption of both goods, which is the case for normal goods.
A fall in income typically leads to decreased consumption of both goods for normal goods.
A good is classified as a normal good when a rise in income leads to a rise in the quantity consumed of that good.
A good is classified as an inferior good when a rise in income leads to a fall in the quantity consumed of that good.
A price increase for one good causes the budget constraint to rotate inward, reducing the quantity of that good that can be purchased.
The substitution effect occurs when a price change gives consumers an incentive to consume less of the good with a relatively higher price and more of the good with a relatively lower price.
The income effect occurs when a higher price reduces the buying power of income, leading to buying less of the good if it is a normal good.
A change in the price of one good can affect the quantity consumed of other goods, not just the good whose price changed.

learning_objectives: describe the behavioral economics approach to understanding decision making.

knowledge_units: Behavioral economics studies how psychological factors influence economic decisions, which are not always logical.
People tend to feel the pain of a loss more strongly than the pleasure of an equivalent gain, a concept known as loss aversion.
Behavioral economics integrates psychological insights into economics to better understand decision-making.
Traditional economic models assume people make rational, consistent decisions based on all available information.
Behavioral economists argue that people's state of mind, including emotions, affects their decisions in predictable ways.
Loss aversion describes the tendency for people to feel the pain of a loss more strongly than the pleasure of an equivalent gain.
Mental accounting is the practice of treating money differently based on its source or intended use, which can lead to seemingly irrational decisions.
Nudges are subtle policy shifts that encourage better decisions without restricting freedom of choice.
Behavioral economics helps explain behaviors that traditional economists might dismiss as irrational.

learning_objectives: Define the term “production inputs,” and differentiate between labor, land, capital, entrepreneurship, technology

knowledge_units: Production inputs are the resources used in the production of goods and services.
The main categories of production inputs are labor, land, capital, entrepreneurship, and technology.
A firm combines inputs of labor, capital, land, and raw or finished component materials to produce outputs.
Production includes any process or service that creates value, not just manufacturing.
Firm decisions about what, how, and how much to produce depend on production and cost conditions.
Market structure is defined by factors such as market power, product similarity, barriers to entry, and basis of competition.
Factors of production are the resources available to an economy for the production of goods and services.
Labor is the human effort that can be applied to the production of goods and services.
Human capital refers to the skills a worker has from education, training, or experience that can be used in production.
Capital is a factor of production that has been produced for use in the production of other goods and services.
Natural resources are resources found in nature that can be used for the production of goods and services.
Technology is the knowledge that can be applied to the production of goods and services.
An entrepreneur is a person who seeks to earn profits by finding new ways to organize factors of production in a market economy.

learning_objectives: Explain diminishing marginal product and diminishing marginal returns

knowledge_units: Marginal product is the additional output produced by one more unit of a variable input.
Diminishing marginal returns occur when adding more of a variable input to a fixed input leads to a smaller increase in output.
Total product is the total quantity of output produced by a firm.
Average product is the total product divided by the quantity of the variable input.
The short run is a planning period over which at least one factor of production is fixed in quantity.
A fixed factor of production is a factor whose quantity cannot be changed during a particular period.
A variable factor of production is a factor whose quantity can be changed during a particular period.
The long run is a planning period over which all factors of production are variable.
A production function describes the relationship between factors of production and the output of a firm.
Marginal product is the change in output resulting from a one-unit change in a variable factor, holding other factors constant.
Average product is the output per unit of a variable factor.
When marginal product is above average product, average product is rising.
When marginal product is below average product, average product is falling.
Increasing marginal returns occur when each additional unit of a variable factor adds more to total output than the previous unit.
Diminishing marginal returns occur when each additional unit of a variable factor adds less to total output than the previous unit, but total output is still increasing.
Negative marginal returns occur when an additional unit of a variable factor reduces total output.
The law of diminishing marginal returns states that the marginal product of any variable factor will eventually decline, assuming the quantities of other factors are unchanged.

learning_objectives: differentiate between explicit and implicit costs, accounting, and economic profit.

knowledge_units: Explicit costs are the obvious, out-of-pocket expenses of running a business, such as rent, ingredients, and staff wages.
Implicit costs are the non-monetary opportunity costs, such as the owner's time and forgone salary, that are harder to measure.
Explicit costs are out-of-pocket payments actually made by a firm, such as wages or rent.
Implicit costs represent the opportunity cost of using resources already owned by the firm, such as an owner's forgone salary.
Accounting profit is total revenue minus explicit costs.
Economic profit is total revenue minus both explicit and implicit costs.
Economic profit determines a firm's true economic success, while accounting profit is used for tax purposes.
An example of an implicit cost is the salary an entrepreneur forgoes by leaving a job to start a business.

learning_objectives: Differentiate between variable and fixed costs

knowledge_units: What you’ll learn to do: define and differentiate between marginal, average, and total cost; compute and graph marginal, average, and total cost.
Differentiate between variable and fixed costs.
Fixed costs are expenditures that do not change with the level of production in the short term.
Variable costs are incurred in the act of producing and increase with the level of production.
Labor is treated as a variable cost because producing more typically requires more workers or work hours.
Diminishing marginal returns occur when each additional unit of a variable input contributes less to overall production, given a fixed factor.
Total costs in the short run can be divided into fixed costs that cannot be changed and variable costs that can be changed.
Average total cost is total cost divided by the quantity of output.
Average total cost curves are typically U-shaped because fixed costs are spread over increasing output initially, but diminishing returns eventually cause costs to rise.
Average variable cost is variable cost divided by quantity of output.
The average variable cost curve always lies below the average total cost curve because average total cost includes average variable cost and average fixed cost.
Marginal cost is the additional cost of producing one more unit of output.
Marginal cost is calculated by dividing the change in total cost by the change in quantity.
The marginal cost curve is generally upward-sloping due to diminishing marginal returns.
The marginal cost curve intersects the average total cost curve at the minimum point of the average total cost curve.
If marginal cost is below average total cost, producing an additional unit reduces average total cost.
If marginal cost is above average total cost, producing an additional unit increases average total cost.

learning_objectives: identify sunk costs.

knowledge_units: Sunk costs are a type of cost that firms examine to better understand profitability.
Firms break down costs into categories including fixed cost, marginal cost, average total cost, average variable costs, and sunk costs.
Fixed costs are often sunk costs that cannot be recouped.
Sunk costs should typically be ignored in decision-making because the spending has already been made and cannot be changed.
Variable costs convey information about a firm's ability to cut costs in the present and how costs will increase if production rises.
Average cost tells a firm whether it can earn profits given the current market price.
Average profit, or profit margin, equals price minus average cost.
If the market price is above average cost, average profit and total profit will be positive.
If the market price is below average cost, profits will be negative.
Marginal cost helps producers understand how profits would be affected by increasing or decreasing production.
The pattern of costs varies among industries and firms, with some having high fixed costs and low marginal costs.
Dividing total costs into fixed and variable costs provides a basis for calculating average total cost, average variable cost, and marginal cost.

learning_objectives: Interpret the relationship between short run and long run costs.

knowledge_units: What you’ll learn to do: differentiate between short-run and long-run costs.
Interpret the relationship between short run and long run costs.
The short run is a planning period over which at least one factor of production is fixed in quantity.
A fixed factor of production is a factor whose quantity cannot be changed during a particular period.
A variable factor of production is a factor whose quantity can be changed during a particular period.
The long run is a planning period over which all factors of production are variable.
A firm simultaneously makes both short-run and long-run decisions, with short-run choices involving fixed factors and long-run choices allowing all factors to be variable.
In the long run, all factors of production are variable, so there are no fixed costs and total cost equals total variable cost.
The long-run average cost (LRAC) curve shows the lowest possible cost per unit at each output level when all factors are variable.
The LRAC curve is derived from the optimal factor mix for each output level, but it does not necessarily pass through the minimum points of short-run average total cost curves.

learning_objectives: define and explain long-run costs, economies of scale, diseconomies of scale, and constant returns to scale.

knowledge_units: Economies of scale refer to the cost advantages that enterprises obtain due to their scale of operation, with cost per unit of output generally decreasing with increasing scale.
Diseconomies of scale occur when a company or business grows so large that the costs per unit increase.
Constant returns to scale occur when increasing inputs leads to a proportional increase in outputs, keeping costs per unit constant.
Long-run costs are the costs incurred by a firm when all inputs are variable, allowing for adjustments in scale of production.
The long run is the period of time when all costs are variable and no costs are fixed.
In the long run, a firm can adjust all inputs, such as building new factories or purchasing new machinery.
A firm will choose the production technology that allows it to produce the desired output at the lowest cost.
Production technology refers to all alternative methods of combining inputs to produce outputs.
Firms often face a choice between different combinations of labor and physical capital, as these inputs can substitute for each other.
As an input becomes more expensive, firms will attempt to conserve on using that input and shift to relatively less expensive inputs.
A profit-seeking firm will select the least expensive production technology given the prices of inputs.
Economies of scale occur when the cost per unit of output decreases as the quantity of output increases.
The long-run average cost curve is derived from a family of short-run average cost curves, each representing a different level of fixed costs.
In the long run, a firm can choose its level of fixed costs to operate on the least expensive average cost curve for any given output level.
Constant returns to scale occur when expanding all inputs does not change the average cost of production.
Diseconomies of scale occur when increasing output leads to higher average costs, often due to management inefficiencies in very large operations.
The shape of the long-run average cost curve determines the number and size distribution of firms in an industry.
If the long-run average cost curve has a unique minimum point, firms in the industry will tend to be similar in size.
If the long-run average cost curve has a flat-bottomed segment of constant returns to scale, firms of various sizes can compete.
The relationship between market demand and the output level at the minimum of the long-run average cost curve predicts the degree of competition in the market.
New production technologies can shift the long-run average cost curve, potentially altering the optimal scale of firms in an industry.

learning_objectives: define the characteristics of perfect competition.

knowledge_units: In perfect competition, firms are price-takers rather than price-makers.
Perfect competition involves identical products from multiple sellers.
Perfect competition is a market structure characterized by many firms producing identical products.
In perfect competition, many buyers and sellers are present in the market.
Perfect competition requires that sellers and buyers have all relevant information to make rational decisions.
Perfect competition involves free entry and exit of firms without restrictions.
A perfectly competitive firm is a price taker that must accept the prevailing market equilibrium price.
A perfectly competitive firm cannot raise its price above the market price without losing all sales to competitors.
The market price in perfect competition is determined solely by overall market supply and demand.
A perfectly competitive firm is a very small player in the overall market, so its output changes do not noticeably affect market price.
Agricultural markets are often used as examples of industries that approximate perfect competition.

learning_objectives: Explain and illustrate the differences between the demand curve for a perfectly competitive firm and that for a perfectly competitive industry

knowledge_units: A perfectly competitive market must accept the price determined by the industry and decide only how much to sell.
A perfectly competitive industry determines the equilibrium price through the interaction of market supply and demand.
Perfect competition is a market model where a large number of firms produce identical goods for a large number of buyers.
In perfect competition, it is easy for new firms to enter the market and for existing firms to leave.
Perfect competition assumes that buyers and sellers have complete information about market conditions.
In a perfectly competitive market, individual buyers and sellers are price takers who accept the market price as given.
The assumption of identical goods is necessary for firms to be price takers in a perfectly competitive market.
A large number of buyers and sellers ensures that no single buyer or seller can influence the market price.
Ease of entry and exit in a perfectly competitive market increases competition and affects the sustainability of economic profits.
Complete information about prices and technology ensures that all market participants buy or sell at the market price.
The model of perfect competition underlies the model of demand and supply, where market forces determine prices.
In a perfectly competitive market, each firm is a price taker, meaning the equilibrium price and industry output are determined by market demand and supply.
A perfectly competitive firm assumes it can sell any quantity of its product at the market price without affecting that price.
Price-taking behavior is central to the model of perfect competition because firms cannot charge a price higher than the market price due to identical products and complete information.
A perfectly competitive firm's total revenue is calculated by multiplying the market price by the quantity of output it produces.
The marginal revenue curve for a perfectly competitive firm is a horizontal line at the market price, which also represents the firm's average revenue and demand curve.
The demand curve facing a perfectly competitive firm is perfectly elastic, meaning the firm can sell any quantity at the given market price.
The market demand curve for a perfectly competitive industry is downward sloping, while the demand curve for an individual firm within that industry is horizontal at the market price.

learning_objectives: Calculate and graph the firm’s average, marginal and total revenues
Determine the profit maximizing output level and price using graphs and demand schedules; is able to calculate and graphically illustrate where marginal revenue equals marginal costs

knowledge_units: Perfectly competitive firms make their production decision based on the firm's costs.
The firm's costs include fixed, variable, average, marginal, and total costs.
A perfectly competitive firm is a price taker and faces a perfectly elastic demand curve at the market price.
The firm's only major decision is what quantity to produce because price is determined by the market.
Total revenue is calculated as price multiplied by quantity sold.
Profit is maximized at the output level where total revenue exceeds total cost by the greatest amount.
Marginal revenue is the additional revenue from selling one more unit and equals the market price for a perfectly competitive firm.
Marginal cost is the additional cost of producing one more unit.
A firm increases profit by expanding output when marginal revenue exceeds marginal cost.
A firm increases profit by reducing output when marginal cost exceeds marginal revenue.
Profit is maximized where marginal revenue equals marginal cost (MR = MC).
For a perfectly competitive firm, the profit-maximizing rule can be stated as producing where price equals marginal cost (P = MC).

learning_objectives: Measure total revenues as the area under the average revenue curves
Calculate and graphically illustrate profit and losses for a perfectly competitive firm

knowledge_units: Total revenues can be measured as the area under the average revenue curve.
Profit and losses for a perfectly competitive firm can be calculated and graphically illustrated using cost and revenue curves.
A perfectly competitive firm earns economic profit when the market price is greater than its average total cost at the profit-maximizing quantity.
A perfectly competitive firm earns zero economic profit when the market price equals its average total cost at the profit-maximizing quantity.
A perfectly competitive firm incurs an economic loss when the market price is less than its average total cost at the profit-maximizing quantity.
Profit or loss for a perfectly competitive firm can be calculated as (price minus average cost) multiplied by quantity.
Total revenue for a perfectly competitive firm is represented graphically as the area of a rectangle with height equal to price and width equal to quantity produced.

learning_objectives: determine the break-even, and the shutdown points of production for a perfectly competitive firm.

knowledge_units: A firm may continue producing even when experiencing losses if it is advantageous to do so.

What you’ll learn to do: determine the break-even, and the shutdown points of production for a perfectly competitive firm In this outcome, you will see why it is sometimes advantageous for a firm to continue producing even when it is experiencing losses.
In the short run, a firm must pay fixed costs even if it shuts down and produces zero output.
A firm should shut down if the market price is below the minimum average variable cost.
The shutdown point is the price at which the marginal cost curve intersects the average variable cost curve.
If the price is above the shutdown point, the firm should continue to operate in the short run even if it incurs losses.
For a perfectly competitive firm, the marginal cost curve above the minimum average variable cost is the firm's supply curve.
A firm maximizes profit by producing where price equals marginal cost, provided the price is at least equal to the minimum average variable cost.

learning_objectives: Explain the concept of “zero economic profit”

knowledge_units: In the short run, firms cannot adjust their fixed inputs.
In the long run, firms can adjust all factors of production to remain productive.
In the long run, firms can adjust all factors of production, unlike in the short run where fixed inputs cannot be changed.
Entry occurs when new firms enter an industry in response to increased industry profits.
Exit is the long-run process of reducing production or ceasing production in response to sustained losses.
In a perfectly competitive market, entry and exit drive the market price to the zero-profit level in the long run.
Zero economic profit occurs when price equals the minimum of average total cost, where no firm has an incentive to enter or exit.
Short-run economic profits attract new firms, increasing supply and driving the price down to zero profit.
Short-run economic losses cause firms to exit, decreasing supply and driving the price up to zero profit.
A constant cost industry has a perfectly elastic supply of inputs, so long-run equilibrium price remains unchanged after demand increases.
An increasing cost industry experiences rising production costs as demand increases, leading to a higher long-run equilibrium price.
A decreasing cost industry experiences falling production costs as demand increases, leading to a lower long-run equilibrium price.

learning_objectives: understand why perfectly competitive markets are efficient.

knowledge_units: Perfectly competitive markets can be both allocatively and productively efficient.
Allocative efficiency means resources are allocated without waste.
Productive efficiency means markets provide the maximal amount of satisfaction for society.
In perfectly competitive markets, profit-maximizing firms and utility-maximizing consumers lead to both productive and allocative efficiency.
Productive efficiency means producing without waste, placing the economy on the production possibility frontier.
In the long run, perfectly competitive markets achieve productive efficiency because price equals the minimum of the long-run average cost curve.
Allocative efficiency means the chosen point on the production possibility frontier is socially preferred.
In perfectly competitive markets, allocative efficiency occurs when price equals marginal cost.
Price measures the social benefit of a good, while marginal cost represents the social cost of production.
When price equals marginal cost, the social benefits of production align with the social costs.
If firms produce less than the allocatively efficient quantity, price exceeds marginal cost, indicating society benefits from increased production.
If firms produce more than the allocatively efficient quantity, price is less than marginal cost, indicating society benefits from decreased production.
Perfect competition in the long run is a hypothetical benchmark; real-world market structures like monopoly do not achieve productive and allocative efficiency.
Real-world issues such as pollution, poverty, and imperfect information are assumed away in the perfect competition model.

learning_objectives: define the characteristics of a monopoly.

knowledge_units: A monopoly is a market structure where a single firm controls the entire market for a product.
Firms seek to become monopolists to eliminate competition and secure guaranteed profits.
A monopoly is a market structure where one firm produces all of the output in a market.
A monopoly faces no significant competition and can charge any price it wishes.
