Sponges exhibit diversity in body forms, including variations in spongocoel shape and the arrangement of feeding chambers.
Sponges lack true tissue layers but have functional 'tissues' composed of specialized cell types.
Pinacocytes form the outer protective layer called the pinacoderm.
Ostia are pores that allow water entry, and in some sponges, they are formed by porocytes that regulate water flow.
The mesohyl is a jelly-like substance containing collagenous fibers that acts like an endoskeleton.
Choanocytes line feeding chambers, generate water currents, and trap food particles via phagocytosis.
Choanocytes can differentiate into sperm for sexual reproduction.
Amoebocytes move through the mesohyl, deliver nutrients, give rise to eggs, and differentiate into other cell types.
Spicules are skeletal structures made of calcium carbonate or silica that provide support and may deter predation.
Sponges are classified into classes based on spicule composition and presence of spongin.
All digestion in sponges is intracellular, requiring food particles to be smaller than individual cells.
Gas exchange, circulation, and excretion occur by diffusion between cells and water passing through the sponge.
Sponges reproduce asexually through fragmentation, budding, or gemmule formation in freshwater species.
Sexual reproduction involves hermaphroditic gamete production, with oocytes retained and sperm ejected via the osculum.
Sponges are generally sessile as adults but can exhibit limited creeping movement via organizational plasticity.

learning_objectives: Compare structural and organizational characteristics of Porifera and Cnidaria
Describe the progressive development of tissues and their relevance to animal complexity
Identify the two general body forms found in the Cnidaria
Describe the identifying features of the major cnidarian classes

knowledge_units: Cnidarians are diploblastic animals that develop from two embryonic layers: ectoderm and endoderm.
The defining cell type for cnidarians is the cnidocyte, which contains a stinging organelle called a nematocyst used for prey capture and defense.
Cnidarians exhibit two distinct body plans: the sessile polyp form and the motile medusa form.
Some cnidarians are dimorphic, exhibiting both polyp and medusa body plans during their life cycle, with the polyp serving as the asexual phase and the medusa as the sexual phase.
Cnidarians have two epithelial layers: an outer epidermis derived from ectoderm and an inner gastrodermis derived from endoderm, separated by a non-living mesoglea.
Cnidarians have a rudimentary nervous system organized as a nerve net, which may include nerve plexi or cords, and is more complex in motile medusae than in sessile polyps.
Cnidarians possess an incomplete digestive system with a single gastrovascular cavity that functions in both digestion and nutrient distribution.
Cnidarians exchange gases by diffusion and lack a circulatory system, which limits body wall thickness.
The phylum Cnidaria is divided into two monophyletic clades: Anthozoa, which include only sessile polyp forms, and Medusozoa, which include species with both polyp and medusa forms.
Anthozoa includes sea anemones, sea pens, and corals.
Sea anemones have a cylindrical body with a mouth surrounded by tentacles bearing cnidocytes.
The pharynx of a sea anemone is lined with ectoderm and contains a siphonoglyph that directs water into it.
The gastrovascular cavity of a sea anemone is divided by mesenteries, which increase surface area for absorption and provide support.
Sea anemones feed on small fish and shrimp, immobilizing prey with nematocysts.
Some sea anemones have mutualistic relationships with hermit crabs or clownfish.
Coral polyps are similar in structure to anemones but are smaller and often colonial.
Many anthozoans, including corals, have symbiotic relationships with zooxanthellae algae that provide nutrients and color.
Anthozoans remain polypoid throughout their lives and can reproduce asexually by budding or fragmentation, or sexually via gametes.
Sexual reproduction in anthozoans produces a free-swimming planula larva that settles into a sessile polyp.
Class Scyphozoa includes all marine jellies, with the medusa as the prominent life cycle stage.
Scyphozoans display a characteristic bell-like morphology with a mouth on the underside surrounded by tentacles bearing nematocysts.
The gastrovascular cavity in scyphozoans may be sectioned into four interconnected diverticuli or radial canals to increase surface area for nutrient absorption and support the body.
Scyphozoans have a nerve net with a nerve ring around the bell edge and sensory organs called rhopalia.
A ring of muscles lining the bell provides contractile force for swimming and drawing in food.
Scyphozoans have separate sexes with gonads formed from the gastrodermis, and gametes are expelled through the mouth.
The life cycle of most scyphozoans includes both sexual medusoid and asexual polypoid body forms, with planula larvae settling as polyps that may bud or produce medusa buds.
Cubozoans have a box-shaped medusa with a square cross-section bell.
Cubozoans have muscular pads called pedalia at the corners of the bell, with one or more tentacles attached to each.
Cubozoans are the most venomous of all cnidarians.
Cubozoans possess image-forming eyes with a cornea, lens, and retina, which are considered true organs.
Cubozoans have separate sexes and internal fertilization.
The cubozoan life cycle includes a planula larva that develops into a polyp, which can form a colony and then transform into a medusa.
Hydrozoa is a diverse group of cnidarians that includes nearly 3,200 species, with most being marine.
Most hydrozoan species exhibit both polypoid and medusoid forms in their life cycles, though some like Hydra only have the polyp form.
The medusoid form of Hydrozoa, called a hydromedusa, has a muscular velum below the margin of the bell.
Many hydrozoans form sessile, branched colonies of specialized polyps that share a common gastrovascular cavity.
Siphonophores are free-floating colonial hydrozoans containing specialized polypoid and medusoid individuals for feeding, defense, or reproduction.
The Portuguese man o' war is a free-floating hydrozoan colony composed of many specialized zooids, not a single organism.
A defining characteristic of hydrozoans is that their gonads are derived from epidermal tissue, unlike other cnidarians where they are derived from gastrodermal tissue.

learning_objectives: Describe the unique anatomical and morphological features of flatworms, rotifers, and Nemertea
Identify an important extracoelomic cavity found in Nemertea
Explain the key features of Platyhelminthes and their importance as parasites

knowledge_units: Lophotrochozoa are triploblastic animals with three germ layers and bilateral symmetry.
Lophotrochozoa are protostomes where the blastopore becomes the mouth.
Acoelomates have a solid mass of mesoderm without a body cavity.
Pseudocoelomates have a body cavity lined by mesoderm only on the outside.
Eucoelomates have a body cavity lined by mesoderm on both the outside and inside.
Eucoelomate protostomes typically form their coelom by schizocoely.
Lophotrochozoa are characterized by either a lophophore feeding apparatus or trochophore larvae.
Flatworms are acoelomate organisms that include both free-living and parasitic forms.
Platyhelminthes consist of two monophyletic lineages: Catenulida and Rhabditophora.
Flatworms have three embryonic tissue layers: ectoderm, endoderm, and mesoderm.
Most flatworms have a branching gastrovascular cavity where the mouth also serves as an anus.
Flatworms have an excretory system with flame cells that regulate salts and excrete nitrogenous wastes.
Most flatworm species are monoecious, with internal fertilization and common asexual reproduction by fission.
Trematodes are internal parasites with complex life cycles involving a primary vertebrate host and a secondary mollusk host.
Schistosomiasis is a serious human disease caused by trematodes of the genus Schistosoma.
Cestodes are internal parasites that lack a digestive system and absorb nutrients from the host's intestine.
Tapeworms have a body composed of proglottids, each containing reproductive structures.
Rotifers are microscopic, mostly aquatic animals named for their ciliated corona that appears to rotate.
The rotifer body is pseudocoelomate and divided into a head, trunk, and foot.
Rotifers are filter feeders that consume dead material, algae, and other microscopic organisms, making them important in aquatic food webs.
Food is directed to the mouth by coronal cilia and processed in a muscular pharynx called the mastax.
Many rotifer species are dioecious and exhibit sexual dimorphism, with males often smaller and lacking a digestive system.
Haplodiploidy is a sex determination method in rotifers where fertilized eggs develop into females and unfertilized eggs into males.
Bdelloid rotifers reproduce exclusively by parthenogenesis, resulting in all-female populations.
Bdelloid rotifers may compensate for genetic insularity by incorporating genes from other species into their genome.
Nemerteans are soft, unsegmented animals with bilateral symmetry and a flattened tube-like morphology.
A unique characteristic of Nemertea is the presence of an eversible proboscis enclosed in a fluid-filled rhynchocoel, which is not part of the coelom.
The proboscis is used for food capture and can be extended by hydrostatic pressure from the rhynchocoel.
Nemerteans have a closed circulatory system derived from the coelomic cavity of the embryo.
The nervous system includes a brain of four ganglia around the rhynchocoel and paired longitudinal nerve cords.
Nemerteans have excellent powers of regeneration and can reproduce asexually by fragmentation.
Most nemerteans are dioecious with external fertilization and spiral cleavage, typical of lophotrochozoan protostomes.

learning_objectives: Describe the unique anatomical and morphological features of mollusks and annelids
Describe the formation of the coelom
Identify an important extracoelomic cavity in mollusks
Describe the major body regions of Mollusca and how they vary in different molluscan classes
Discuss the advantages of true body segmentation
Describe the features of animals classified in phylum Annelida

knowledge_units: Annelids and mollusks are lophotrochozoan protostomes that share trochophore larvae, indicating common ancestry.
Mollusca is the second most species-rich animal phylum, with nearly 100,000 described extant species.
Annelids and mollusks are bilaterally symmetrical, cephalized, triploblastic, schizocoelous eucoelomates.
Mollusks are predominantly marine animals that also inhabit freshwater and terrestrial habitats.
The chief locomotor structure in mollusks is usually a muscular foot.
Most internal organs in mollusks are contained in a region called the visceral mass.
Overlying the visceral mass is a fold of tissue called the mantle, which contains respiratory structures called gills.
The mouths of most mollusks contain a specialized feeding organ called a radula, except in bivalves.
The mantle secretes a calcium-carbonate-hardened shell in most mollusks, though it is reduced in cephalopods.
Mollusks are schizocoelous eucoelomates, but the coelomic cavity in adults is largely reduced to a cavity around the heart.
The mantle cavity is the major internal body chamber in mollusks due to coelomic reduction.
The radula is a rasp-like organ with chitinous teeth used to shred or scrape food, present in all mollusk groups except bivalves.
Most mollusks are dioecious with external fertilization, except in terrestrial mollusks and cephalopods.
Most aquatic mollusks produce a trochophore larva, while cephalopods develop directly into juvenile forms.
Phylum Mollusca is organized into eight classes, including Caudofoveata, Solenogastres, Monoplacophora, Polyplacophora, Gastropoda, Cephalopoda, Bivalvia, and Scaphopoda.
Class Polyplacophora (chitons) bear eight limy plates and have a broad, ventral foot adapted for suction.
Class Bivalvia includes clams, oysters, and mussels, which are filter-feeders enclosed in two-part shells and lack a radula.
Class Gastropoda includes snails and slugs, characterized by torsion during embryonic development that rotates the visceral mass.
Class Cephalopoda includes octopuses and squids, which are carnivorous predators with a well-developed nervous system and closed circulatory system.
Cephalopods use chromatophores, iridophores, and leucophores for rapid color changes, likely for camouflage and signaling.
Class Scaphopoda (tusk shells) have a single conical shell open at both ends and lack ctenidia, using the mantle cavity for respiration.
Phylum Annelida comprises true, segmented worms found in marine, terrestrial, and freshwater habitats.
Annelids exhibit metamerism, or true segmentation, where internal and external features are repeated in each body segment.
Metamerism allows annelids to increase body size by adding compartments and enhances movement efficiency.
Annelids have a body plan divided into head, body, and pygidium, with segments arising sequentially via teloblastic growth.
The annelid epidermis is protected by a thin collagenous cuticle that does not require periodic shedding for growth.
Annelids possess a well-developed, complete digestive system with varied feeding mechanisms across the phylum.
Most annelids have a closed circulatory system with dorsal and ventral blood vessels connected by transverse loops in each segment.
Gas exchange in annelids occurs across the moist body surface, with polychaetes using vascularized parapodia for respiration.
Excretion in annelids is facilitated by a pair of metanephridia present in every segment.
Annelids may be monoecious or dioecious, with cross-fertilization preferred even in hermaphroditic species.
Phylum Annelida includes class Polychaeta (polychaetes) and class Oligochaeta (earthworms, leeches, and relatives).
Polychaetes are characterized by parapodia bearing clusters of bristles, while oligochaetes have a permanent clitellum and reduced chaetae.
Leeches lack setae, have anterior and posterior suckers, and may show mismatched external and internal segmentation to accommodate blood ingestion.

learning_objectives: Describe the structural organization of nematodes
Describe the importance of Caenorhabditis elegans in research
Describe the features of Tardigrades

knowledge_units: The superphylum Ecdysozoa includes the phyla Nematoda and Arthropoda, which are among the most diverse animal groups.
Ecdysozoans are characterized by a cuticle, a tough but flexible exoskeleton that protects against water loss and predators.
All members of Ecdysozoa undergo periodic molting, a process called ecdysis, where the old cuticle is shed and replaced by a new one.
Nematodes are triploblastic, bilaterally symmetrical pseudocoelomates with a tubular morphology and circular cross-section.
Nematodes include both free-living and parasitic forms.
The free-living nematode Caenorhabditis elegans is extensively used as a model system in biological research.
Nematodes have a complete digestive system with a distinct mouth and anus.
The nematode cuticle is rich in collagen and chitin, forming a protective armor.
Nematodes have only a longitudinal muscle layer, resulting in a whiplike body movement.
Nitrogenous wastes in nematodes are largely released directly across the body wall.
Most nematodes have four longitudinal nerve cords that fuse anteriorly to form a pharyngeal nerve ring.
Nematodes employ a variety of reproductive strategies, including monoecious, dioecious, and parthenogenetic.
Caenorhabditis elegans is mostly monoecious with self-fertilizing hermaphrodites and some males.
Nematode embryonic development includes four larval stages with molting between each stage.
Adverse conditions can lead to formation of a dauer larva in some nematodes.
Some nematodes exhibit eutely, with a specific number of cells in the body.
Parasitic nematodes often have complex life cycles involving multiple hosts and can cause significant medical impacts.
Tardigrades are microscopic animals found in marine, freshwater, and damp terrestrial environments worldwide.
Tardigrades are known for entering cryptobiosis, a state that provides resistance to extreme environmental challenges like desiccation, temperature extremes, vacuum, pressure, and radiation.
During cryptobiosis, tardigrades can suspend metabolic activity for years and survive the loss of up to 99% of their body water.
Tardigrades have a cylindrical body with four pairs of legs ending in claws, covered by a chitinous cuticle.
Tardigrades feed using a chitinous stylet to pierce plant or animal cells and a muscular pharynx to ingest contents.
Tardigrades lack specialized circulatory and respiratory systems, with a hemocoel as the main body cavity.
Most tardigrades are dioecious, with external fertilization and direct development involving a fixed number of cells.

learning_objectives: Compare the internal systems and appendage specializations of phylum Arthropoda
Discuss the environmental importance of arthropods
Discuss the reasons for arthropod success and abundance

knowledge_units: Arthropods are coelomate organisms characterized by a sturdy chitinous exoskeleton and jointed appendages.
All arthropods periodically undergo molting and ecdysis as they grow.
Arthropods are eucoelomate, protostomic organisms often with complicated life cycles.
Arthropods are characterized by jointed appendages, segmented bodies, and a chitinous exoskeleton.
The arthropod exoskeleton is made of chitin, a waterproof polysaccharide, and must be shed through molting to allow growth.
Arthropods have an open circulatory system with a hemocoel and a dorsal heart that pumps hemolymph.
Respiratory systems in arthropods vary: insects and myriapods use tracheae, crustaceans use gills, and terrestrial chelicerates use book lungs.
Phylum Arthropoda is the most species-rich animal phylum, with insects forming the largest class.
Arthropods are classified into five subphyla: Trilobita, Chelicerata, Myriapoda, Crustacea, and Hexapoda.
Chelicerates have a body divided into a prosoma and opisthosoma, and their first appendages are chelicerae.
Myriapods are terrestrial arthropods with numerous legs, including centipedes and millipedes.
Crustaceans are predominantly aquatic arthropods with two pairs of antennae and biramous appendages.
Hexapods, or insects, have six legs and typically three body tagmata: head, thorax, and abdomen.
The evolution of wings in insects was a key innovation that contributed to their ecological dominance.
Insects exhibit diverse metamorphosis patterns, including complete and incomplete metamorphosis.

learning_objectives: Describe the distinguishing characteristics of echinoderms
Describe the distinguishing characteristics of chordates

knowledge_units: Echinoderms and chordates belong to the superphylum Deuterostomia.
Deuterostomes are characterized by the mouth developing as a secondary opening opposite the blastopore, which becomes the anus.
Deuterostomes typically exhibit radial regulative cleavage and form an enterocoelom from coelomic pouches.
Cleavage in most deuterostomes is indeterminate, meaning early embryonic cell fates are not fixed.
The deuterostome clades are Chordata and Ambulacraria, which includes echinoderms and hemichordates.
Echinoderms produce an endoskeleton made of tiny skeletal ossicles covered by epidermis.
Echinoderms are exclusively marine, bottom-dwelling organisms with about 7,000 described living species.
Adult echinoderms exhibit pentaradial symmetry, typically with arms arrayed in multiples of five.
Echinoderms have an endoskeleton made of calcareous ossicles covered by the epidermis.
Echinoderms possess a unique ambulacral (water vascular) system derived from the coelom.
The water vascular system includes a central ring canal, radial canals, and tube feet that function in locomotion, feeding, and sensory perception.
Echinoderms have a relatively simple nervous system consisting of a circumoral nerve ring and radial nerves without a brain.
Echinoderms are dioecious with external fertilization, and their early larval stages have bilateral symmetry.
Echinoderms can reproduce asexually by fragmentation and regenerate lost body parts.
The phylum Echinoderms is divided into five extant classes: Asteroidea, Ophiuroidea, Echinoidea, Crinoidea, and Holothuroidea.
Sea stars (Asteroidea) have thick arms extending from a central disk, with organs branching into the arms, and use tube feet for gripping and grasping prey.
Sea stars are carnivores that can evert their stomachs to digest prey externally, such as into mollusk shells.
Brittle stars (Ophiuroidea) have long, thin, flexible arms sharply demarcated from the central disk and move by lashing or pulling with their arms.
Sea urchins and sand dollars (Echinoidea) lack arms, have a continuous internal shell called a test, and use a feeding structure called Aristotle's lantern.
Crinoids (Crinoidea) include sessile sea lilies and mobile feather stars, which are suspension feeders using feather-like arms lined with tube feet.
Sea cucumbers (Holothuroidea) exhibit functional bilateral symmetry as adults due to an extended oral-aboral axis and have reduced tube feet.
Some sea cucumbers are unique among echinoderms in having hemoglobin circulating in their coelomic fluid.
Chordates share five key features at some stage of development: a notochord, a dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, a post-anal tail, and an endostyle/thyroid gland.
In some chordate groups, these distinguishing traits are present only during embryonic development.
The phylum Chordata includes invertebrate clades such as Urochordata (tunicates) and Cephalochordata (lancelets).

learning_objectives: Describe the major evolutionary innovations that distinguish vertebrates from invertebrate chordates, including the emergence of quadrupeds and the amniotic egg.
Explain how modifications of keratinous epidermal structures contributed to the diversification of vertebrates, such as scales, hair, and feathers.
Identify the primary threats to vertebrate species and summarize current conservation efforts, such as the Biodiversity Action Plan.

knowledge_units: Vertebrates exhibit two major evolutionary innovations from invertebrate chordates: the emergence of quadrupeds and the evolution of the amniotic egg.
The amniotic egg freed terrestrial animals from dependence on water for fertilization and embryonic development.
Modifications of keratinous epidermal structures in vertebrates gave rise to scales, claws, hair, and feathers.
Scales in reptiles sealed their skins against water loss, while hair and feathers provided insulation to support endothermy.
Vertebrate species face extinction primarily due to habitat loss and pollution.
The Biodiversity Action Plan is an international program designed to protect species and habitats.

learning_objectives: Describe the distinguishing characteristics of chordates
Identify the derived characteristics of craniates that set them apart from other chordates
Describe the developmental fate of the notochord in vertebrates

knowledge_units: Vertebrates belong to the phylum Chordata within the kingdom Animalia.
Chordata is one of the two major phyla of deuterostomes, which are characterized by embryonic development where the mouth forms second.
The phylum Chordata includes both vertebrates and two groups of invertebrate chordates.

Chordates are characterized by five key features that appear at some stage of development: a notochord, a dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal gill arches or slits, a post-anal tail, and an endostyle/thyroid gland.
The notochord is a flexible, rod-shaped mesodermal structure that provides skeletal support and a site for muscle attachment in chordates.
In vertebrates, the notochord is present only during embryonic development, where it induces neural tube development and is later replaced by the vertebral column.
Chordates possess five distinctive features at some point during their development: a notochord, dorsal hollow tubular nerve cord, pharyngeal slits, endostyle/thyroid gland, and a post-anal tail.
Cephalochordata are invertebrate chordates that retain all five chordate features in the adult stage.
In Cephalochordata, the notochord extends into the head region.
Cephalochordates are suspension feeders that use pharyngeal slits to filter food particles from water.
Urochordata (tunicates) are invertebrate chordates whose adults lack a notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, and post-anal tail.
The larval form of urochordates possesses all five chordate features, but most are lost during metamorphosis into the adult form.
Adult tunicates retain pharyngeal slits and an endostyle as chordate features.
Most tunicates are sessile suspension feeders that filter plankton and detritus from seawater.
Craniates/vertebrates are characterized by the presence of a cranium, mandible, and other facial bones.
Vertebrates are named for the vertebral column, which is composed of vertebrae that form a backbone.
In vertebrates, vertebrae initially form in segments around the embryonic notochord but eventually replace it in adults.
In most derived vertebrates, the notochord becomes the nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral discs.
Craniata/Vertebrata is a monophyletic group that shares the five basic chordate characteristics with other chordate subphyla.
Gnathostomes are animals that possess jaws and include fishes and tetrapods.
Tetrapods are vertebrates with four limbs and include amphibians, reptiles, birds, and mammals.
Amniotes are tetrapods whose eggs contain four extraembryonic membranes and are adapted for terrestrial living.

learning_objectives: Describe the difference between jawless and jawed fishes
Discuss the distinguishing features of sharks and rays compared to other modern fishes

knowledge_units: Fishes are the most species-rich clade within the Vertebrata.
Jawless fishes were the earliest vertebrates, with jawed fishes evolving later.
The Agnatha (jawless fishes) include hagfishes and lampreys, which have a distinct cranium and complex sense organs.
Jawless fishes (Agnatha) are craniates that lack jaws and paired lateral appendages (fins).
Recent molecular and embryological data support that living agnathans (cyclostomes) are monophyletic.
The defining features of living jawless fishes include the lack of jaws, lack of paired lateral appendages, and lack of internal ossification and scales.
Some early jawless fishes, such as ostracoderms, were encased in bony armor, unlike present-day jawless fishes.
Hagfishes (class Myxini) are eel-like scavengers that feed using keratinized teeth on a movable cartilaginous plate.
Hagfishes have a cartilaginous skull and a notochord that is not replaced by a vertebral column.
Hagfishes produce slippery mucus from slime glands to escape predators.
Lampreys (class Petromyzontida) possess extrinsic eye muscles, semicircular canals, a true cerebellum, and simple vertebral elements called arcualia.
Lampreys have a parasitic stage in their life cycle where they attach to fish using a rasping, toothed mouth.
Lampreys spawn in fresh water, and their larvae (ammocoetes) resemble adult cephalochordates.
Gnathostomes are vertebrates that possess true jaws, which evolved from the first pair of gill arches in jawless fishes.
The evolution of jaws and paired fins allowed gnathostomes to shift from scavenging and suspension feeding to active predation.
Most modern fishes are gnathostomes belonging to the clades Chondrichthyes and Osteichthyes.
Chondrichthyes are jawed fishes with a skeleton made of cartilage, which is a secondarily derived trait from bony ancestors.
Sharks have abrasive skin covered with tooth-like placoid scales, and their teeth likely evolved from these scales.
Sharks possess electroreceptors called ampullae of Lorenzini to detect electromagnetic fields produced by prey.
Sharks have a lateral line system to detect movement and vibration in water, similar to hearing in terrestrial vertebrates.
Most sharks are ovoviviparous, retaining fertilized eggs in the oviduct where embryos are nourished by yolk and born alive.
Rays and skates are distinguished from sharks by their flattened bodies, pectoral fins fused to the head, and ventral gill slits.
Osteichthyes, or bony fishes, are characterized by an ossified skeleton and include the vast majority of present-day fishes.
Many bony fishes have a swim bladder, a gas-filled organ that helps control buoyancy and is homologous to lungs in other vertebrates.
Actinopterygii (ray-finned fishes) have fins supported by slender bones, while Sarcopterygii (lobe-finned fishes) have fleshy, lobed fins.

learning_objectives: Describe the important difference between the life cycle of amphibians and the life cycles of other vertebrates
Distinguish between the characteristics of Urodela, Anura, and Apoda
Describe the evolutionary history of amphibians

knowledge_units: Amphibians are vertebrate tetrapods that include frogs, salamanders, and caecilians.
The term 'amphibian' refers to the metamorphosis and the mix of aquatic and terrestrial phases in their life cycle.
Amphibians cannot stray far from water because their reproduction is tied to aqueous environments.
Amphibians evolved during the Devonian period and were the earliest terrestrial tetrapods.
Amphibians represent an evolutionary transition from water to land that occurred over many millions of years.
Amphibians are the only living true vertebrates that have made a transition from water to land in both their ontogeny and phylogeny.
Most amphibians are tetrapods with four well-developed limbs, though some salamanders have reduced hindlimbs and all caecilians are limbless.
Extant amphibians have moist, permeable skin with mucus glands that facilitates water absorption and serves as a respiratory surface.
Amphibians use three respiratory surfaces: skin, lungs, and the buccal cavity, with air moved into the lungs by positive pressure.
All extant adult amphibians are carnivorous, and their teeth are pedicellate, with a calcified root and crown separated by noncalcified tissue.
Amphibians have image-forming eyes with color vision, and frogs have specialized inner ear structures for detecting a range of sound frequencies.
The first tetrapods were now-extinct amphibian species dating to nearly 400 million years ago.
Tetrapods evolved from lobe-finned freshwater fishes during the Devonian period, representing a shift from aquatic respiration and swimming to air-breathing and terrestrial movement.

Early aquatic tetrapods like Ichthyostega and Acanthostega had both gills and lungs, four limbs, and skeletal structures similar to modern tetrapods, but their limbs were not fully adapted for terrestrial support.
Tiktaalik roseae is considered an intermediate form between sarcopterygian fishes and early tetrapods, with features like a neck and weight-bearing fin bones.
The move onto land gave early tetrapods access to new nutrient sources and fewer predators, leading to their widespread distribution during the early Carboniferous period.
All living amphibians are classified in the subclass Lissamphibia, which is divided into three clades: Urodela (salamanders), Anura (frogs), and Apoda (caecilians).
Salamanders (Urodela) are probably the most similar to ancestral amphibians and typically have a generalized tetrapod body plan with four limbs and a tail.
Most salamanders are lungless and respire through their skin or external gills.
Salamanders reproduce using internal fertilization, where males deposit a spermatophore that the female picks up.
Frogs (Anura) have a body plan specialized for jumping, using their hind limbs to capture prey on land.
Frog eggs are fertilized externally and are typically laid in moist environments to prevent dehydration.
The life cycle of most frogs includes a larval stage (tadpole) that undergoes metamorphosis into an adult form.
During metamorphosis, tadpoles lose their gills and tail, develop limbs, and transform their digestive system for carnivorous feeding.
Caecilians (Apoda) are limbless amphibians adapted for burrowing or aquatic lifestyles.
Caecilians have internal fertilization, and most species bear live young, nourishing them with oviduct tissue and skin secretions.
During the Paleozoic Era, vertebrates moved from water to land, influenced by the emergence of land plants and changing atmospheric conditions.
The late Paleozoic was a time of vertebrate diversification, including the emergence of amniotes that gave rise to mammals and reptiles.

learning_objectives: Describe the main characteristics of amniotes
Explain the difference between anapsids, synapsids, and diapsids, and give an example of each
Identify the characteristics of reptiles
Discuss the evolution of reptiles

knowledge_units: Reptiles are distinguished from amphibians by their terrestrially adapted egg, which is supported by four extraembryonic membranes: the yolk sac, amnion, chorion, and allantois.
The amnion forms a fluid-filled cavity that provides the embryo with its own internal aquatic environment.
The evolution of extraembryonic membranes led to less dependence on water for development, allowing amniotes to branch out into drier environments.
The eggs of birds, reptiles, and a few mammals have shells that permit the retention of water and nutrients for the developing embryo.
Bird egg shells are hardened with calcium carbonate, making them rigid, while most nonavian reptile egg shells are leathery and require a moist environment.
Most mammals do not lay eggs; instead, the embryo grows within the mother’s body, with the placenta derived from two of the extraembryonic membranes.
The amniotic egg is the key characteristic of amniotes.
The shell of the amniotic egg provides protection while allowing gas exchange.
The albumin provides water and protein, and the yolk provides nutrients to the embryo.
The yolk sac transports nutrients, the chorion facilitates gas exchange, the allantois stores wastes and aids respiration, and the amnion protects and hydrates the embryo.
In mammals, the placenta, derived from the chorion and allantois, transports nutrients and manages gas exchange and waste.
The first amniotes evolved from tetrapod ancestors approximately 340 million years ago during the Carboniferous period.
Early amniotes diverged into two main lines: synapsids and sauropsids.
Synapsids included therapsids, a clade from which mammals evolved.
Sauropsids were divided into anapsids and diapsids.
Diapsids gave rise to reptiles, including dinosaurs and birds.
The key difference between synapsids, anapsids, and diapsids is the number of temporal fenestrae behind each eye.
Anapsids have no temporal fenestrae, synapsids have one, and diapsids have two.
Turtles are now considered diapsids that secondarily lost their temporal fenestrae.
Diapsids diverged into Archosauromorpha and Lepidosauromorpha during the Mesozoic period.
Lepidosaurs include modern lizards, snakes, and tuataras.
Archosaurs include crocodilians, extinct ichthyosaurs, pterosaurs, dinosaurs, and birds.
Birds evolved from maniraptoran theropod dinosaurs.
Derived characteristics of amniotes include the amniotic egg, thicker waterproof skin, and rib ventilation of the lungs.
Reptiles are tetrapods, including limbless species descended from four-limbed ancestors.
Reptiles lay calcareous or leathery eggs on land, with internal fertilization and various reproductive modes like ovoviviparity and viviparity.
Scaly skin containing keratin and waxy lipids reduces water loss, enabling reptiles to live on land.
Reptiles breathe exclusively with lungs due to their occlusive skin, using muscular mechanisms to create negative pressure for ventilation.
Most reptiles are ectotherms that rely on environmental heat and behavioral adaptations for thermoregulation, allowing them to survive on fewer calories than endotherms.
Reptiles originated approximately 300 million years ago during the Carboniferous period.
Early amniotes diverged into synapsids, anapsids, and diapsids during the Permian period.
Diapsid reptiles further diverged into stem archosaurs and lepidosaurs during the Permian period.
Archosaurs became the dominant terrestrial group during the Triassic period, possibly due to the extinction of large-bodied anapsids and synapsids.
Pterosaurs were distinct from true dinosaurs and had adaptations for flight, including hollow bones and wing membranes.
Dinosaurs are divided into Saurischia (lizard-hipped) and Ornithischia (bird-hipped) based on pelvic structure.
Birds evolved from the saurischian lineage, not the ornithischian lineage.
Dinosaurs were the largest land animals to have lived, with some species like Argentinosaurus reaching nearly 40 meters in length.
Early dinosaurs like Herrerasaurus were bipedal with legs positioned directly below the pelvis.
Ornithischian dinosaurs were all herbivores and included diverse forms such as ankylosaurs and horned dinosaurs like Triceratops.
Both ornithischian and saurischian dinosaurs provided parental care for their young.
The extinction of non-avian dinosaurs occurred about 65 million years ago, coinciding with a large asteroid impact and massive volcanic activity.
Pterosaurs were flying reptiles that dominated Mesozoic skies for over 170 million years.
Pterosaurs had ultralight skeletons with a unique pteroid bone that strengthened the forewing membrane.
The pterosaur wing was supported by a greatly elongated fourth finger.
Pterosaurs had a reptilian wing composed of muscle, connective tissue, and blood vessels reinforced with fibrous cords.
Dinosaurs were a diverse group of terrestrial reptiles that dominated the Mesozoic era.
Some dinosaurs were quadrupeds and others were bipeds, with carnivorous and herbivorous species.
Dinosaurs laid eggs, and fossil evidence suggests some exhibited parental care.
Birds are the only living descendants of theropod dinosaurs.
Class Reptilia includes four living clades: Crocodilia, Sphenodontia, Squamata, and Testudines.
Crocodilians are descended from terrestrial reptiles and can walk and run on land, though they spend most of their time in water.
Sphenodontia, represented by tuataras, have a primitive diapsid skull and a vestigial parietal eye that senses light.
Squamata, the largest reptile clade, includes lizards and snakes, which evolved from a lepidosaurian ancestor.
Most lizards have four limbs, eyelids, and external ears, while snakes lack these features.
Snakes have a highly flexible skull with mandibles connected by skin and muscle, allowing them to swallow large prey.
Testudines are characterized by a bony or cartilaginous shell incorporated into their skeletal system, consisting of a carapace and plastron.
Turtles retract their necks in different ways: Cryptodira use a vertical S-curve, while Pleurodira use a horizontal curve.
All reptiles are ectotherms, and turtles are oviparous, laying eggs on land without parental care.

learning_objectives: Describe the evolutionary history of birds
Describe the derived characteristics in birds that facilitate flight

knowledge_units: Birds evolved from maniraptoran theropod dinosaurs about 150 million years ago.
Feathers probably appeared in the common ancestor of both ornithischian and saurischian dinosaur lineages.
Feathers are homologous to reptilian scales and mammalian hair.
Birds rely on feathers, wings, and other body modifications for flight.
Birds are endothermic and homeothermic, maintaining an elevated and constant body temperature to support the high metabolic rate required for active flight.
Contour feathers have interlocking barbules that create a strong, flexible surface for flight, while down feathers trap air for insulation.
Flight feathers are asymmetrical and curved to generate lift, with primary feathers providing thrust and secondary feathers providing lift.
The pectoralis major muscles power the downstroke and the supracoracoideus muscles power the upstroke, both attached to a keeled sternum for flight.
Pneumatized bones are hollow with internal struts to reduce weight, aiding flight.
Birds lack a urinary bladder and excrete uric acid as concentrated urate salts to conserve water and reduce weight.
Birds have a unidirectional airflow respiratory system with air sacs and parabronchi, creating a counter-current exchange for efficient gas exchange.
The furcula (wishbone) is formed by fused clavicles and provides support to the shoulder girdle during flight.
Birds have a single functional left ovary in most species to reduce body mass.
The uropygial gland produces oils for waterproofing and maintaining feather flexibility.
Birds evolved within the clade Dinosauria, specifically from the theropod subgroup of Saurischia.
Numerous similarities between late theropod fossils and birds include hip and wrist bone structure and the presence of a wishbone.
The clade Neornithes includes all living birds and descendants from their most recent common maniraptoran ancestor.
Archaeopteryx is an intermediate fossil with dinosaurian traits like teeth and a bony tail, and avian traits like flight feathers.
The arboreal hypothesis proposes that flight evolved from tree-dwelling ancestors that glided using feathers before developing flapping flight.
The terrestrial hypothesis suggests flight evolved from running ancestors where wings were used for balance and capturing prey.
Feathers and endothermy likely coevolved, with each trait reinforcing the evolution of the other.
Enantiornithes were dominant Cretaceous birds with teeth and a shortened tail, but they did not survive past the Cretaceous period.
Ornithurae, characterized by a short, fused tail or pygostyle, emerged in the Cretaceous and gave rise to modern birds.
After the Cretaceous extinction, Ornithurae underwent a large radiation, becoming the dominant birds in the Cenozoic era.
Modern birds are classified into three major clades: Paleognathae (flightless ratites), Galloanserae (pheasants, ducks), and Neoaves (all other birds).

learning_objectives: Name and describe the distinguishing features of the three main groups of mammals
Describe the likely line of evolutionary descent that produced mammals
List some derived features that may have arisen in response to mammals’ need for constant, high-level metabolism
Identify the major clades of eutherian mammals

knowledge_units: Mammals are vertebrates that possess hair and mammary glands.
Modern mammals belong to three clades: monotremes, marsupials, and eutherians (or placental mammals).
Hair, composed of keratin, is a defining characteristic of mammals and serves functions including insulation, sensory perception, and social signaling.
Mammals are endothermic, and hair helps retain heat generated by metabolic activity.
Mammalian skin contains specialized secretory glands, including sebaceous glands for sebum production, eccrine glands for sweat, apocrine glands for scent, and mammary glands for milk.
The mammalian lower jaw consists of a single bone, the dentary, which articulates with the squamosal bone of the skull.
In mammals, the quadrate and articular bones have moved into the middle ear to become the incus and malleus, respectively.
Mammals possess heterodont dentition with different tooth types and are diphyodonts, having two sets of teeth in their lifetime.
Mammals have a four-chambered heart with a sinoatrial node that regulates heartbeat, and their red blood cells lack nuclei.
Mammalian kidneys have a loop of Henle in the nephron, allowing for the production of concentrated urine.
Mammalian skulls have two occipital condyles, a secondary palate, turbinate bones, and typically seven cervical vertebrae.
Mammals have a muscular diaphragm, movable eyelids, and fleshy external ears (pinnae).
In many mammals, the cerebral cortex is highly convoluted, and eutherian mammals possess a corpus callosum linking the two cerebral hemispheres.
Mammals are synapsids characterized by a single postorbital opening in the skull.
Cynodonts, a group of therapsids, are thought to be the ancestors of mammals.
Endothermy in synapsids likely co-evolved with skeletal changes that improved food processing and ambulation.
Later synapsids evolved cheeks for holding food and heterodont teeth specialized for chewing.
A secondary palate separates chewing from respiration, allowing simultaneous breathing and chewing.
The zygomatic arch in mammals and cynodonts indicates the presence of masseter muscles for chewing.
Mammals evolved from therapsids in the late Triassic period, with the earliest fossils from the early Jurassic.
Morganucodonts were transitional mammals with features of both reptilian and mammalian jaws and were diphyodonts.
There are three major groups of living mammals: monotremes, marsupials, and placental mammals.
Monotremes are unique among mammals because they lay eggs.
Marsupials give birth to very premature young that often continue development in a pouch.
Eutherian mammals possess a complex chorioallantoic placenta for nutrient exchange during development.
Eutherian mammals are classified into two major clades: Atlantogenata and Boreoeutheria.
The Atlantogenata clade includes Afrotheria and Xenarthra.
The Boreoeutheria clade includes Euarchontoglires and Laurasiatheria.

learning_objectives: Describe the derived features that distinguish primates from other animals
Describe the defining features of the major groups of primates
Identify the major hominin precursors to modern humans
Explain why scientists are having difficulty determining the true lines of descent in hominids

knowledge_units: Order Primates includes lemurs, tarsiers, monkeys, apes, and humans.
Non-human primates live primarily in tropical or subtropical regions of South America, Africa, and Asia.
The characteristics and evolution of primates are studied to understand the evolution of our own species.
Primates possess adaptations for climbing trees due to their arboreal heritage.
Primate adaptations include a rotating shoulder joint, separated big toes and thumbs for gripping, and stereoscopic vision for depth perception.
Primates have larger brains than most other mammals, flattened nails instead of claws, typically one offspring per pregnancy, and a trend toward upright posture.
Order Primates is divided into Strepsirrhini (wet-nosed primates) and Haplorhini (dry-nosed primates).
Strepsirrhines tend to be nocturnal, have larger olfactory brain centers, and are generally smaller with smaller brains than anthropoids.
Haplorhines are mostly diurnal and depend more on vision than strepsirrhines.
Strepsirrhines can synthesize vitamin C, while haplorhines must obtain it from their diet.
Proto-primates were the first primate-like mammals, roughly similar to squirrels and tree shrews in size and appearance.
The oldest known primate-like mammals with a relatively robust fossil record date to about 65 million years ago.
The first true primates date to about 55 million years ago and resembled present-day prosimians such as lemurs.
Evolutionary trends in early primates included larger brains and eyes, and smaller muzzles.
Anthropoid monkeys evolved from prosimians during the Oligocene epoch.
New World monkeys (Platyrrhini) have broad noses, while Old World monkeys (Catarrhini) have narrow, downward-pointing noses.
New World monkeys are all arboreal and most have prehensile tails, whereas Old World monkeys include both arboreal and ground-dwelling species and never have prehensile tails.
Apes evolved from catarrhines in Africa approximately 25 million years ago and are generally larger than monkeys, lack a tail, and have larger brains relative to body size.
When walking quadrupedally, monkeys walk on their palms, while apes support the upper body on their knuckles.
The lesser apes include gibbons and siamangs, while the great apes include chimpanzees, bonobos, gorillas, orangutans, and humans.
Chimpanzees and bonobos are the species most closely related to humans, with genetic evidence suggesting their lineages separated 5 to 7 million years ago.
Chimpanzees and bonobos are separated by the Congo River, with bonobos being slighter, having longer legs, and being less aggressive than chimpanzees.
The family Hominidae includes the great apes and humans.
Humans and chimpanzees diverged from a common hominoid ancestor approximately six million years ago.
The term hominin refers to species that evolved after the split from the common ancestor with chimpanzees, making them more closely related to humans.
Key features that differentiate humans from other hominoids include bipedalism, increased brain size, and a fully opposable thumb.
Determining the true lines of descent in hominins is difficult because multiple species often existed at the same time, and many are not ancestral to modern humans.
Australopithecus is a genus of hominin that evolved in eastern Africa about four million years ago and is thought to be ancestral to the genus Homo.
Australopithecus exhibited bipedalism but also had features more similar to great apes, such as greater sexual dimorphism and a smaller relative brain size.
The genus Paranthropus had a muscular build and large grinding teeth adapted for a coarse vegetarian diet, and it is not thought to be ancestral to modern humans.
The genus Homo first appeared between 2.5 and 3 million years ago, with Homo habilis showing features more similar to modern humans than australopiths.
Homo erectus was the first hominin to migrate out of Africa and had features more similar to modern humans, including a larger brain and reduced sexual dimorphism.
Archaic Homo sapiens species evolved from Homo erectus and had brain sizes similar to modern humans but differed in skull features.
Molecular evidence from mitochondrial DNA and the Y chromosome suggests that anatomically modern humans arose in Africa and migrated out of Africa about 100,000 years ago.

learning_objectives: Explain the essential roles plants play in human survival and resource provision.
Distinguish between gymnosperms and angiosperms based on seed structure and examples.
Describe the common structural components shared by all plant species.
Identify the key environmental factors that influence plant growth and response.

knowledge_units: Plants are essential for human survival because they produce oxygen through photosynthesis and provide resources such as food, shelter, medicines, and industrial products.
Seed plants are divided into gymnosperms, which have seeds not enclosed by a fleshy fruit, and angiosperms, which are flowering plants.
All plant species share a common structural body consisting of stems, roots, and leaves.
All plant species transport water, minerals, and sugars through their bodies in a similar manner.
All plant species respond to environmental factors such as light, gravity, competition, temperature, and predation.

learning_objectives: Describe the shoot organ system and the root organ system
Distinguish between meristematic tissue and permanent tissue
Identify and describe the three regions where plant growth occurs
Summarize the roles of dermal tissue, vascular tissue, and ground tissue
Compare simple plant tissue with complex plant tissue

knowledge_units: Plants use energy from sunlight to form sugars during photosynthesis.
Plant cells have cell walls, plastids, and a large central vacuole, which are structures not found in animal cells.
Vascular plants have two distinct organ systems: a shoot system and a root system.
The shoot system consists of vegetative parts like leaves and stems, and reproductive parts like flowers and fruits.
The shoot system generally grows above ground to absorb light for photosynthesis.
The root system is usually underground, where it supports the plant and absorbs water and minerals.
Plant tissue systems are classified into meristematic tissue and permanent tissue.
Meristematic tissue cells are undifferentiated or incompletely differentiated and continue to divide, contributing to plant growth.
Permanent tissue consists of plant cells that are no longer actively dividing.
Apical meristems enable a plant to extend in length, lateral meristems facilitate growth in thickness, and intercalary meristems allow monocot leaf blades to increase in length from the base.
Meristematic cells differentiate into three main types of permanent tissue: dermal, vascular, and ground tissue.
Dermal tissue covers and protects the plant, vascular tissue transports water, minerals, and sugars, and ground tissue functions in photosynthesis, support, and storage.
Dermal tissue is a simple tissue, while vascular tissue is a complex tissue composed of xylem and phloem.

Xylem transports water and nutrients from the roots and includes vessel elements, tracheids, and xylem parenchyma, while phloem transports organic compounds and consists of sieve cells, companion cells, phloem parenchyma, and phloem fibers.

learning_objectives: Describe the main function and basic structure of stems
Compare and contrast the roles of dermal tissue, vascular tissue, and ground tissue
Distinguish between primary growth and secondary growth in stems
Summarize the origin of annual rings
List and describe examples of modified stems

knowledge_units: Stems provide support to the plant and help transport water, minerals, and sugars between roots and leaves.
Stems are characterized by the presence of nodes and internodes.
Nodes are points of attachment for leaves, aerial roots, and flowers.
An axillary bud is found in the axil between a leaf and the stem and can give rise to a branch or flower.
The apical meristem is located within the apical bud at the shoot apex.
The stem is primarily made up of simple tissues formed from parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma cells.
Parenchyma cells are responsible for metabolic functions such as photosynthesis and help repair wounds.
Collenchyma cells are elongated cells with unevenly thickened walls that provide structural support to the stem and leaves.
Sclerenchyma cells provide support and have secondary cell walls thickened with lignin; they include fibers and sclereids.
The stem has three tissue systems: dermal, vascular, and ground tissue, each with specific functions.
Dermal tissue of the stem consists primarily of epidermis, which protects underlying tissue, and in woody plants includes cork cells known as bark.
Vascular tissue in the stem is arranged in vascular bundles containing xylem and phloem.
Xylem tissue conducts water and includes tracheids and vessel elements, which are dead at maturity.
Phloem tissue transports organic substances like sugars and includes sieve-tube cells and companion cells.
Ground tissue is mostly parenchyma cells and includes pith and cortex regions in the stem.
Primary growth is the increase in length of stems and roots resulting from cell division in the apical meristem.
Secondary growth is the increase in stem thickness caused by cell division in lateral meristems.
Herbaceous plants mostly undergo primary growth with little secondary growth, while woody plants exhibit noticeable secondary growth.
Indeterminate growth allows stems and roots to continue growing throughout a plant's life, unlike determinate growth in leaves and flowers.
Apical dominance is the influence of the apical bud that suppresses the growth of axillary buds, shaping plant form.
The vascular cambium produces secondary xylem to the inside and secondary phloem to the outside, contributing to stem thickening.
The cork cambium produces cork cells containing suberin, which forms a protective bark that reduces water loss.
The periderm, consisting of cork cambium, cork cells, and phelloderm, replaces the epidermis in mature plants.
Annual rings form from alternating early wood (large-diameter cells) and late wood (thick-walled cells) produced by the vascular cambium in different seasons.
A rhizome is a modified stem that grows horizontally underground with nodes and internodes.
Corms are rounded, fleshy modified stems that store food to help plants survive winter.
Stolons are stems that run near the ground surface and can give rise to new plants at nodes.
Runners are a type of stolon that runs above ground and produces new clone plants at nodes.
Tubers are modified stems that store starch and arise as swollen ends of stolons.
Bulbs are modified stems that function as underground storage units, appearing as enlarged fleshy leaves.
Tendrils are slender, twining modified stems that enable plants to climb for support.
Thorns are modified branches that appear as sharp outgrowths to protect the plant.

learning_objectives: Identify the two types of root systems
Describe the three zones of the root tip and summarize the role of each zone in root growth
Describe the structure of the root
List and describe examples of modified roots

knowledge_units: Roots anchor the plant to the soil, absorb and transport water and minerals, and store photosynthesis products.
Some roots are modified to absorb moisture and exchange gases.
Adventitious roots emerge above the ground from the shoot.
Dicots have a tap root system, which consists of a main root that grows vertically and gives rise to lateral roots.
Monocots have a fibrous root system, which forms a dense network of roots near the soil surface.
A tap root system penetrates deep into the soil, while a fibrous root system helps prevent soil erosion.
Plants in dry areas often have deep root systems, whereas plants in areas with abundant water tend to have shallower root systems.
The root tip is protected by the root cap, which is continuously replaced as it gets damaged during growth.
The root tip can be divided into three zones: a zone of cell division, a zone of elongation, and a zone of maturation and differentiation.
The zone of cell division contains the actively dividing cells of the root meristem.
The zone of elongation is where newly formed cells increase in length, lengthening the root.
The zone of maturation is where root cells differentiate into special cell types, beginning at the first root hair.
The epidermis is the outer layer of cells that provides protection and aids in absorption.
Root hairs are extensions of epidermal cells that increase surface area for water and mineral absorption.
The endodermis, exclusive to roots, separates the stele from the ground tissue and acts as a checkpoint for materials entering the vascular system.
The Casparian strip, a waxy region on endodermal cell walls, forces water and solutes to cross plasma membranes to control entry into the vascular system.
The pericycle, the outermost cell layer of the vascular tissue, can give rise to lateral roots.
In dicot roots, xylem and phloem are arranged alternately in an X shape, while in monocot roots, vascular tissue is arranged in a ring around the pith.
Some roots are modified to be bulbous for starch storage.
Aerial roots and prop roots are aboveground modifications that provide additional support to anchor the plant.
Tap roots, such as those in carrots, turnips, and beets, are modified for food storage.
Epiphytic roots, like those of orchids, develop spongy tissue to absorb moisture and enable growth on other plants.
In the banyan tree, aerial roots develop from branches and reach the ground to provide additional support.
In screwpine, aboveground prop roots develop from nodes to provide additional support in sandy tropical soils.

learning_objectives: Identify the parts of a typical leaf
Describe the internal structure and function of a leaf
Compare and contrast simple leaves and compound leaves
List and describe examples of modified leaves

knowledge_units: Leaves are the main sites for photosynthesis, the process by which plants synthesize food.
The thickness, shape, and size of leaves are adapted to the environment to maximize a plant's chances of survival in a particular habitat.
A typical leaf has a leaf blade called the lamina, which is the widest part of the leaf.
Leaves may be attached to the stem by a petiole, or they may be sessile if they lack a petiole.
Stipules are small green appendages usually found at the base of the petiole.
The midrib runs the length of the leaf and branches into veins of vascular tissue.
The arrangement of veins in a leaf is called the venation pattern.
Monocots have parallel venation, while dicots have reticulate venation.
Phyllotaxy refers to the arrangement of leaves on a stem.
Leaf arrangement is classified as alternate, spiral, opposite, or whorled based on the number and placement of leaves at each node.
In an alternate arrangement, leaves are positioned on alternating sides of the stem in a flat plane.
In a spiral arrangement, leaves are arrayed in a spiral pattern along the stem.
In an opposite arrangement, two leaves arise at the same node and connect opposite each other along the branch.
In a whorled arrangement, three or more leaves are connected at a single node.
A simple leaf has a blade that is either undivided or has lobes that do not reach the midrib.
A compound leaf has a blade that is completely divided into individual leaflets attached to a central rachis.
Palmately compound leaves have leaflets radiating from a single point, resembling the palm of a hand.
Pinnately compound leaves have leaflets arranged along a central midrib, resembling a feather.
The epidermis is the outermost layer of the leaf on both sides, helping regulate gas exchange through stomata.
Stomata are openings in the epidermis surrounded by two guard cells that regulate their opening and closing.
The cuticle is a waxy layer covering the leaf surface that reduces water loss from transpiration.
Trichomes are small hairs on the leaf surface that can deter herbivory and reduce transpiration.
The mesophyll is the layer below the epidermis in dicot leaves, containing palisade parenchyma and spongy parenchyma.
Palisade parenchyma consists of tightly packed, column-shaped cells, while spongy parenchyma has loosely arranged cells with air spaces for gas exchange.
Vascular bundles in the leaf contain xylem for transporting water and minerals and phloem for transporting photosynthetic products.
Coniferous plants in cold environments have needle-like leaves with sunken stomata and reduced surface area to minimize water loss.
Cacti in hot climates have leaves reduced to spines, which along with succulent stems, help conserve water.
Aquatic plants often have wide, floating lamina and a thick waxy cuticle to repel water.
In light-scarce tropical rainforests, many plants have exceptionally broad leaves to maximize sunlight capture.
Epiphytes, such as bromeliads and orchids, grow on other plants to access sunlight and have specialized tissues for water capture and storage.
Carnivorous plants like the Venus flytrap have modified leaves to capture insects, providing a supplementary nitrogen source in nutrient-poor environments.
Swamp plants like mangroves and cypress trees have pneumatophores, which are upward-growing roots specialized for gas exchange in oxygen-poor soils.
Aquatic plants like wild rice have aerenchyma, air-filled tissue in roots that facilitates oxygen diffusion to submerged root tips.

learning_objectives: Compare and contrast sexual and asexual reproduction in plants.
Explain the role of pollinating agents in plant sexual reproduction.
Describe how flowers attract pollinators through their characteristics.
Identify different methods of pollination, including wind, water, and self-pollination.

knowledge_units: Plants have evolved both sexual and asexual reproductive strategies, unlike animals which rely almost exclusively on sexual reproduction.
Plant sexual reproduction usually depends on pollinating agents, while asexual reproduction is independent of these agents.
Flowers attract pollinators through characteristics such as bright colors, fragrances, and interesting shapes and sizes.
Methods of plant pollination include wind, water, and self-pollination.

learning_objectives: Describe the two stages of a plant’s lifecycle
Compare and contrast male and female gametophytes and explain how they form in angiosperms
Describe the reproductive structures of a plant
Describe the components of a complete flower
Describe the development of microsporangium and megasporangium in gymnosperms

knowledge_units: Plants have two distinct lifecycle stages: the haploid gametophyte and the diploid sporophyte.
The gametophyte produces male and female gametes by mitosis, and their fusion forms a diploid zygote that develops into the sporophyte.
The diploid sporophyte produces spores by meiosis, which divide by mitosis to form the haploid gametophyte.
In higher plants, the sporophyte stage dominates the lifecycle, with the gametophyte borne on the sporophyte.
During the reproductive phase, plants produce flowers, which are borne on a receptacle and are used for taxonomic classification.
The lifecycle of angiosperms involves alternation of generations between a haploid gametophyte and a diploid sporophyte.
A complete flower has four whorls: calyx, corolla, androecium, and gynoecium.
The calyx consists of sepals that protect the unopened bud, and the corolla consists of petals.
The androecium is the male reproductive whorl containing stamens with anthers that hold microsporangia.
The gynoecium is the female reproductive whorl composed of carpels, each with a stigma, style, and ovary.
A flower is complete if all four whorls are present and incomplete if any are missing.
Perfect flowers contain both androecium and gynoecium, while staminate flowers have only androecium and carpellate flowers have only gynoecium.
Monoecious species bear male and female flowers on the same plant, whereas dioecious species bear them on separate plants.
The male gametophyte develops in the microsporangium, where microspore mother cells undergo meiosis to produce microspores that become pollen grains.
Mature pollen grains contain a generative cell and a pollen tube cell; the generative cell divides to form two sperm cells during pollen tube growth.
The female gametophyte develops through megasporogenesis, where a megasporangium cell undergoes meiosis to produce one surviving megaspore.
The surviving megaspore undergoes mitosis to form an eight-nucleate, seven-cell embryo sac, which includes the egg cell, synergids, antipodal cells, and a central cell with polar nuclei.
The integuments protect the megasporangium and later become the seed coat, leaving a micropyle for pollen tube entry.
The lifecycle of a gymnosperm is characterized by alternation of generations, with the sporophyte being the green leafy plant and the cones containing the gametophytes.
In gymnosperms, male cones produce microspores that develop into pollen, which is the male gametophyte.
Female cones produce megaspores that develop into the multicellular female gametophyte within an archegonium.
Pollen is released from male cones and carried by wind to female cones, making self-pollination difficult due to the spatial separation of male and female cones.
Upon landing on a female cone, the pollen tube grows to deliver sperm nuclei to the female gametophyte for fertilization.
Fertilization in gymnosperms results in a diploid zygote that develops into an embryo within a seed, which lacks a fruit covering.
In angiosperms, the female gametophyte is enclosed within the ovule inside the ovary, whereas in gymnosperms, it is exposed on the bracts of the female cone.
Double fertilization occurs in angiosperms but is absent in gymnosperms.
In gymnosperms, male and female gametophytes are on separate male and female cones, while in angiosperms, they are part of the flower.
Wind pollination is important in gymnosperms, whereas animal pollination is more common in angiosperms.

learning_objectives: Describe what must occur for plant fertilization
Explain cross-pollination and the ways in which it takes place
Describe the process that leads to the development of a seed
Define double fertilization

knowledge_units: Pollination in angiosperms is the transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma of the same or another flower.
Pollination in gymnosperms involves pollen transfer from the male cone to the female cone.
Upon transfer, pollen germinates to form a pollen tube and sperm for fertilizing the egg.
Self-pollination occurs when pollen is deposited on the stigma of the same flower or another flower on the same plant.
Cross-pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther of one flower to the stigma of another flower on a different individual of the same species.
Self-pollination leads to less genetic diversity because genetic material from the same plant is used.
Cross-pollination leads to greater genetic diversity because the microgametophyte and megagametophyte are derived from different plants.

Plants have developed mechanisms to avoid self-pollination, such as different maturation times of pollen and ovary, physical features like heterostyly, and separate male and female flowers on the same or different plants.
Most pollinators are biotic agents like insects, bats, and birds, while some plants are pollinated by abiotic agents like wind and water.
Bees are important pollinators that are attracted to flowers with blue, yellow, or other colors, strong scents, tubular shapes, and nectar guides.
Nectar guides are regions on flower petals visible to bees that help guide them to the flower's center for efficient pollination.
Pollen sticks to the fuzzy hair of bees and is transferred to other flowers during visits, facilitating cross-pollination.
Many flies are attracted to flowers with decaying smells, dull colors, and nectar, such as those on corpse flowers or voodoo lilies.
Butterflies pollinate brightly colored, fragrant flowers that are open during the day and have nectar guides.
Moths pollinate pale or white, flat flowers during late afternoon and night, such as the yucca plant.
The yucca moth and yucca plant have a symbiotic relationship where the moth deposits pollen on the stigma for fertilization and lays eggs in the ovary, with larvae feeding on developing seeds.
Bats pollinate nocturnal flowers in tropical and desert regions, such as agave, guava, and morning glory.
Flowers pollinated by bats are typically large, white or pale-colored, and have a strong fragrance to attract bats at night.
Bat-pollinated flowers produce large amounts of nectar and have wide mouths to accommodate the bat's head.
As bats feed on nectar, pollen adheres to their faces and heads and is transferred between flowers, facilitating cross-pollination.
Birds such as hummingbirds and sun birds act as pollinators for plants like orchids and wildflowers.
Flowers pollinated by birds are typically sturdy, brightly colored, odorless, and open during the day.
Bird-pollinated flowers often have a curved, tubular shape to allow access for the bird's beak.
Pollen is deposited on a bird's head and neck as it seeks nectar and is transferred to subsequent flowers it visits.
Wind-pollinated plants, such as conifers and many angiosperms like grasses and maples, are pollinated by wind.
Flowers adapted to wind pollination are typically small, green, lack petals, produce large amounts of pollen, and do not produce nectar or scent.
In wind-pollinated species, microsporangia hang out of the flower to release lightweight pollen that is carried by the wind.
Wind-pollinated flowers often emerge early in spring before leaves to avoid blocking wind movement.
Pollen from wind-pollinated plants is deposited on the exposed feathery stigma of the flower.
Some plants, such as Australian sea grass and pond weeds, are pollinated by water, where pollen floats on water and is deposited inside a flower upon contact.
Some orchid species use food deception by offering bright colors and scents without nectar to attract pollinators like bumblebees, which then transfer pollen.
Other orchids use sexual deception by emitting compounds that mimic insect pheromones to attract pollinators, such as male wasps, which transfer pollen during attempted mating.
Double fertilization in angiosperms involves one sperm fertilizing the egg to form a diploid zygote and the other sperm fusing with two polar nuclei to form a triploid endosperm.
The pollen tube grows through the style to reach the ovule, guided by chemicals from synergids in the embryo sac.
The generative cell in pollen divides to form two sperm cells.
After fertilization, the fertilized ovule develops into a seed and the ovary tissues become the fruit.
The zygote divides into a terminal cell that forms the embryo and a basal cell that forms the suspensor for nutrient transport.
In dicot seeds, the embryo develops a heart shape due to two rudimentary cotyledons.
In non-endospermic dicots, endosperm food reserves are moved into the cotyledons.
A typical seed contains a seed coat, cotyledons, endosperm, and an embryo.
In monocot seeds, the single cotyledon (scutellum) absorbs food reserves from the endosperm during germination.
In endospermic dicots, the two cotyledons act as absorptive organs to take up food reserves from the endosperm.
The seed coat is formed from the integuments of the ovule sac.
The embryonic axis consists of the plumule, radicle, and hypocotyl.
In dicot seeds, the hypocotyl extends above ground to form the stem, while in monocots it does not.
Upon germination in dicots, the plumule hook protects the plumule as it pushes through soil until exposure to light.
In monocot seeds, the coleorhiza protects the primary root and the coleoptile protects the primary shoot during germination.
Seed dormancy is a period of inactivity that helps seeds survive unfavorable conditions.
Seed germination occurs under favorable conditions such as moisture, light, cold, or chemical treatments.
Scarification softens the seed coat to promote germination in some seeds.
Small-seeded species often require light as a germination cue to ensure germination near the soil surface.
After fertilization, the ovary of the flower usually develops into the fruit.
Botanically, a fruit is defined as a ripened ovary.
Fruits enclose the seeds and developing embryo, providing them with protection.
Fruits may be classified as simple, aggregate, multiple, or accessory based on their origin.
A simple fruit develops from a single carpel or fused carpels of a single ovary.
An aggregate fruit develops from more than one carpel in the same flower.
A multiple fruit develops from an inflorescence or cluster of flowers.
Accessory fruits are not derived from the ovary but from another part of the flower, such as the receptacle.
Fruits generally consist of three layers: exocarp, mesocarp, and endocarp, collectively called the pericarp.
Fruits can be classified as dry or fleshy, and as dehiscent or indehiscent based on seed release.
The primary function of a fruit is to facilitate seed dispersal.
Seeds must be dispersed away from the parent plant to reduce competition and find favorable germination conditions.
Fruits can be dispersed by wind, water, animals, or through self-dispersal mechanisms.
Wind-dispersed fruits are often lightweight with structures like wings or parachutes to aid transport.
Water-dispersed fruits are buoyant, allowing them to float to new locations.
Animals disperse seeds by eating fruits and excreting the seeds, or by carrying fruits attached to their bodies.
Seed dormancy allows plants to disperse their progeny through time, enabling germination when conditions are favorable.

learning_objectives: Compare the mechanisms and methods of natural and artificial asexual reproduction
Describe the advantages and disadvantages of natural and artificial asexual reproduction
Discuss plant life spans

knowledge_units: Asexual reproduction in plants does not require the investment of producing flowers, attracting pollinators, or seed dispersal.
Asexual reproduction produces genetically identical offspring because no mixing of male and female gametes occurs.
Plants produced asexually survive well under stable environmental conditions due to carrying identical genes to their parents.
Many types of roots, such as corms, bulbs, tubers, rhizomes, and stolons, can exhibit asexual reproduction.
Apomixis is a method of asexual reproduction where seeds are produced without fertilization from diploid ovule or ovary tissue.
An advantage of asexual reproduction is that the resulting plant reaches maturity faster and is sturdier than a seedling.
Asexual reproduction can occur through natural or artificial means assisted by humans.
Natural asexual reproduction in plants includes growth from buds on stems, as seen in ginger, onion, gladioli, and dahlia.
Some plants, like sweet potato, produce new plants from adventitious roots or runners.
In plants such as Bryophyllum and kalanchoe, leaf margin buds can detach and grow into independent plants.
Certain plants can be propagated through cuttings alone as a natural method of asexual reproduction.
Grafting involves attaching a scion from a desirable plant onto a rooted stock plant to produce a new plant with combined traits.
In grafting, the vascular systems of the scion and stock fuse to form a graft, allowing the scion to produce shoots, flowers, and fruits.
Cutting is a method where a stem portion with nodes and internodes is placed in moist soil or water to develop roots and grow into a new plant.
Layering involves bending a stem attached to the parent plant, covering it with soil to encourage root formation before transplanting.
Micropropagation uses plant tissue culture to produce many plants from a single plant under sterile laboratory conditions.
Micropropagation allows for the rapid propagation of rare, endangered, or economically important plant species.
In micropropagation, plant material is sterilized and cultured on a nutrient medium to form callus, from which plantlets develop.
Plant life span is the length of time from the beginning of development to the death of a plant.
Annual plants complete their life cycle in one season, biennials in two seasons, and perennials in two years or more.
Monocarpic plants flower only once in their lifetime and then die, while polycarpic plants flower multiple times.
Genetics and environmental conditions, such as disease, drought, and competition, influence plant life span.
Senescence is the aging process in plants, marked by biochemical changes like chloroplast breakdown and leaf yellowing.
Nutrient recycling involves the breakdown and salvage of components from dying plant parts for use in other tissues.
Hormones like cytokinins and ethylene can delay senescence, while abscisic acid can cause premature senescence.

chapter_title: The Animal Body: Basic Form and Function

learning_objectives: Explain how the arctic fox illustrates the relationship between animal form and function, and describe how primary tissues contribute to complex organs and organ systems in maintaining homeostasis.

knowledge_units: The arctic fox illustrates the relationship between animal form and function.
Primary tissues make up more complex organs and organ systems.
Homeostasis allows an animal to maintain a balance between its internal and external environments.

learning_objectives: Describe the various types of body plans that occur in animals
Describe limits on animal size and shape
Relate bioenergetics to body size, levels of activity, and the environment

knowledge_units: Animals have distinct body plans that limit their size and shape.
Animal bodies are designed to interact with their specific environments.
Studying an organism's environment provides information about its anatomy and physiology.
Animal body plans are classified as asymmetrical, radial, or bilateral based on symmetry.
