Radial symmetry is characterized by an up-and-down orientation where any plane cut along the longitudinal axis produces equal halves, and it is common in aquatic, attached organisms.

Bilateral symmetry is characterized by a plane cut from front to back separating the animal into definite right and left sides, and it enables a high level of mobility in both land-based and aquatic animals.
Animals with bilateral symmetry in water often have a fusiform shape to reduce drag and enable high-speed swimming.
Aquatic organisms are constrained in shape by water's higher viscosity and drag forces, while land-dwelling organisms are constrained mainly by gravity.
An exoskeleton provides protection, prevents water loss, and serves as muscle attachment sites for arthropods.
Exoskeletons are made of tough polymers like chitin and are often biomineralized with materials such as calcium carbonate.
Arthropods must molt their exoskeleton to grow, which limits continuous growth and can affect maturation timing.
Doubling an animal's body size increases its weight by a factor of eight, requiring thicker exoskeletons that limit maximum size.
Endoskeletons are more efficient than exoskeletons because muscles attach externally, making it easier to compensate for increased mass.
An animal's size with an endoskeleton is determined by the skeletal and muscle mass needed for support and movement.
Diffusion is effective over a specific distance and limits the size that an individual cell can attain.
The surface-to-volume ratio decreases as a cell gets bigger, making diffusion less efficient.
Multicellularity allows for specialization of cells and organ systems to overcome diffusion limits.
Surface-to-volume ratio applies to relationships like muscle mass and skeletal support or heat dissipation.
Animals obtain energy from food and convert nutrients to ATP for cellular use.
Endotherms maintain constant body temperature using insulation like fur, fat, or feathers.
Ectotherms depend on the environment for body heat due to lack of insulation.
Metabolic rate is the energy expended over time, measured in calories or kilocalories.
Basal metabolic rate (BMR) measures endotherms at rest, while standard metabolic rate (SMR) measures ectotherms.
Smaller endothermic animals have higher BMR per body weight due to greater surface area-to-mass ratio.
Higher activity levels increase an animal's BMR or SMR.
Torpor is a state of decreased activity and metabolism that helps animals survive adverse conditions like extreme temperatures or food scarcity.
A sagittal plane divides the body into right and left portions.
A frontal plane separates the front from the back of the body.
A transverse plane divides the animal into upper and lower portions.
The dorsal cavity contains the cranial and vertebral cavities.
The ventral cavity contains the thoracic and abdominopelvic cavities.

learning_objectives: Describe epithelial tissues
Discuss the different types of connective tissues in animals
Describe three types of muscle tissues
Describe nervous tissue

knowledge_units: Multicellular, complex animals have four primary tissue types: epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous.
Tissues are groups of similar cells that carry out related functions.
Tissues combine to form organs, which have specific, specialized functions within the body.
Organs are organized into organ systems to perform functions, and organ systems together create an entire organism.
Epithelial tissues cover the outside of organs and line the lumens of organs in single or multiple layers of cells.
Epithelia are classified by cell shape and number of cell layers into simple (single layer) and stratified (multiple layers) types.
Squamous epithelial cells are flat and round, facilitating diffusion in simple arrangements and providing protection in stratified arrangements.
Cuboidal epithelial cells are cube-shaped and commonly found in glandular tissues where they secrete materials.
Columnar epithelial cells are taller than they are wide and are specialized for absorption in the digestive tract.
Pseudostratified columnar epithelia appear stratified but are simple, with cilia that move mucus in the respiratory tract.
Transitional epithelia in the urinary system can stretch and change shape to accommodate volume changes in the bladder.
Connective tissues consist of living cells and a nonliving matrix made of ground substance.
The principal cell of connective tissues is the fibroblast, which produces fibers.
The matrix in connective tissues determines its density, with higher cell or fiber concentration resulting in a less dense matrix.
Connective tissues contain organic protein fibers: collagen for strength, elastic for flexibility, and reticular for support.
Loose connective tissue contains a sampling of all connective tissue components and is found around blood vessels and organs.
Fibrous connective tissues contain large amounts of collagen fibers and can be arranged irregularly or regularly.
Cartilage is a connective tissue with a large amount of matrix and cells called chondrocytes in lacunae.
Hyaline cartilage has few fibers and is found in shark skeletons, fetal bones, and at the ends of long bones.
Elastic cartilage has many elastic fibers and is found in ears and the larynx.
Fibrocartilage has many collagen fibers and comprises intervertebral discs.
Bone is a connective tissue with an organic matrix for flexibility and an inorganic matrix of mineral salts for hardness.
Bone contains osteoblasts for formation, osteocytes for maintenance, and osteoclasts for breakdown.
Compact bone is organized into osteons with Haversian canals, while spongy bone consists of trabeculae.
Adipose tissue is a connective tissue made of adipocytes that store fat for energy, insulation, and cushioning.
Blood is a connective tissue with a fluid matrix called plasma and contains red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.
Erythrocytes are red blood cells that transport oxygen to tissues.
Leukocytes are white blood cells that include lymphocytes for immune response, neutrophils for phagocytosis, and monocytes that become macrophages.
Platelets are cytoplasmic fragments that participate in blood coagulation.
There are three types of muscle in animal bodies: smooth, skeletal, and cardiac.
Smooth muscle lacks striations, has a single central nucleus, and is under involuntary control.
Skeletal muscle has striations, multiple peripheral nuclei, and is under voluntary control.
Cardiac muscle has striations, a single central nucleus, and is under involuntary control.
Cardiac muscle cells are connected by intercalated discs that facilitate electrical impulse transmission.
Nervous tissues are composed of cells specialized to receive and transmit electrical impulses between specific body locations.
The neuron is the main cell of the nervous system, featuring a cell body, dendrites for receiving input, and a single axon for transmitting impulses.
Glial cells support neurons by regulating their chemical environment, insulating axons, and providing nutritional and waste management.
A nerve is a structure consisting of neurons and glial cells.

learning_objectives: Define homeostasis
Describe the factors affecting homeostasis
Discuss positive and negative feedback mechanisms used in homeostasis
Describe thermoregulation of endothermic and ectothermic animals

knowledge_units: Homeostasis is the process by which animal organs and organ systems constantly adjust to internal and external changes to maintain a dynamic equilibrium.
Homeostasis is dynamic because it constantly adjusts to changes encountered by the body's systems.
Homeostasis is an equilibrium because body functions are kept within specific ranges.
Homeostasis maintains equilibrium around a set point despite normal fluctuations.
A stimulus is a change in the internal or external environment detected by a receptor.
The response to a stimulus adjusts a deviation parameter back toward the set point.
Examples of homeostatic adjustments include cooling the body when too warm and lowering blood glucose after a meal.
Homeostasis is maintained by negative feedback loops that reverse a change in the environment.
Positive feedback loops maintain or accelerate a stimulus, pushing the organism further from homeostasis.
Homeostasis is controlled by the nervous and endocrine systems.
Negative feedback loops are the predominant mechanism used in homeostasis.
A set point in a feedback loop can be adjusted, and the loop works to maintain the new setting.
Acclimatization involves changes in body systems to maintain a set point in response to environmental changes.
Body temperature affects enzyme activity, with activity doubling for every ten degree Celsius rise until denaturation occurs.
Enzyme activity decreases by half for every ten degree Celsius drop in temperature, with some exceptions like certain fish that can withstand freezing.
Animals that do not control their body temperature are ectotherms.
Animals that maintain a constant body temperature in the face of environmental changes are called homeotherms.
Endotherms are animals that rely on internal sources for body temperature.
Heat can be exchanged between an animal and its environment through radiation, evaporation, convection, and conduction.
Endothermic animals use insulation such as fur, fat, or feathers to conserve heat.
Vasodilation increases blood flow to the body's surface to facilitate heat loss, while vasoconstriction reduces peripheral blood flow to conserve heat.
Countercurrent heat exchange transfers heat from arteries to veins to prevent cooling of internal organs.
Ectothermic animals regulate body temperature through behavioral adaptations such as seeking cooler or warmer environments.
Shivering and brown fat generate metabolic heat to help maintain body temperature in endotherms.
The nervous system is important to thermoregulation.
The processes of homeostasis and temperature control are centered in the hypothalamus of the advanced animal brain.

chapter_title: Animal Nutrition and the Digestive System

learning_objectives: Explain why animals must digest and absorb food to obtain necessary nutrients.
Describe the macromolecules in food and the simple molecules required for cellular function.
Summarize the multistep process of digestion and absorption.
Discuss the health consequences of imbalances between food intake, energy storage, and energy expenditure.

knowledge_units: Animals must convert consumed macromolecules into simple molecules required for cellular functions through digestion and absorption.
The biological molecules necessary for animal function are amino acids, lipid molecules, nucleotides, and simple sugars.
Imbalances between food intake, energy storage, and energy expenditure can lead to health consequences such as obesity and increased risk of type-2 diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

learning_objectives: Explain the processes of digestion and absorption
Compare and contrast different types of digestive systems
Explain the specialized functions of the organs involved in processing food in the body
Describe the ways in which organs work together to digest food and absorb nutrients

knowledge_units: Animals obtain nutrition by consuming other organisms and can be classified as herbivores, carnivores, or omnivores based on their diet.
Nutrients and macromolecules in food are not immediately accessible to cells and require processes within the animal body to be modified for cellular function.
As animals evolved in complexity, their digestive systems also evolved to accommodate various dietary needs.
Herbivores are animals whose primary food source is plant-based and have evolved digestive systems capable of handling large amounts of plant material.
Carnivores are animals that eat other animals, with obligate carnivores relying entirely on animal flesh and facultative carnivores also eating non-animal food.
Omnivores are animals that eat both plant- and animal-derived food.
A gastrovascular cavity is a digestive system with only one opening that serves as both mouth and anus.
Organisms such as flatworms, comb jellies, and cnidarians use a gastrovascular cavity for digestion.
An alimentary canal is a more advanced digestive system with a separate mouth and anus.
In an alimentary canal, food passes through specialized organs like the crop and gizzard for storage and digestion before nutrient absorption in the intestine.
Vertebrates have evolved complex digestive systems to adapt to their dietary needs.
A monogastric digestive system consists of a single stomach chamber.
In monogastric digestion, teeth physically break down food and saliva enzymes begin chemical digestion.
Peristalsis moves food through the esophagus to the stomach.
The stomach's acidic environment (pH 1.5-2.5) enhances enzyme activity for digestion.
Enzymes from the liver, small intestine, and pancreas continue digestion in the small intestine.
Nutrients are absorbed into the bloodstream across the epithelial cells of the small intestine.
The large intestine absorbs water and compacts waste into feces.
Birds have a digestive system adapted to process unmasticated food due to lack of teeth.
The avian stomach has two chambers: the proventriculus for gastric juices and the gizzard for mechanical grinding.
Birds have high metabolic rates to efficiently process food and maintain low body weight for flight.
Ruminants are herbivores with multi-chambered stomachs to digest large amounts of cellulose.
The four compartments of the ruminant stomach are the rumen, reticulum, omasum, and abomasum.
Microbes in the ruminant stomach chambers break down cellulose and ferment ingested food.
The abomasum is the true stomach in ruminants where gastric juices are secreted.
Pseudo-ruminants have a three-chamber stomach and a large cecum for fermenting plant material.
Microorganisms in the digestive system of pseudo-ruminants break down cellulose.
The oral cavity is the point of entry for food into the digestive system, where mastication breaks food into smaller particles.
Saliva contains salivary amylase, which begins the conversion of starches into maltose, and lingual lipase, which begins the breakdown of fats.
The epiglottis closes the glottis during swallowing to prevent food from entering the trachea.
Peristalsis in the esophagus is an involuntary, unidirectional wave that pushes food toward the stomach.
The gastro-esophageal sphincter opens to allow food into the stomach and closes to prevent stomach contents from moving back into the esophagus.
The stomach secretes gastric juices in a highly acidic environment (pH 1.5–2.5) to chemically break down food.
The small intestine completes the digestion of proteins, fats, and carbohydrates and absorbs nutrients into the bloodstream.
Villi and microvilli in the small intestine increase surface area to enhance nutrient absorption efficiency.
The large intestine reabsorbs water and mineral salts from undigested material and stores waste.
The rectum stores feces until defecation, which is controlled by involuntary and voluntary sphincters.
Accessory organs such as the liver, pancreas, and gallbladder add secretions that aid in digestion.
The liver produces bile for fat digestion and detoxifies blood, while the pancreas secretes bicarbonate to neutralize acidic chyme and enzymes for protein and carbohydrate digestion.

learning_objectives: Explain why an animal’s diet should be balanced and meet the needs of the body
Define the primary components of food
Describe the essential nutrients required for cellular function that cannot be synthesized by the animal body
Explain how energy is produced through diet and digestion
Describe how excess carbohydrates and energy are stored in the body

knowledge_units: The animal diet provides materials for biosynthesis, including growth, maintenance, and reproduction.
The diet is the source of materials for ATP production in cells.
A balanced diet must provide the minerals and vitamins required for cellular function.
An animal's diet should be well balanced and provide nutrients required for bodily function.
An animal's diet should provide minerals and vitamins required for maintaining structure and regulation necessary for good health and reproductive capability.

learning_objectives: Describe the process of digestion
Detail the steps involved in digestion and absorption
Define elimination
Explain the role of both the small and large intestines in absorption

knowledge_units: Obtaining nutrition and energy from food is a multistep process.
The first step in digestion for true animals is ingestion, the act of taking in food.
The process of digestion includes the steps of ingestion, digestion, absorption, and elimination.
Ingestion is the process of taking in food through the mouth.
During ingestion, teeth, saliva, and tongue mechanically break down food into a bolus.
Salivary enzymes begin the chemical breakdown of food during ingestion.
The combined mechanical and chemical processes during ingestion prepare food for swallowing and passage through the esophagus.
Digestion is the mechanical and chemical breakdown of food into small organic fragments suitable for absorption.
Carbohydrate digestion begins in the mouth with salivary amylase breaking starch into maltose.
Pancreatic amylase in the small intestine continues carbohydrate digestion, and brush border enzymes break disaccharides into monosaccharides.
Monosaccharides are absorbed across the intestinal epithelium into the bloodstream.
Protein digestion starts in the stomach where pepsin breaks proteins into peptides.
In the small intestine, pancreatic enzymes like trypsin and chymotrypsin further break peptides, and peptidases reduce them to amino acids for absorption.
Lipid digestion occurs mainly in the small intestine where bile emulsifies fats into small globules.
Emulsification increases the surface area for pancreatic lipases to efficiently break down lipids into fatty acids and glycerides.
Long-chain fatty acids and monoglycerides are absorbed into intestinal cells and repackaged into chylomicrons for transport via the lymphatic system.
Fat-soluble vitamins are absorbed with lipids, while water-soluble vitamins are absorbed directly into the bloodstream.
Elimination is the final step in digestion involving the removal of undigested food content and waste products.
In the colon, most water is reabsorbed from undigested material, and intestinal flora aid in digestion.
Semi-solid waste is moved through the colon by peristalsis and stored in the rectum, which triggers neural signals for elimination.
Solid waste is eliminated through the anus via peristaltic movements of the rectum.
Emesis is the forceful expulsion of food through the mouth, often in response to irritants like viruses or bacteria, and is regulated by the medulla.

learning_objectives: Discuss the role of neural regulation in digestive processes
Explain how hormones regulate digestion

knowledge_units: The brain is the control center for the sensation of hunger and satiety.
The functions of the digestive system are regulated through neural and hormonal responses.
The cephalic phase of digestion is initiated by neural responses to the sight, smell, or thought of food, leading to salivation and gastric juice secretion.
The gastric phase begins when food enters the stomach and is stimulated by stomach distension, decreased pH, and the presence of undigested material.
The intestinal phase starts when chyme enters the small intestine and controls the rate of gastric emptying through hormonal and neural coordination.
Neural regulation prepares digestive muscles for peristaltic movements and detects satiety signals when the stomach is full.
Gastrin is secreted by stomach G cells in response to proteins and stimulates hydrochloric acid release for protein digestion.
Somatostatin stops hydrochloric acid release when the stomach is empty via a negative feedback mechanism.
Secretin stimulates the pancreas to produce alkaline bicarbonate solution to neutralize acidic chyme in the duodenum.
Cholecystokinin (CCK) stimulates pancreatic juice production and gallbladder bile release into the duodenum.
Gastric inhibitory peptide slows intestinal peristalsis to allow more time for digestion and absorption of fatty foods.

learning_objectives: Describe the role of the nervous system as an organism's control center.
Explain how the nervous system processes sensory information from both external and internal environments.
Identify examples of behaviors controlled by the nervous system, such as eating, sleeping, and finding a mate.
Recognize that the nervous system manages both voluntary actions (e.g., turning pages) and involuntary functions (e.g., breathing, temperature regulation).

knowledge_units: The nervous system serves as an organism's control center.
The nervous system processes sensory information from both external and internal environments.
The nervous system controls behaviors such as eating, sleeping, and finding a mate.
The nervous system manages both voluntary actions, like turning pages, and involuntary functions, like breathing and temperature regulation.

learning_objectives: List and describe the functions of the structural components of a neuron
List and describe the four main types of neurons
Compare the functions of different types of glial cells

knowledge_units: Nervous systems vary in structure and complexity across the animal kingdom, ranging from simple nerve nets to centralized brains and spinal cords.
Vertebrate nervous systems are more complex, centralized, and specialized than invertebrate nervous systems, with a central nervous system (CNS) and a peripheral nervous system (PNS).
The nervous system is composed of neurons, which transmit signals, and glia, which provide support and complementary information processing functions.
There are four major types of neurons that share several important cellular components.
Neurons are specialized cells that communicate with each other and other cell types to control behaviors.
A neuron's cell body (soma) contains a nucleus and other organelles.
Dendrites are tree-like structures that receive messages from other neurons at synapses.
The axon hillock integrates signals from multiple synapses and connects the cell body to the axon.
An axon is a tube-like structure that propagates integrated signals to axon terminals.
Axon terminals synapse on other neurons, muscle, or target organs and release chemicals to communicate signals.
Myelin, produced by glial cells, insulates axons to increase the speed of signal conduction.
Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in the myelin sheath where the signal is recharged as it travels along the axon.
Neurons are broadly divided into four basic types: unipolar, bipolar, multipolar, and pseudounipolar.
Unipolar neurons have one structure extending from the soma and are found in insects.
Bipolar neurons have one axon and one dendrite extending from the soma, such as retinal bipolar cells.
Multipolar neurons have one axon and multiple dendrites and are the most common type, like Purkinje cells.
Pseudounipolar neurons have a single process that branches into two structures, and most sensory neurons are pseudounipolar.

learning_objectives: Describe the basis of the resting membrane potential
Explain the stages of an action potential and how action potentials are propagated
Explain the similarities and differences between chemical and electrical synapses
Describe long-term potentiation and long-term depression

knowledge_units: All nervous system functions require communication between neurons.
Neurons communicate using electrical and chemical signals.
A neuron typically integrates signals from multiple other neurons before transmitting its own signal.
The resting membrane potential is a negative voltage difference across the neuronal membrane, typically around -70 mV.
The resting membrane potential is primarily established by the concentration gradients of ions, especially potassium (K+), across the cell membrane.
The neuronal membrane is more permeable to potassium ions than sodium ions, leading to a net loss of positive charge from the cell interior and a negative resting potential.
The sodium-potassium pump actively transports three sodium ions out and two potassium ions into the cell, helping to maintain the ion concentration gradients that support the resting potential.
An action potential is a brief reversal of the membrane potential that propagates a signal along a neuron's axon.
An action potential is initiated when a depolarizing stimulus brings the membrane potential to a threshold of about -55 mV.
Action potentials are all-or-nothing events; once threshold is reached, the membrane fully depolarizes to about +40 mV.
During an action potential, voltage-gated sodium channels open to allow sodium influx, causing depolarization, and then inactivate, beginning the refractory period.
Voltage-gated potassium channels open during repolarization, allowing potassium efflux to restore the negative resting potential, often causing a brief hyperpolarization.
Myelin sheaths insulate axons and increase the speed of action potential conduction by preventing current leak.
Action potentials are regenerated at the nodes of Ranvier, which contain voltage-gated ion channels, enabling saltatory conduction.
Saltatory conduction allows the action potential to 'jump' between nodes, increasing conduction speed and energy efficiency.
Synapses are the sites where information is transmitted from one neuron to another.
The neuron transmitting the signal is called the presynaptic neuron, and the neuron receiving the signal is called the postsynaptic neuron.
There are two types of synapses: chemical and electrical.
At a chemical synapse, an action potential in the presynaptic neuron triggers the release of neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft.
Neurotransmitter release at a chemical synapse is initiated by calcium ions entering the presynaptic terminal.
Neurotransmitter diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, opening ligand-gated ion channels.
Neurotransmitter binding can cause an excitatory postsynaptic potential (EPSP) or an inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP).
Neurotransmitter is removed from the synaptic cleft by diffusion, enzymatic degradation, or reuptake into the presynaptic neuron.
In an electrical synapse, the presynaptic and postsynaptic membranes are connected by gap junctions that allow direct ion flow.
Signaling in electrical synapses is virtually instantaneous and can be bidirectional, unlike the delayed, unidirectional signaling in chemical synapses.
Electrical synapses are important for synchronizing neuronal activity, such as in regulating slow-wave sleep.
Summation is the process where multiple presynaptic inputs create EPSPs around the same time to sufficiently depolarize the postsynaptic neuron to fire an action potential.
The net change in postsynaptic membrane voltage, determined by the balance of excitatory and inhibitory inputs, determines whether the postsynaptic cell reaches its threshold of excitation.
Synaptic summation and the threshold for excitation act as a filter to prevent random noise from being transmitted as important information.

learning_objectives: Identify the spinal cord, cerebral lobes, and other brain areas on a diagram of the brain
Describe the basic functions of the spinal cord, cerebral lobes, and other brain areas

knowledge_units: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord.
The CNS is protected by three layers of meninges: dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater.
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is produced in the ventricles and cushions the brain, acting as a shock absorber.
Hydrocephalus is a condition where blocked ventricles cause CSF buildup, leading to brain swelling and potential damage.
The brain includes the cerebral cortex, limbic system, basal nuclei, thalamus, hypothalamus, and cerebellum.
The cerebral cortex is divided into two hemispheres connected by the corpus callosum.
The cerebral cortex is organized into four lobes: frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital.
The frontal lobe contains the motor cortex for movement and is involved in cognitive functions like decision-making.
The parietal lobe processes somatosensation and proprioception.
The occipital lobe is primarily involved in vision.
The temporal lobe processes sounds and contains the hippocampus, which is essential for memory formation.
Mammals have exceptionally large brains relative to body size compared to other vertebrates.
Increased cortical surface area, due to folding, is associated with intelligence and complex social behaviors in mammals.
The basal nuclei play important roles in movement control, posture, and motivation.
The thalamus acts as a gateway to and from the cortex, regulating consciousness, arousal, and sleep states.
The hypothalamus controls the endocrine system via the pituitary gland and regulates homeostasis and circadian rhythms.
The limbic system regulates emotion, fear, motivation, and memory formation.
The cerebellum controls balance and aids in coordinating movement and learning new motor tasks.
The brainstem connects the brain to the spinal cord and coordinates motor control signals and vital functions.
The spinal cord carries sensory and motor information between the brain and the body.
The spinal cord controls quick, unconscious motor reflexes through local synaptic connections.
Damage to the spinal cord can lead to paralysis depending on the injury location and severity.

learning_objectives: Describe the organization and functions of the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems
Describe the organization and function of the sensory-somatic nervous system

knowledge_units: The peripheral nervous system (PNS) connects the central nervous system (CNS) to the rest of the body.
The autonomic nervous system controls bodily functions without conscious control.
The sensory-somatic nervous system transmits sensory information to the CNS and sends motor commands to the muscles.
The sympathetic nervous system is responsible for the 'fight or flight' response to dangerous situations.
The sympathetic nervous system accelerates heart rate and inhibits digestion to prepare the body for physical strain.
Most sympathetic preganglionic neurons originate in the spinal cord and release acetylcholine onto postganglionic neurons in sympathetic ganglia.
Sympathetic postganglionic neurons release norepinephrine onto target organs, causing effects like dilating airways and increasing heart rate.
The parasympathetic nervous system allows an animal to 'rest and digest' after stressful situations.
Parasympathetic preganglionic neurons have cell bodies in the brainstem and sacral spinal cord and release acetylcholine onto postganglionic neurons near target organs.
Parasympathetic postganglionic neurons release acetylcholine onto target organs, slowing heart rate and stimulating digestion.
The sensory-somatic nervous system consists of cranial and spinal nerves containing both sensory and motor neurons.
Sensory neurons transmit information from sensory organs to the central nervous system.
Motor neurons transmit movement commands from the central nervous system to muscles.
Sensory and motor neurons have a single synapse between the central nervous system and the target organ.
Acetylcholine is the primary neurotransmitter at sensory-somatic synapses.
Cranial nerves can be sensory, motor, or mixed in function.
Spinal nerves contain both sensory and motor axons and connect the spinal cord to the body.

learning_objectives: Describe the symptoms, potential causes, and treatment of several examples of nervous system disorders

knowledge_units: A correctly functioning nervous system involves appropriate synapse firing, muscle movement, memory formation, and emotion regulation.
Millions of people in the United States are affected by nervous system disorders each year.
Scientists have identified potential causes for many nervous system disorders and viable treatments for some.
Ongoing research aims to improve prevention and treatment for all nervous system disorders.
Neurodegenerative disorders are characterized by a loss of nervous system functioning usually caused by neuronal death that worsens over time.
The symptoms of a neurodegenerative disease are related to where in the nervous system neuronal death occurs.
Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause of dementia in the elderly.
Symptoms of Alzheimer's disease include disruptive memory loss, confusion, difficulty with tasks, poor judgment, and personality changes.
Alzheimer's disease is associated with amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles, and brain volume loss.
A rare early-onset form of Alzheimer's disease is caused by mutations in one of three known genes.
The more prevalent late-onset form of Alzheimer's disease has a genetic component, including the APOE E4 variant.
Current treatments for Alzheimer's disease focus on managing symptoms, such as by increasing acetylcholine neurotransmission.
Parkinson's disease causes the loss of dopamine neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to movement-related symptoms.
Symptoms of Parkinson's disease include tremor, slowed movement, speech changes, balance problems, and rigid muscles.
Parkinson's disease is associated with Lewy bodies in dopaminergic neurons.
Treatment for Parkinson's disease includes L-DOPA to increase dopamine neurotransmission.
Neurodevelopmental disorders occur when the development of the nervous system is disturbed.
Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by impaired social skills, repetitive behaviors, and unusual language use.
ASD is more prevalent in males than females, with current estimates suggesting one in 44 children will develop the disorder.
The causes of ASD are largely unknown, but it is thought to involve incorrect neural wiring and may have a genetic component.
Treatment for autism typically combines behavioral therapies and medications to manage associated conditions, but there is no cure.

Attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder affecting three to five percent of children and adults, with symptoms including inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity.
ADHD is more prevalent in males than females and often involves comorbid conditions like depression or obsessive compulsive disorder.
The cause of ADHD is unknown but may involve dysfunction in prefrontal cortex development and neurotransmission, with a strong genetic component.
Treatment for ADHD often includes behavioral therapies and stimulant medications, which have a calming effect in these patients.

learning_objectives: Explain how sensory systems in bilaterally symmetric animals detect stimuli from both external and internal environments.
Describe how natural selection drives the development of sensory systems, leading to adaptations like electrosensitivity in sharks.
Compare sensory adaptations in different species, such as the electrosensitivity of sharks versus the sensory capabilities of most land animals.

knowledge_units: Sensory systems in bilaterally symmetric animals detect stimuli from both external and internal environments.
The development of sensory systems is driven by natural selection, leading to adaptations suited to specific environmental demands.
Sharks possess electrosensitivity, a sensory adaptation for detecting electrical fields, which is not common in most land animals.

learning_objectives: Identify the general and special senses in humans
Describe three important steps in sensory perception
Explain the concept of just-noticeable differences in sensory perception

knowledge_units: Humans have five special senses: olfaction, gustation, equilibrium, vision, and hearing.
General senses, also called somatosensation, respond to stimuli like temperature, pain, pressure, and vibration.
Sensory transduction is the process of converting a stimulus into an electrical signal in the nervous system.
In one type of sensory transduction, a specialized sensory receptor cell activates an associated afferent neuron.
In the second type of sensory transduction, a sensory nerve ending itself acts as the sensory receptor.
Free nerve endings can be stimulated by several different stimuli, showing little receptor specificity.
Reception is the activation of sensory receptors by stimuli such as mechanical, chemical, or temperature changes.
A sensory receptor's receptive field is the region in space where it can respond to a stimulus.
Receptive fields vary by sense, with touch requiring contact, hearing allowing moderate distances, and vision perceiving stimuli at very far distances.
Transduction is the translation of a sensory signal into an electrical signal in the nervous system.
A receptor potential is a graded electrical potential change produced at a sensory receptor.
Receptor potentials vary in magnitude with the strength of the stimulus.
If a receptor potential depolarizes the neuron to threshold, it triggers an action potential.
Sensory receptors exhibit receptor specificity, meaning they are specialized to respond to particular types of stimuli.
Sensory systems encode the type, location, duration, and relative intensity of a stimulus.
The intensity of a stimulus is encoded by the rate of action potentials and the number of receptors activated.
Perception is an individual's interpretation of a sensation that occurs at higher levels in the nervous system, specifically in the brain.
Sensory signals from most senses are routed through the thalamus to specific regions of the cortex for processing.
The just-noticeable difference (JND) is the smallest detectable difference between two stimuli.
Weber's Law states that the just-noticeable difference in a stimulus is proportional to the magnitude of the original stimulus.

learning_objectives: Describe four important mechanoreceptors in human skin
Describe the topographical distribution of somatosensory receptors between glabrous and hairy skin
Explain why the perception of pain is subjective

knowledge_units: Somatosensation includes all sensation received from the skin, mucous membranes, limbs, and joints.
The epidermis is the outermost layer of skin that serves as a barrier to water and pathogens.
The dermis is a thicker layer below the epidermis containing blood vessels, sweat glands, and hair follicles.
The hypodermis is the fatty subcutaneous tissue that attaches the dermis to bone and muscle and supplies nerves and blood vessels.

Mechanoreceptors in the skin are classified as encapsulated or unencapsulated, with free nerve endings being unencapsulated dendrites that are sensitive to painful stimuli, temperature, and light touch.
There are four primary tactile mechanoreceptors in human skin: Merkel's disks, Meissner's corpuscles, Ruffini endings, and Pacinian corpuscles.
Merkel's disks are slow-adapting, encapsulated nerve endings located in the upper layers of skin that respond to light touch and have small receptive fields for precise localization.
Touch receptors are denser in glabrous skin (e.g., fingertips and lips) compared to hairy skin, making glabrous skin more sensitive.
The two-point discrimination test is used to estimate the density of pressure receptors by determining the minimum distance at which two points are perceived as separate.
Krause end bulbs detect cold and Ruffini endings detect warmth in the skin.
Thermoreceptors are located in the dermis, skeletal muscles, liver, and hypothalamus and are activated by different temperatures.
Temperature sensations are conducted along the same pathways as pain sensations, so an intense temperature stimulus can be perceived as pain.
Pain is the perception of nociception, which is the neural processing of injurious stimuli in response to tissue damage.
Pain can be caused by harmless stimuli that mimic damaging stimuli, such as capsaicin, which activates the same calcium channels as warm receptors.
Nociceptive information is communicated to the brain via pathways that project to the thalamus and primary somatosensory cortex for conscious perception.
One nociceptive pathway projects directly to the hypothalamus to modulate autonomic nervous system functions like the fight-or-flight response.

learning_objectives: Explain in what way smell and taste stimuli differ from other sensory stimuli
Identify the five primary tastes that can be distinguished by humans
Explain in anatomical terms why a dog’s sense of smell is more acute than a human’s

knowledge_units: Taste and smell are interconnected senses that both involve molecules of the stimulus entering the body and bonding to receptors.
The primary tastes detected by humans are sweet, sour, bitter, salty, and umami.
Umami is a fundamental taste attributable to the amino acid L-glutamate and is associated with savory, protein-rich substances.
Odors are molecules in the air that bind to specific olfactory receptors, and humans have about 350 olfactory receptor subtypes.
Taste and smell work together to create the perception of flavor, which is reduced with congested nasal passages.
Odorants dissolve in the olfactory epithelium, where specialized olfactory receptors send impulses directly to the olfactory bulb.
Humans have about 12 million olfactory receptors, while dogs have about 1 billion, contributing to a more acute sense of smell.
Each olfactory sensory neuron has only one type of receptor specialized to detect specific odorants.
Olfactory stimulation is the only sensory information that directly reaches the cerebral cortex without relay through the thalamus.
Pheromones are chemicals released by an animal that affect the behavior or physiology of animals of the same species.
Pheromones are not consciously perceived in the same way as other odors.
The vomeronasal organ (VNO) is a tubular, fluid-filled olfactory organ present in many vertebrates that is sensitive to pheromones.
Pheromonal signals are sent to the amygdala and hypothalamus, not the main olfactory bulb.
The primary organ of taste is the taste bud, which is a cluster of gustatory receptors located within papillae on the tongue.
There are five primary tastes in humans, each with a corresponding type of receptor.
Salty tastants provide sodium ions that directly excite taste neurons.
Sour tastants are acids that trigger changes in ion channels to depolarize taste neurons.
Sweet, bitter, and umami tastants bind to G-protein coupled receptors to excite specialized neurons.
Olfactory signals travel directly to the olfactory cortex, amygdala, and other brain areas, bypassing the thalamus initially.
Taste signals project from taste cells to the medulla, then to the thalamus and primary gustatory cortex.

learning_objectives: Describe the relationship of amplitude and frequency of a sound wave to attributes of sound
Trace the path of sound through the auditory system to the site of transduction of sound
Identify the structures of the vestibular system that respond to gravity

knowledge_units: Audition enables an organism to detect and receive information about danger and participate in communal exchanges.
The vestibular system is not involved in hearing but detects an animal's own movement, including linear and angular acceleration and deceleration, and balance.
Sound waves are mechanical pressure waves that require a medium to travel and cannot propagate in a vacuum.
Frequency of a sound wave is perceived as pitch, with higher frequencies corresponding to higher pitches.
Most humans can perceive sounds with frequencies between 30 and 20,000 Hz.
Amplitude of a sound wave is perceived as volume, with greater amplitude corresponding to louder sounds.
Sound waves are collected by the pinna and travel through the auditory canal to vibrate the tympanum.
The middle ear contains three ossicles (malleus, incus, and stapes) that transfer energy from the tympanum to the inner ear.
The ossicles amplify sound vibrations and are unique to mammals, though other vertebrates use a single stapes for transmission.
The ear transduces mechanical pressure waves into nerve impulses that the brain perceives as sound.
Sound vibrations are transmitted from the tympanum through the middle ear bones to the oval window of the inner ear.
The cochlea contains the basilar membrane, which acts as a mechanical analyzer for sound frequency.
Different regions of the basilar membrane vibrate in response to different sound frequencies due to changes in its mechanical properties.
Inner hair cells are the primary receptors for conveying auditory information to the brain via afferent neurons.
The vestibular system detects linear acceleration (gravity) and angular acceleration and deceleration by evaluating inertia on receptive cells.
The vestibular system uses hair cells, similar to the auditory system, but excites them in different ways.
The utricle and saccule respond to linear acceleration, such as gravity, through hair cells embedded in a gelatinous layer with calcium carbonate crystals.
When the head tilts, gravity pulls the crystals, shifting the gelatin and bending stereocilia to signal head position for balance.
The three semicircular canals are fluid-filled loops arranged in three spatial planes to detect angular acceleration and deceleration.
Each semicircular canal has a cupula with hair cells that bend when fluid shifts due to head rotation, signaling changes in velocity.
The semicircular canals are not sensitive to constant velocity but to changes in velocity, such as acceleration or deceleration.
Vestibular hair cells communicate through bipolar neurons to the cochlear nucleus in the medulla, with projections to the cerebellum for coordinated movements.
Vestibular signals project to the temporal cortex for feelings of dizziness and to autonomic brainstem areas for motion sickness.
Projections to the primary somatosensory cortex monitor self-movement, and lesions there can cause misperception of vertical objects as tilted.

learning_objectives: Explain how electromagnetic waves differ from sound waves
Trace the path of light through the eye to the point of the optic nerve
Explain tonic activity as it is manifested in photoreceptors in the retina

knowledge_units: Vision is the ability to detect light patterns from the environment and interpret them as images.
The visual systems of species have evolved to attend to the most-important stimuli to manage the volume of sensory information.
About one-third of the human cerebral cortex is dedicated to analyzing and perceiving visual information.
Light is composed of electromagnetic waves and can travel in a vacuum, unlike sound waves which require a medium.
Wavelength of light manifests as hue, with longer wavelengths perceived as red and shorter wavelengths as violet.
Wave amplitude of light is perceived as luminous intensity or brightness.
White light is perceived when light stimulates equally the color receptors in the human eye.
The apparent color of an object is determined by the wavelengths of light it reflects.
Photoreceptive cells in the retina transduce light into nervous impulses.
The cornea and crystalline lens refract light to focus images on the retina.
The iris regulates light entry by adjusting pupil size in response to ambient light levels.
Rods and cones are the site of transduction of light to a neural signal.
Photopigments in rods and cones, such as rhodopsin, contain an opsin protein and retinal, which absorbs light.
Light causes isomerization of retinal from cis to trans form, activating rhodopsin.
Activation of rhodopsin leads to closing of Na+ channels, causing hyperpolarization of photoreceptors.
There are three types of cones (S, M, L) with different photopsins, each maximally responsive to different wavelengths, enabling trichromatic color vision.
Perceived color results from the ratio of activity among the three cone types.
Visual signals travel from photoreceptors to bipolar cells to ganglion cells, with processing occurring in the retina.
Photoreceptors exhibit tonic activity, being slightly active even without light stimulation.
In the absence of light, rods and cones inhibit bipolar cells; light hyperpolarizes photoreceptors, removing inhibition and activating bipolar cells.
Ganglion cells send action potentials along the optic nerve, encoding visual signals based on changes in retinal activity.
Horizontal cells create lateral inhibition, sharpening edges and enhancing contrast in images.
The myelinated axons of ganglion cells form the optic nerves, which carry different visual qualities in separate pathways.
The magnocellular pathway carries information about form, movement, depth, and brightness differences.
The parvocellular pathway carries information about color and fine detail.
Visual information crosses at the optic chiasma to allow coordination between both eyes.
Visual signals are routed through the thalamus, where magnocellular and parvocellular pathways remain in distinct layers.
From the thalamus, visual signals travel to the primary visual cortex and then diverge to the parietal and temporal lobes.
The parietal lobe stream carries magnocellular ('where') information.
The temporal lobe stream carries both magnocellular ('where') and parvocellular ('what') information.
A pathway from the retina to the superior colliculus coordinates eye movements and integrates visual with auditory information.
A pathway from the retina to the suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) of the hypothalamus helps regulate the body's circadian rhythm.

learning_objectives: Explain the role of the endocrine system in controlling body processes through hormone production and regulation.
Describe how hormones function as chemical messengers to maintain homeostasis.
Identify key functions of the endocrine system, including growth, metabolism, and sexual development.
Provide examples of common endocrine system diseases in humans, such as thyroid disease and diabetes mellitus.
Explain how the endocrine system controls metamorphosis in organisms like frogs.

knowledge_units: The endocrine system controls body processes through the production, secretion, and regulation of hormones.
Hormones function as chemical messengers that influence cellular and organ activity to maintain homeostasis.
The endocrine system plays a role in growth, metabolism, and sexual development.
Common endocrine system diseases in humans include thyroid disease and diabetes mellitus.
In organisms that undergo metamorphosis, such as frogs, the process is controlled by the endocrine system.

learning_objectives: List the different types of hormones
Explain their role in maintaining homeostasis

knowledge_units: Hormones are chemicals released into body fluids that travel to target cells to elicit a response.
The endocrine system consists of cells, tissues, and organs that secrete hormones.
Hormones can be classified into three chemical structure classes: lipid-derived, amino acid-derived, and peptide hormones.
Lipid-derived hormones can diffuse across plasma membranes, while amino acid-derived and peptide hormones cannot.
Most lipid hormones are derived from cholesterol and are structurally similar to it.
The primary class of lipid hormones in humans is the steroid hormones.
Steroid hormones are insoluble in water and are transported by transport proteins in blood.
Steroid hormones remain in circulation longer than peptide hormones due to their transport by proteins.
Amino acid-derived hormones are small molecules derived from the amino acids tyrosine and tryptophan.
Amino acid-derived hormones have chemical names ending in '-ine'.
Examples of amino acid-derived hormones include epinephrine, norepinephrine, thyroxine, and melatonin.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine are synthesized in the adrenal medulla.
Thyroxine is produced by the thyroid gland.
Melatonin is secreted by the pineal gland and regulates sleep cycles.

Peptide hormones are polypeptide chains that include short chains like antidiuretic hormone and oxytocin, small proteins like growth hormone, and large glycoproteins like follicle-stimulating hormone.
Peptide hormones are water-soluble and cannot pass through plasma membranes, so their receptors are located on the surface of target cells.
Peptide hormones are stored in vesicles and released in response to specific stimuli, such as insulin release in response to high blood glucose levels.

learning_objectives: Explain how hormones work
Discuss the role of different types of hormone receptors

knowledge_units: Hormones mediate changes in target cells by binding to specific hormone receptors.
Hormones only affect cells that possess the necessary receptors for that hormone.
The number of receptors for a hormone determines the cell's sensitivity to that hormone.
In up-regulation, the number of receptors increases in response to rising hormone levels, increasing cell sensitivity.
In down-regulation, the number of receptors decreases in response to rising hormone levels, reducing cellular activity.
Hormones can act directly by binding to intracellular receptors to modulate gene transcription.
Hormones can act indirectly by binding to cell surface receptors to stimulate signaling pathways.
Lipid-derived hormones such as steroid hormones diffuse across the plasma membrane of target cells to bind intracellular receptors in the cytoplasm or nucleus.
The hormone-receptor complex acts as a transcription regulator, altering gene expression by increasing or decreasing mRNA synthesis of specific genes.
Changes in gene expression lead to the synthesis of proteins that can alter cell structure or produce enzymes, thereby regulating specific cell processes.
Lipid-insoluble hormones bind to receptors on the outer surface of the plasma membrane because they cannot diffuse through it.
Binding of a lipid-insoluble hormone to a cell surface receptor activates an intracellular signaling pathway without the hormone entering the cell.
The hormone that initiates the signaling pathway is called a first messenger, which activates a second messenger in the cytoplasm.
Cyclic AMP (cAMP) is an important second messenger that is produced when adenylyl cyclase is activated by a G-protein.
G-proteins are activated when a hormone binds to its membrane receptor, causing them to bind GTP instead of GDP.
Activated protein kinases transfer phosphate groups to substrate molecules, changing their structure and activating them.
The signaling pathway amplifies the hormone's effect, as one hormone binding event can lead to the production of many second messenger molecules.
Phosphodiesterase (PDE) deactivates cAMP to control hormone activity and prevent overproduction of cellular products.
The specific cellular response to a lipid-insoluble hormone depends on the types of receptors and substrate molecules present in the cell.

learning_objectives: Explain how hormones regulate the excretory system
Discuss the role of hormones in the reproductive system
Describe how hormones regulate metabolism
Explain the role of hormones in different diseases

knowledge_units: Hormones modulate many different body processes including the excretory system, reproductive system, metabolism, blood calcium concentrations, growth, and the stress response.
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) regulates the amount of water excreted by the kidneys by increasing water reabsorption from kidney tubules.
ADH secretion is controlled by the hypothalamus in response to blood volume or water concentration, and is reduced by substances like alcohol.
Chronic underproduction of ADH or a mutation in the ADH receptor results in diabetes insipidus, causing increased thirst and urine production.
Aldosterone, a mineralocorticoid produced by the adrenal cortex, maintains water balance by enhancing sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion in kidney tubules.
Aldosterone release is stimulated by low blood pressure, low blood sodium, low blood volume, or high blood potassium.
The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is activated by low blood pressure, leading to increased aldosterone release and water retention.
Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus stimulates the anterior pituitary to release follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH).
FSH and LH regulate the gonads and are called gonadotropins; FSH stimulates gamete production and LH stimulates hormone production by the gonads.
In males, FSH stimulates sperm cell maturation, and LH stimulates testosterone production by the testes.
Testosterone promotes sperm production and masculine characteristics.
The fight-or-flight response is an evolved mechanism to counter stress-induced disruptions of homeostasis.
The sympathetic nervous system stimulates an increase in blood glucose levels during the initial alarm phase of stress.
Glucocorticoids mobilize lipid and protein reserves and stimulate gluconeogenesis to meet long-term energy needs during stress.
The hypothalamus signals the adrenal medulla via nerve impulses for short-term stress responses and the adrenal cortex via ACTH for long-term stress responses.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine increase blood glucose levels by stimulating glycogen breakdown and glucose release from the liver.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine increase oxygen availability by increasing heart rate and dilating bronchioles.
Long-term stress response involves ACTH stimulating the adrenal cortex to release corticosteroids, which affect cellular metabolism.
Glucocorticoids primarily affect glucose metabolism by stimulating glucose synthesis and have anti-inflammatory properties.
Mineralocorticoids like aldosterone regulate ion and water balance by stimulating reabsorption of water and sodium in the kidney.
Hypersecretion of glucocorticoids can cause Cushing’s disease, while hyposecretion can cause Addison’s disease.

learning_objectives: Explain how hormone production is regulated
Discuss the different stimuli that control hormone levels in the body

knowledge_units: Hormone production and release are primarily controlled by negative feedback systems.
In negative feedback, a substance's rising level signals to stop further release, maintaining hormone concentration within a narrow range.
Endocrine glands are stimulated to synthesize and release hormones by humoral, hormonal, and neural stimuli.
A humoral stimulus controls hormone release in response to changes in extracellular fluids such as blood or ion concentration.
A rise in blood glucose levels triggers the pancreatic release of insulin.
Insulin causes blood glucose levels to drop, which signals the pancreas to stop producing insulin in a negative feedback loop.
Hormonal stimuli refers to the release of a hormone in response to another hormone.
The hypothalamus produces hormones that stimulate the anterior pituitary gland.
The anterior pituitary releases hormones that regulate hormone production by other endocrine glands.
Thyroid-stimulating hormone from the anterior pituitary stimulates the thyroid gland to produce T3 and T4.
Rising blood concentrations of T3 and T4 inhibit the pituitary and hypothalamus in a negative feedback loop.
Neural stimuli refer to the nervous system directly stimulating endocrine glands to release hormones.
In a short-term stress response, neuronal signaling from the sympathetic nervous system directly stimulates the adrenal medulla to release epinephrine and norepinephrine.

learning_objectives: Describe the role of different glands in the endocrine system
Explain how the different glands work together to maintain homeostasis

knowledge_units: The endocrine system releases hormones that act on target cells to regulate development, growth, energy metabolism, reproduction, and many behaviors.
The nervous system releases neurotransmitters or neurohormones that regulate neurons, muscle cells, and endocrine cells.
The nervous and endocrine systems work in a coordinated manner because neurons can regulate the release of hormones.
The hypothalamus integrates the endocrine and nervous systems and initiates endocrine responses to environmental changes.
The hypothalamus synthesizes and secretes regulatory hormones that control the endocrine cells in the anterior pituitary gland.
The pituitary gland is attached to the hypothalamus via the pituitary stalk and is often called the 'master gland'.
The anterior pituitary is regulated by releasing or release-inhibiting hormones from the hypothalamus, which travel via the hypophyseal portal system.
The anterior pituitary produces seven tropic hormones, including growth hormone, prolactin, and thyroid-stimulating hormone, whose secretion is controlled by hypothalamic regulatory hormones.
The posterior pituitary contains axons from hypothalamic neurons and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, such as antidiuretic hormone and oxytocin.
The posterior pituitary does not produce hormones but stores and releases hormones synthesized by the hypothalamus into the bloodstream.
The thyroid gland is located in the neck and consists of two lobes connected by an isthmus.
Thyroid follicles produce and store thyroid hormones in a colloid containing thyroglobulin.
Thyroid follicle cells synthesize the hormones thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3).
Thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) from the anterior pituitary stimulates the release of T3 and T4.
Thyroid hormones increase the rate of mitochondrial ATP production.
Calcitonin is produced by parafollicular cells of the thyroid in response to high blood calcium levels.
Calcitonin lowers blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclast activity in bones and stimulating calcium excretion in kidneys.
The parathyroid glands produce parathyroid hormone (PTH) to increase blood calcium concentrations when calcium ion levels fall below normal.
PTH enhances reabsorption of calcium ions by the kidneys.
PTH stimulates osteoclast activity and inhibits osteoblast activity to release calcium from bone.
PTH stimulates the synthesis and secretion of calcitriol by the kidneys, which enhances calcium absorption by the digestive system.
PTH and calcitonin work in opposition to maintain homeostatic calcium ion levels in body fluids.
The adrenal glands are located on top of each kidney and consist of an outer adrenal cortex and an inner adrenal medulla that secrete different hormones.
The adrenal cortex has three distinct regions: the zona glomerulosa produces mineralocorticoids, the zona fasciculata produces glucocorticoids, and the zona reticularis produces androgens.
Aldosterone, the main mineralocorticoid, regulates sodium ion concentration in body fluids and is stimulated by decreased blood sodium, volume, or pressure, or increased potassium.
Glucocorticoids like cortisol stimulate glucose synthesis and gluconeogenesis in the liver and promote fatty acid release from adipose tissue to maintain blood glucose levels.
The adrenal medulla contains cells that produce epinephrine and norepinephrine, which increase heart rate, breathing rate, blood pressure, and blood glucose levels in response to stress.
Release of epinephrine and norepinephrine is stimulated by neural impulses from the sympathetic nervous system, originating from the hypothalamus to prepare the body for the fight-or-flight response.
The pancreas is a heterocrine gland with both exocrine and endocrine functions.
The endocrine cells of the pancreas are organized into pancreatic islets containing alpha and beta cells.
Alpha cells produce glucagon to raise blood glucose levels by promoting glycogen breakdown and glucose release from the liver.
Beta cells produce insulin to lower blood glucose levels by increasing glucose uptake in body cells and glycogen synthesis in muscles and the liver.
Glucagon and insulin work together to regulate blood glucose levels and maintain homeostasis.
The pineal gland produces melatonin, which is regulated by the photoperiod.
Melatonin production increases during the dark photoperiod (night) and decreases during the day.
Melatonin can inhibit reproductive functions in some mammals by decreasing sperm, oocyte, and reproductive organ production and maturation.
Melatonin acts as an antioxidant, protecting the central nervous system from free radicals.
Melatonin is involved in regulating biological rhythms, including circadian rhythms such as the sleep-wake cycle.
The gonads are the male testes and female ovaries, which produce steroid hormones.
The testes produce androgens, such as testosterone, which promote the development of secondary sex characteristics and sperm production.
The ovaries produce estradiol and progesterone, which cause secondary sex characteristics and prepare the body for childbirth.
The heart releases atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) in response to increased blood volume, which reduces blood volume and blood pressure by promoting sodium and water excretion.
The gastrointestinal tract produces hormones such as gastrin, secretin, and cholecystokinin to aid digestion by triggering the release of gastric juices.

The kidneys release renin to regulate blood volume via the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, calcitriol to aid calcium absorption, and erythropoietin to stimulate red blood cell production in response to low oxygen.
The thymus produces thymosins that contribute to the development of the immune response.
Adipose tissue produces leptin in response to food intake, which promotes satiety and influences reproductive functions.

learning_objectives: Describe the primary functions of the muscular and skeletal systems in providing support, protection, and movement.
Explain how injury or disease, such as arthritis or malnutrition, can impair the musculoskeletal system and affect mobility.
Identify common types of joint replacement surgeries and the current limitations of prosthetic joint durability.
Discuss ongoing research aimed at improving prosthesis design through the use of advanced materials like carbon fiber.

knowledge_units: The skeletal system provides support, protects internal organs, and supports body weight.
The muscular system contracts to pull on bones, enabling diverse movements such as standing, walking, and grasping.
Injury or disease, such as arthritis or malnutrition, can impair the musculoskeletal system and affect mobility.
Joint replacement surgery is common for hips and knees, but current prostheses have limited durability and wear out quickly.
Research is focused on using advanced materials like carbon fiber to improve prosthesis durability.

learning_objectives: Discuss the different types of skeletal systems
Explain the role of the human skeletal system
Compare and contrast different skeletal systems

knowledge_units: A skeletal system supports the body, protects internal organs, and allows for movement.
There are three main types of skeletal systems: hydrostatic skeleton, exoskeleton, and endoskeleton.
A hydrostatic skeleton uses fluid-filled compartments to provide support and movement in soft-bodied animals.
An exoskeleton is an external hard encasement that provides defense, support, and movement in arthropods.
An endoskeleton consists of internal hard, mineralized structures that provide support, protection, and movement in vertebrates.
The human skeleton is an endoskeleton with five main functions: support, mineral storage, blood cell production, organ protection, and movement.
The human skeletal system is divided into the axial skeleton and the appendicular skeleton.
The axial skeleton includes the skull, vertebral column, and thoracic cage, providing support and protection for the brain, spinal cord, and thoracic organs.
The appendicular skeleton includes the bones of the upper and lower limbs and their girdles, facilitating movement and manipulation.
The vertebral column protects the spinal cord, supports the head, and allows for movement through intervertebral discs.
The thoracic cage encloses and protects the heart and lungs and enables breathing through changes in thoracic volume.
The pectoral girdle attaches the upper limbs to the axial skeleton, while the pelvic girdle attaches the lower limbs and bears body weight.
The transition of vertebrates onto land required changes in body design to address challenges like gravity and lack of buoyancy.
Gravity on land led to increased strengthening and ossification of limbs to support body weight.
A firmer, more ossified vertebral column evolved in terrestrial tetrapods to reduce torsional strain from lateral undulations.
The loss of direct attachment between the pectoral girdle and the head reduced jarring to the head from limb impacts.
Early tetrapods had limbs splayed to the side, requiring large muscles for movement, which was energy inefficient.
Later tetrapods evolved limbs placed under the body, allowing more efficient forward motion with less force per stride.
The rotation of the femur and humerus in later tetrapods pointed limbs and digits forward for efficient locomotion.

learning_objectives: Classify the different types of bones in the skeleton
Explain the role of the different cell types in bone
Explain how bone forms during development

knowledge_units: Bone is a connective tissue that constitutes the endoskeleton and contains specialized cells and a matrix of mineral salts and collagen fibers.
Calcification is the process of deposition of mineral salts on the collagen fiber matrix that crystallizes and hardens the tissue.
The bones of the human skeleton are classified by their shape into long bones, short bones, flat bones, sutural bones, sesamoid bones, and irregular bones.
Long bones are longer than they are wide, have a shaft (diaphysis) and two ends (epiphyses), and most limb bones are long bones.
Short bones are cube-like in shape and include the bones of the wrist and ankle.
Flat bones are thin and broad, providing protection or muscle attachment surfaces, such as the sternum and ribs.
Irregular bones have complex shapes, such as vertebrae and hip bones.
Sesamoid bones are small, flat bones that develop inside tendons, like the patellae.
Sutural bones are small, flat, irregularly shaped bones found between the flat bones of the skull.
Bones are organs composed of various tissues including blood, connective tissue, nerves, and bone tissue.
Osteocytes are the living cells of bone tissue that form the mineral matrix of bones.
There are two types of bone tissue: compact and spongy.
Compact bone forms the hard external layer of all bones and provides protection and strength.
Compact bone tissue consists of osteons, which are cylindrical structures containing a mineral matrix and living osteocytes.
Spongy bone forms the inner layer of all bones and consists of trabeculae arranged as rods or plates.
Red bone marrow is found between the trabeculae of spongy bone and forms blood cells.
Spongy bone reduces bone density and allows the ends of long bones to compress under stress.
Spongy bone is prominent in areas of bones that are not heavily stressed or where stresses arrive from many directions.
Osteoblasts are bone cells responsible for bone formation by synthesizing and secreting the organic and inorganic parts of the extracellular matrix.
Osteoclasts are large, multinucleated cells that remove bone structure by releasing enzymes and acids to dissolve the bony matrix, helping regulate blood calcium levels.
Osteocytes are mature bone cells that maintain normal bone structure by recycling mineral salts in the bony matrix.
Osteoprogenitor cells are stem cells that divide to produce osteoblasts and are important in bone repair.
Ossification is the process of bone formation by osteoblasts.
Intramembranous ossification is the development of bone from fibrous membranes.
Endochondral ossification is the development of bone from hyaline cartilage.
Bone growth through ossification continues until approximately age 25.
After age 25, ossification functions primarily in bone remodeling and repair.
Intramembranous ossification is the process of bone development from fibrous membranes.
Intramembranous ossification forms the flat bones of the skull, the mandible, and the clavicles.
During intramembranous ossification, mesenchymal cells differentiate into osteoblasts at the ossification center.
Osteoblasts secrete extracellular matrix and deposit calcium to harden the matrix during intramembranous ossification.
Endochondral ossification is the process of bone development from hyaline cartilage.
Endochondral ossification forms all bones except the flat bones of the skull, mandible, and clavicles.
In endochondral ossification, chondrocytes form a hyaline cartilage template that calcifies, leading to chondrocyte death and cavity formation.
Blood vessels invade cavities in the calcified cartilage, and osteoblasts and osteoclasts convert it into spongy bone during endochondral ossification.
Osteoclasts break down spongy bone to create a medullary cavity in the diaphysis during endochondral ossification.
The periosteum, a dense irregular connective tissue sheath, forms around bones during endochondral ossification.
Secondary ossification centers form in the epiphyses as blood vessels and osteoblasts convert hyaline cartilage into spongy bone.
The epiphyseal plate, a region of hyaline cartilage, is responsible for lengthwise growth of long bones until adolescence.
Long bones lengthen through the addition of bone tissue at the epiphyseal plate.
Chondrocytes on the epiphyseal side of the epiphyseal plate divide, with one cell moving toward the diaphysis, maturing, and being replaced by bone through calcification.
Epiphyseal plate closure occurs when cartilage cells stop dividing and all cartilage is replaced by bone, resulting in the fusion of the epiphysis and diaphysis.
Appositional growth increases bone diameter by adding bony tissue at the bone surface through the action of osteoblasts and osteoclasts.
Bone remodeling is the replacement of old bone tissue by new bone tissue through deposition by osteoblasts and resorption by osteoclasts.
Normal bone growth and maintenance require vitamins D, C, and A, minerals like calcium and phosphorus, and hormones such as parathyroid hormone and calcitonin.
Bone remodeling allows bones to adapt to mechanical stress by becoming thicker and stronger, while bones not under normal stress lose mass.
Bone repair after a fracture occurs in four stages: hematoma formation, fibrocartilaginous callus formation, bony callus formation, and remodeling of the bony callus.

learning_objectives: Classify the different types of joints on the basis of structure
Explain the role of joints in skeletal movement

knowledge_units: A joint is the point where two or more bones meet.
Joints are responsible for movement and stability in the skeleton.
Structural classification divides joints into bony, fibrous, cartilaginous, and synovial joints based on their material and presence of a cavity.
Fibrous joints are held together by fibrous connective tissue and generally allow little to no movement.
Cartilaginous joints are connected by cartilage and allow very little movement.
Synovial joints have a synovial cavity filled with synovial fluid that lubricates the joint, allowing for greater movement.
The more mobile a synovial joint is, the weaker it tends to be.
Joints are functionally classified into three categories: synarthroses, amphiarthroses, and diarthroses.
Synarthroses are immovable joints, such as sutures, gomphoses, and synchondroses.
Amphiarthroses are joints that allow slight movement, including syndesmoses and symphyses.
Diarthroses are joints that allow free movement, as seen in synovial joints.
Synovial joints allow gliding, angular, rotational, and special movements.
Gliding movements occur when relatively flat bone surfaces move past each other.
Angular movements change the angle between bones and include flexion, extension, hyperextension, abduction, adduction, and circumduction.
Rotational movement involves a bone rotating around its longitudinal axis.
Special movements include inversion, eversion, protraction, retraction, elevation, depression, dorsiflexion, plantar flexion, supination, pronation, and opposition.
Synovial joints are classified into six categories based on the shape and structure of the joint.
The shape of a synovial joint determines the type of movement it permits.
Planar joints have flat or slightly curved articulating surfaces that allow gliding movements.
Hinge joints permit movement in one plane, similar to a door hinge.
Pivot joints allow rotational movement around a single axis.
Condyloid joints permit angular movement along two axes.
Saddle joints allow a greater range of angular movement than condyloid joints.
Ball-and-socket joints permit the greatest range of motion, allowing movement in all directions.

learning_objectives: Classify the different types of muscle tissue
Explain the role of muscles in locomotion

knowledge_units: The body contains three types of muscle tissue: skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle, and smooth muscle.
Skeletal muscle tissue attaches to bones or skin and controls locomotion and voluntary movement.
Skeletal muscle is also called voluntary muscle because it can be consciously controlled.
Skeletal muscle tissue has a striated appearance due to the regular arrangement of actin and myosin proteins.
Smooth muscle tissue is found in the walls of hollow organs and around passages and is involuntary.
Cardiac muscle tissue is only found in the heart and is involuntary.
Cardiac muscle is striated and distinguished by the presence of intercalated disks.
Skeletal muscle fibers are large cells with a plasma membrane called the sarcolemma that conducts action potentials to trigger contraction.
Myofibrils are long cylindrical structures within muscle fibers that run the entire length of the cell and attach to the sarcolemma.
The striated appearance of skeletal muscle results from repeating bands of actin and myosin proteins along myofibrils.
Sarcomeres are the functional units of skeletal muscle, defined as the region between two consecutive Z discs.
Thick filaments are composed of myosin and are located in the A band of a myofibril.
Thin filaments are composed of actin, tropomyosin, and troponin, and they attach to Z discs.
Tropomyosin blocks actin binding sites at rest, preventing actin-myosin interactions.
Troponin binds to tropomyosin, actin, and calcium ions to regulate muscle contraction.
Muscle contraction occurs when thick and thin filaments slide past each other without shortening, causing the sarcomere to shorten.
The sliding filament theory explains muscle contraction through the binding of myosin to actin, forming cross-bridges that generate filament movement.
During sarcomere shortening, the H zone and I band shorten while the A band remains the same length, and the zone of overlap increases.
Muscle shortening occurs when myosin heads bind to actin and pull actin inward, a process that requires energy from ATP.
ATP binding to myosin causes myosin to release actin, allowing detachment of the cross-bridge.
Hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and inorganic phosphate by myosin ATPase provides energy to change the myosin head into a 'cocked' high-energy position.
The power stroke is the step where myosin releases stored energy to pull actin toward the M line, producing force and causing sarcomere shortening.
After the power stroke, ATP binding to myosin allows the cross-bridge cycle to restart for further muscle contraction.
Tropomyosin blocks myosin binding sites on actin molecules to prevent cross-bridge formation in resting muscle.
Troponin binds to tropomyosin and helps position it on actin, and also binds calcium ions.
Calcium ions bind to troponin, causing conformational changes that allow tropomyosin to move away from myosin binding sites on actin.
