In terrestrial environments, net primary productivity is estimated by measuring aboveground biomass per unit area, excluding roots.
Warm and wet climates optimize photosynthesis and plant growth, leading to high net primary productivity and biomass production.
Dry and cold environments have lower photosynthetic rates, resulting in less biomass and reduced food availability for animal communities.

learning_objectives: Identify the two major abiotic factors that determine terrestrial biomes
Recognize distinguishing characteristics of each of the eight major terrestrial biomes

knowledge_units: Terrestrial biomes are categorized based on land, while aquatic biomes include ocean and freshwater biomes.
The eight major terrestrial biomes are distinguished by characteristic temperatures and amounts of precipitation.
Temperature variation on a daily and seasonal basis is important for predicting the geographic distribution of a terrestrial biome and its vegetation type.
The same terrestrial biome can occur in geographically distinct areas with similar climates.
Tropical wet forests are found in equatorial regions and are characterized by year-round broadleaf evergreen vegetation.
Tropical wet forests have very stable temperature and sunlight profiles, with temperatures ranging from 20°C to 34°C and consistent daily sunlight of 11–12 hours.
Annual rainfall in tropical wet forests ranges from 125 cm to 660 cm, with high monthly variability but sufficient moisture year-round.
Tropical wet forests have high net primary productivity due to ideal temperatures and precipitation, leading to very high species diversity.
The tropical wet forest biome has distinct horizontal layers, including the forest floor, understory, canopy, and emergent layer, providing diverse habitats.
Epiphytes are plants that grow on other plants in tropical wet forests without harming them.
Savannas are grasslands with scattered trees characterized by hot temperatures and a distinct dry season.
The dry season in savannas limits the growth of forest trees, resulting in grasses and forbs dominating the landscape.
Fire is a key disturbance in savannas, and plants have evolved deep root systems to resprout quickly after fires.
Subtropical deserts are located between 15° and 30° north and south latitude.
Subtropical deserts are characterized by very low annual precipitation, often less than 30 cm, with little monthly variation and unpredictable rainfall.
Subtropical hot deserts experience extreme temperature fluctuations due to the lack of atmospheric water, with very high daytime temperatures and near-freezing nighttime temperatures.
Vegetation in subtropical deserts is adapted to low and unpredictable precipitation, with many plants being annuals or having water-conserving adaptations like deep roots and reduced foliage.
Desert animals exhibit adaptations such as nocturnal behavior and burrowing to cope with the harsh conditions.
The chaparral biome is characterized by annual rainfall between 65 cm and 75 cm, with most precipitation occurring in winter.
Chaparral plants are adapted to periodic fires, with some species producing seeds that germinate only after a hot fire.
Post-fire ashes in the chaparral biome are rich in nutrients like nitrogen, which fertilize the soil and promote plant regrowth.
Temperate grasslands are characterized by pronounced annual temperature fluctuations with hot summers and cold winters.
Annual precipitation in temperate grasslands ranges from 25 cm to 75 cm, which is relatively low and limits tree growth.
The dominant vegetation in temperate grasslands consists of dense grasses that support grazing animals.
Fires caused by lightning are a natural disturbance in temperate grasslands, and their suppression can lead to conversion to scrub or forest.
Controlled burns are often used in temperate grassland management to suppress tree growth and maintain grasses.
Temperate forests are found in mid-latitude regions with temperatures ranging from -30 °C to 30 °C and precipitation between 75 cm and 150 cm annually.
Deciduous trees are the dominant plants in temperate forests, losing their leaves in fall and remaining leafless in winter.
The net primary productivity of temperate forests is less than that of tropical wet forests due to the dormant winter period.
Temperate forests have less diversity of tree species compared to tropical wet forests.
Soils in temperate forests are rich in nutrients due to a thick layer of leaf litter that decays and returns nutrients to the soil.
The boreal forest is characterized by cold, dry winters and short, cool, wet summers.
Cold-tolerant cone-bearing coniferous plants, such as pines, spruce, and fir, predominate in the boreal forest.
Evergreen coniferous trees can photosynthesize earlier in spring than deciduous trees because needle-like leaves require less energy to warm.
Soils in boreal forest regions tend to be acidic with little available nitrogen, giving coniferous trees that retain nitrogen-rich needles a competitive advantage.
The net primary productivity of boreal forests is lower than that of temperate and tropical wet forests.
Boreal forests have high aboveground biomass due to long-lived, slow-growing tree species.
Plant species diversity in boreal forests is less than in temperate and tropical wet forests.
The structure of a boreal forest is often only a tree layer and a ground layer, lacking the pronounced layered structure of tropical wet forests.
The Arctic tundra is characterized by extremely cold temperatures, with average winter temperatures around -34°C and average summer temperatures between 3°C and 12°C.
The Arctic tundra has a very short growing season of approximately 10–12 weeks, during which there is almost 24 hours of daylight allowing for rapid plant growth.
The Arctic tundra receives very low annual precipitation with little variation, and cold temperatures result in little evaporation.
Plants in the Arctic tundra are generally low to the ground, with little species diversity, low net primary productivity, and low aboveground biomass.
The soils of the Arctic tundra may contain permafrost, which prevents deep root penetration and slows organic matter decay, inhibiting nutrient release.

learning_objectives: Describe the effects of abiotic factors on the composition of plant and animal communities in aquatic biomes
Compare and contrast the characteristics of the ocean zones
Summarize the characteristics of standing water and flowing water freshwater biomes

knowledge_units: Abiotic factors such as light, temperature, and water movement influence the composition of aquatic biomes.
Water absorbs light, creating a depth limit for sunlight penetration in aquatic ecosystems.
The ocean is divided into the pelagic realm (open water) and the benthic realm (ocean bottom).
The photic zone is the upper portion of the ocean where light penetrates, while the aphotic zone is deeper and lacks sufficient light for photosynthesis.
The intertidal zone is the area between high and low tide, characterized by variable conditions and organisms adapted to desiccation and wave action.
The neritic zone extends from the intertidal zone to the edge of the continental shelf, where light supports photosynthesis and a productive food chain.
The pelagic or open oceanic zone is beyond the neritic zone, with thermal stratification and scarce nutrients, supporting plankton-based food chains.
The benthic realm is the deep-water region beyond the continental shelf, rich in nutrients from dead organisms and supporting diverse life.
The abyssal zone is the deepest part of the ocean, characterized by cold temperatures, high pressure, and chemosynthetic bacteria at hydrothermal vents.
Coral reefs are ocean ridges formed by marine invertebrates in warm shallow waters, often with mutualistic relationships with photosynthetic algae.
Estuaries are coastal areas where salt water and fresh water mix, forming a unique marine biome.
Coral reefs are built over thousands of years by animals that deposit calcium carbonate.
Climate change and human activity pose dual threats to coral reef survival.
Rising ocean temperatures cause coral bleaching by expelling symbiotic algae.
Increased ocean acidity from dissolved CO2 interferes with coral calcification.
Estuaries are biomes where fresh water meets the ocean, creating brackish water.
Salinity variation in estuaries is a physiological challenge for inhabitants.
Halophytic plants have adaptations like root filters to tolerate salt.
Estuarine animals like mussels and clams exhibit behavioral adaptations to salinity changes.
Freshwater biomes include lakes, ponds, rivers, streams, and wetlands.
Thermal stratification occurs in lakes and ponds during summer.
Nitrogen and phosphorus are limiting nutrients in lakes and ponds, and their excess causes algal blooms.
Algal blooms can lead to oxygen depletion and dead zones.
Rivers and streams vary in abiotic features along their length, such as current speed and sedimentation.
Wetlands are environments with permanently or periodically saturated soil and are characterized by hydrology, hydrophytic vegetation, and hydric soils.

learning_objectives: Define global climate change
Summarize the effects of the Industrial Revolution on global atmospheric carbon dioxide concentration
Describe three natural factors affecting long-term global climate
List two or more greenhouse gases and describe their role in the greenhouse effect

knowledge_units: Global climate change is defined as altered global weather patterns, especially a worldwide increase in temperature and resulting climate changes, due largely to rising atmospheric carbon dioxide levels.
All biomes are universally affected by global conditions such as climate, which shape each biome's environment.
Climate refers to the long-term, predictable atmospheric conditions of a specific area, characterized by consistent seasonal temperature and rainfall ranges.
Weather refers to the conditions of the atmosphere during a short period of time, typically forecasted for 48-hour cycles.
A specific weather event, such as a cold week in June, does not provide evidence of global climate change because it is a short-term occurrence rather than a long-term trend.
Global climate change can be understood by examining evidence of change, drivers of change, and documented results of change.
Antarctic ice cores provide indirect evidence of past climate by trapping air bubbles that reveal temperature and carbon dioxide data.
The Industrial Revolution, beginning around 1750, was powered by fossil fuels, leading to increased atmospheric carbon dioxide.
Milankovitch cycles are natural drivers of climate change caused by slight changes in Earth's orbit over periods of 19,000 to 100,000 years.
Variation in the sun's intensity is a natural factor that directly influences Earth's temperature.
Volcanic eruptions are a natural driver of climate change that can cause short-term cooling through haze-effect cooling.
Greenhouse gases, such as carbon dioxide, methane, water vapor, nitrous oxide, and ozone, trap heat in the atmosphere, driving the greenhouse effect.
The greenhouse effect is the warming of Earth due to greenhouse gases in the atmosphere absorbing and re-radiating thermal energy.
Human activities, primarily the burning of fossil fuels, have rapidly increased atmospheric carbon dioxide concentrations beyond historical levels.
Methane is a greenhouse gas produced by anaerobic decomposition, natural gas fields, and melting ocean clathrates, contributing to a positive feedback loop in global warming.
Global warming has been associated with at least one planet-wide extinction event during the geological past, such as the Permian extinction event.
Modern-day phenomena such as retreating glaciers and melting polar ice cause a continual rise in sea level.
The loss of ice from glaciers and polar ice caps is leading to increases in the global sea level.
Temperature and precipitation play key roles in determining the geographic distribution and phenology of plants and animals.
Changes in climate can negatively affect organisms, such as by causing a mismatched timing between plants and their pollinators.

learning_objectives: Define the term 'invasive species' using Asian carp as an example.
Explain why Asian carp are considered beneficial in their native range but problematic in the United States.
Describe the ecological impact of Asian carp on native species and community structure.

knowledge_units: Asian carp are a group of species including silver, black, grass, and big head carp.
In their native range, Asian carp are an important aquaculture food resource.
In the United States, Asian carp are considered a dangerous invasive species.
Asian carp disrupt community structure and composition, threatening native species.

learning_objectives: Describe how ecologists measure population size and density
Describe three different patterns of population distribution
Use life tables to calculate mortality rates
Describe the three types of survivorship curves and relate them to specific populations

knowledge_units: Populations consist of all individuals of a species living within a specific area and fluctuate due to environmental changes, natural disasters, and competition.
Demography is the statistical study of population dynamics using mathematical tools to investigate how populations respond to biotic and abiotic changes.
Life tables, which detail the life expectancy of individuals within a population, were originally developed by life insurance companies to set insurance rates.
Population size is the total number of individuals in a population, and population density is the number of individuals within a specific area or volume.
Quadrat sampling is used to estimate population size and density for immobile or slow-moving organisms by counting individuals within marked-off square areas.

Mark and recapture is a method used to estimate population size for mobile organisms by marking a sample, releasing them, and then recapturing a second sample to calculate the ratio of marked to unmarked individuals.
The mark and recapture method assumes that the proportion of marked individuals in the second sample reflects the proportion of marked individuals in the entire population.
Limitations of the mark and recapture method include animals learning to avoid capture, animals preferring to be retrapped, and potential harm from marking techniques.
Species dispersion patterns describe the spatial relationship between individuals in a population at a given time.
The three main dispersion patterns are uniform, random, and clumped.
Uniform dispersion occurs when individuals are equally spaced, often due to territorial behavior or allelopathy.
Random dispersion occurs when individuals are spaced unpredictably, as with wind-dispersed seeds.
Clumped dispersion occurs when individuals are grouped together, often due to social behavior or habitat heterogeneity.
Dispersion patterns provide information about species interactions beyond what population density alone can indicate.
Demography is the statistical study of population changes over time, including birth rates, death rates, and life expectancies.
Population size, density, dispersion pattern, sex ratio, and age structure can influence birth and death rates.
A population remains stable when birth and death rates are equal; it increases when birth rates exceed death rates and decreases when death rates exceed birth rates.
Life tables divide a population into age groups and provide data on mortality rates and life expectancy for each interval.
Mortality rate in a life table is calculated as the number of individuals dying during an age interval divided by the number surviving at the beginning of that interval, multiplied by 1000.
Survivorship curves graph the number of individuals surviving at each age interval and are used to compare life histories of different populations.
Type I survivorship curves, seen in humans and most primates, show high survival until old age with most death occurring later in life.
Type II survivorship curves, seen in birds, show a relatively constant mortality rate across all age intervals.
Type III survivorship curves, seen in trees, marine invertebrates, and most fishes, show high mortality early in life with few individuals surviving to old age.

learning_objectives: Describe how life history patterns are influenced by natural selection
Explain different life history patterns and how different reproductive strategies affect species’ survival

knowledge_units: A species' life history describes the series of events over its lifetime, including resource allocation for growth, maintenance, and reproduction.
Life history traits are genetically determined and shaped by the environment and natural selection.
All species have an energy budget that must balance energy intake with use for metabolism, reproduction, parental care, and energy storage.
Fecundity is the potential reproductive capacity of an individual, often inversely related to the amount of parental care provided.
Species with high fecundity typically produce many offspring with little parental care, while species with low fecundity produce fewer offspring with extensive parental care.
Plants with low fecundity produce few energy-rich seeds, whereas plants with high fecundity produce many small, energy-poor seeds.
Organisms that reproduce at an early age have a greater chance of producing offspring but may sacrifice growth and health maintenance.
Organisms that reproduce later in life may have greater fecundity or better parental care but risk not surviving to reproductive age.
Semelparity is a life history strategy where a species reproduces only once and then dies, using most resources in a single reproductive event.
Iteroparity is a life history strategy where a species reproduces multiple times during its lifetime.
Reproduction has a cost in terms of lifespan for male fruit flies, as mating reduces their longevity.
Males that do not mate live longer, allowing more future mating opportunities.
Larger female fruit flies have greater fecundity, producing twice as many offspring per mating as smaller females.
Resource-depleted males with limited sperm supplies preferentially mate with larger, more fecund females to maximize reproductive success.
The physiological state of an organism can influence its mating behavior to optimize the use of limited reproductive resources.
Energy expended on reproduction may reduce lifespan, but this is not evolutionarily significant if reproduction has already occurred.
Behavioral changes in response to resource depletion are important for maximizing gene transmission in natural selection.

learning_objectives: Explain the characteristics of and differences between exponential and logistic growth patterns
Give examples of exponential and logistic growth in natural populations
Describe how natural selection and environmental adaptation led to the evolution of particular life history patterns

knowledge_units: Population ecologists use mathematical models to describe and predict changes in population dynamics.
Some long-accepted population models are being modified or abandoned due to their lack of predictive ability.
Exponential growth is an accelerating pattern of increasing population size when resources are unlimited.
Bacteria reproduce by prokaryotic fission, and under unlimited nutrients, their population doubles each generation, producing a J-shaped growth curve.
The population growth rate is calculated as the birth rate minus the death rate.
The intrinsic rate of increase (r) represents the difference between per capita birth and death rates and can be positive, negative, or zero.
A positive r indicates population growth, a negative r indicates decline, and a zero r indicates zero population growth.
Logistic growth occurs when population growth slows and levels off as resources become limited, reaching a maximum population size called carrying capacity (K).
The logistic growth model incorporates carrying capacity to moderate growth rate, with the term (K-N)/K representing the fraction of carrying capacity available for further growth.
When population size (N) is small relative to carrying capacity, growth is approximately exponential; when N approaches K, growth slows or stops.
Intraspecific competition intensifies as population size increases, limiting resources and contributing to the logistic growth pattern.
Examples of logistic growth include yeast in a test tube and wild populations like sheep and harbor seals, which oscillate around carrying capacity.

learning_objectives: Give examples of how the carrying capacity of a habitat may change
Compare and contrast density-dependent growth regulation and density-independent growth regulation, giving examples
Give examples of exponential and logistic growth in wild animal populations
Describe how natural selection and environmental adaptation lead to the evolution of particular life history patterns

knowledge_units: The logistic model assumes a constant carrying capacity, but in reality, carrying capacity varies due to seasonal changes and natural events.
Populations engage in interspecific competition, sharing resources with other species, which affects their growth.
Density-dependent factors are biological and their effect on population growth depends on population density.
Density-independent factors are typically physical or chemical and affect mortality regardless of population density.
In density-dependent regulation, higher population density usually leads to higher mortality and lower reproductive rates.
An example of density-dependent regulation is the giant intestinal roundworm, where denser populations exhibit lower fecundity.
Density-dependent and density-independent factors can interact, influencing population recovery after environmental disturbances.
Climate change and human hunting are commonly thought to have contributed to the extinction of woolly mammoths.
K-selected species are adapted to stable, predictable environments and tend to have few, large offspring with long-term parental care.
R-selected species are adapted to unpredictable or changing environments and tend to have many, small offspring with little parental care.
K-selected species typically mature late, have greater longevity, and experience increased competition.
R-selected species typically mature early, have lower longevity, and experience decreased competition.
The r- and K-selection theory has been reconsidered and modified because many species did not fit its predictions and it ignored age-specific mortality.
Modern demographic-based models of life history evolution incorporate age structure and mortality factors along with ecological concepts from r- and K-selection theory.

learning_objectives: Discuss exponential human population growth
Explain how humans have expanded the carrying capacity of their habitat
Relate population growth and age structure to the level of economic development in different countries
Discuss the long-term implications of unchecked human population growth

knowledge_units: Earth's human population is growing rapidly, raising concerns about environmental sustainability.
Long-term exponential growth carries potential risks of famine, disease, and large-scale death.
Humans have increased the carrying capacity of their environment through technology, but this has caused unprecedented environmental changes.
Human activities such as ozone depletion, acid rain erosion, and global climate change may reduce Earth's carrying capacity for humans.
Human population is currently experiencing exponential growth even though reproduction is far below its biotic potential.
A consequence of exponential human population growth is a reduction in the time needed to add a particular number of humans to the Earth.
Humans can consciously alter their environment to increase carrying capacity, which helps overcome density-dependent growth regulation.
Human intelligence, society, and communication enable the development and transmission of technologies like agriculture and shelter construction.
Migration has allowed humans to inhabit nearly all inhabitable land on Earth.
Public health measures, sanitation, antibiotics, and vaccines have reduced the limiting effect of infectious diseases on human population growth.
Age structure is the proportion of a population at different age ranges and allows prediction of population growth.
Countries with rapid population growth typically have a pyramidal age structure with a preponderance of younger individuals.
A pyramidal age structure with many young individuals is most often observed in underdeveloped countries.
Developed countries with slow or zero population growth have age structures with a greater proportion of older individuals.
The highest population growth rates tend to occur in less economically developed countries.
Unchecked human population growth cannot continue indefinitely due to the laws of exponential growth.
Some nations have implemented policies to control population growth, such as China's one-child policy.
Other nations have made efforts to increase population growth after birth rates dipped.
Population growth contributes to the endangerment of the natural environment.
Human activity's role in causing climate change is a debated socio-political issue in some countries.

learning_objectives: Discuss the predator-prey cycle
Give examples of defenses against predation and herbivory
Describe the competitive exclusion principle
Give examples of symbiotic relationships between species
Describe community structure and succession

knowledge_units: All populations occupying the same habitat form a community.
Species diversity is the number of species in a habitat and their relative abundance.
Ecology is studied at the community level to understand how species interact and compete for resources.
Predation involves the consumption of prey by a predator, and their populations often cycle in relation to each other.
The lynx and snowshoe hare populations exhibit a classic predator-prey cycle lasting approximately 10 years, with predator numbers lagging behind prey numbers.
Herbivory is the consumption of plants by animals, and some plants have evolved defenses against it while others benefit from seed distribution through herbivory.
Species have evolved mechanical defenses, such as thorns or hard shells, to physically deter predators and herbivores.
Chemical defenses, like toxins produced by plants or animals, deter predation and herbivory.
Camouflage allows species to avoid detection by blending in with their surroundings.
Aposematic coloration uses bright colors to warn predators of unpleasant taste, toxins, or defensive abilities.
In Batesian mimicry, a harmless species mimics the warning coloration of a harmful species to avoid predation.
In Müllerian mimicry, multiple defended species share similar warning coloration.
In Emsleyan/Mertensian mimicry, a deadly prey species mimics a less dangerous one to protect both from predators.
The competitive exclusion principle states that two species cannot occupy the same niche in a habitat.
Resource partitioning allows species to coexist by evolving to use different resources, areas, or feeding times, thereby occupying different microniches.
Symbiotic relationships are close, prolonged interactions between different species that affect the abundance and distribution of their populations.
In commensalism, one species benefits while the other is neither harmed nor benefited.
In mutualism, both species benefit from their interaction.
In parasitism, the parasite benefits while the host is harmed.
Parasites typically do not kill their hosts quickly to allow time for reproduction and transmission.
Some parasites have complex life cycles requiring multiple host species to complete their reproduction.
Communities are characterized by their structure (types and numbers of species) and dynamics (changes over time).
Foundation species are primary producers that have the greatest influence on community structure.
Foundation species may physically modify the environment to create and maintain habitats for other organisms.
Biodiversity is measured by species richness (number of species) and relative species abundance (evenness).
Species richness is highest near the equator and decreases toward the poles due to factors like temperature and rainfall.
Relative species abundance is the number of individuals in a species relative to the total individuals in all species.
A keystone species is one whose presence is critical for maintaining biodiversity and community structure.

learning_objectives: Compare innate and learned behavior
Discuss how movement and migration behaviors are a result of natural selection
Discuss the different ways members of a population communicate with each other
Give examples of how species use energy for mating displays and other courtship behaviors
Differentiate between various mating systems
Describe different ways that species learn

knowledge_units: Behavior is the change in activity of an organism in response to a stimulus.
Behavioral biology is the study of the biological and evolutionary bases for behavioral changes.
Innate behaviors have a strong genetic component and are largely independent of environmental influences.
Learned behaviors result from environmental conditioning and are flexible and dynamic.
Innate behaviors are involuntary responses to stimuli, such as reflex actions.
Kinesis is undirected movement in response to a stimulus, like woodlice increasing speed in unfavorable temperatures.
Taxis is directed movement toward or away from a stimulus, such as Tetrahymena thermophila moving toward a chemical source.
A fixed action pattern is a series of movements that, once initiated by a stimulus, continues to completion even if the stimulus is removed.
Migration is long-range seasonal movement of animals, evolved as a response to variation in resource availability.
Migration can be obligate, facultative, or incomplete, depending on whether animals always, sometimes, or partially migrate.
Foraging is the search for and exploitation of food resources, with optimal foraging behaviors favored by natural selection.
Animals communicate using signals such as visual, chemical, aural, tactile, or pheromone-based stimuli.
Pheromones are chemical signals secreted to elicit specific behaviors from other individuals of the same species.
Courtship displays are ritualized visual behaviors designed to attract and convince a member of the opposite sex to mate.
Aggressive displays communicate willingness and ability to fight, often reducing actual fighting by allowing assessment of opponents.
Distraction displays are altruistic behaviors that attract predators away from nests, benefiting offspring at the individual's risk.
Altruistic behaviors lower the fitness of the individual but increase the fitness of another individual.
Kin selection explains altruistic behaviors toward relatives by increasing inclusive fitness through shared genes.
Reciprocal altruism involves unrelated individuals helping each other with the expectation of future reciprocation.
Intersexual selection involves individuals of one sex choosing mates based on traits like visual, aural, or chemical cues.
Intrasexual selection involves competition for mates within the same sex, often through displays or aggressive rituals.
Monogamous mating systems involve one male and one female paired for at least one breeding season.
Polygynous mating systems involve one male mating with multiple females, with females typically providing most parental care.
Polyandrous mating systems involve one female mating with multiple males, with males often providing parental care.
Learned behaviors allow an organism to adapt to changes in the environment and are modified by previous experiences.
Habituation is a simple form of non-associative learning in which an animal stops responding to a stimulus after repeated exposure without punishment or reward.
Imprinting is a type of non-associative learning that occurs at a particular life stage, is rapid, and is independent of the species involved.
Imprinting encourages young animals to stay near a caregiver, increasing their chances of survival.
Conditioned behaviors are a type of associative learning where a stimulus becomes associated with a consequence.
In classical conditioning, a conditioned response becomes associated with a conditioned stimulus that was previously unrelated to it.
Classical conditioning can occur after a single pairing of stimuli, not necessarily requiring multiple exposures.
In operant conditioning, a behavior is modified by its consequences through positive or negative reinforcement.
Operant conditioning is the basis for most animal training, where animals learn to associate behaviors with rewards or punishments.
Cognitive learning involves the manipulation of information using the mind and is the most prominent method of human learning.
Cognitive learning includes learning by imitating others, taking instructions, and using sensory inputs to form mental representations.
Wolfgang Köhler demonstrated that chimpanzees are capable of abstract thought through cognitive learning by solving puzzles.
H. C. Blodgett's maze experiments with rats showed that cognitive learning can occur in simple mammals, not just primates.
E. C. Tolman proved that rats form a cognitive map in their minds, demonstrating that cognitive learning is distinct from conditioning.
Sociobiology is the extension of population biology and evolutionary theory to social organization.
Sociobiology proposes that animal and human behavior can be explained almost solely in terms of genetics and natural selection.
Sociobiology is controversial because it is criticized for ignoring environmental effects on behavior, reflecting the nature versus nurture debate.
Sociobiology links genes with behaviors and is associated with biological determinism, the belief that all behaviors are hardwired into our genes.

learning_objectives: Explain how the 1993 hantavirus outbreak in the southwestern United States illustrates the importance of understanding ecosystem dynamics.
Describe the role of the Centers for Disease Control (CDC) in investigating and managing the hantavirus outbreak.
Discuss how traditional ecological knowledge, such as that of Navajo healers, contributed to understanding the connection between environmental changes and disease emergence.
Analyze the relationship between rainfall, mouse populations, and the spread of the Sin Nombre virus as an example of ecosystem response to environmental change.

knowledge_units: The 1993 hantavirus outbreak in the southwestern United States was a rare lung disease with a high fatality rate that affected previously healthy young adults.
The Centers for Disease Control (CDC) is the United States government agency responsible for investigating and managing potential epidemics, and it was brought in to investigate the 1993 outbreak.
Navajo healers had traditional ecological knowledge that connected rainfall patterns to mouse population changes, which could have predicted the 1993 outbreak.
The cause of the 1993 outbreak was the hantavirus Sin Nombre, and insights from traditional Navajo medicine helped scientists characterize the disease and implement health measures.
The 1993 hantavirus outbreak illustrates the importance of understanding ecosystem dynamics and how ecosystems respond to environmental changes.

learning_objectives: Describe the basic ecosystem types
Explain the methods that ecologists use to study ecosystem structure and dynamics
Identify the different methods of ecosystem modeling
Differentiate between food chains and food webs and recognize the importance of each

knowledge_units: Competition for limited resources is a key characteristic of ecosystems and natural selection.
Ecosystems are categorized into three broad types: freshwater, ocean water, and terrestrial.
Ocean ecosystems are the most common, comprising over 70 percent of Earth's surface and include shallow ocean, deep ocean water, and deep ocean surface types.
Freshwater ecosystems are the rarest, occurring on only 1.8 percent of Earth's surface and include lakes, rivers, streams, and springs.
Terrestrial ecosystems are grouped into biomes such as tropical rain forests, savannas, deserts, coniferous forests, deciduous forests, and tundra.
Ecosystems are complex and subject to disturbances from natural processes and human activities.
Equilibrium is the steady state of an ecosystem where organisms are in balance with their environment.
Resistance is the ability of an ecosystem to remain at equilibrium despite disturbances.
Resilience is the speed at which an ecosystem recovers equilibrium after being disturbed.
A food chain is a linear sequence of organisms through which nutrients and energy pass, with each organism occupying a trophic level.
Energy loss between trophic levels limits the length of food chains due to the second law of thermodynamics.
A food web is a holistic, nonlinear model that more accurately represents ecosystem structure and dynamics than a food chain.
Grazing food webs have photosynthetic organisms at their base, while detrital food webs are based on decomposers feeding on decaying organic matter.
The evolution of species within an ecosystem can alter the ecosystem environment, as shown by the three-spined stickleback study.
Ecosystem dynamics is the study of changes in ecosystem structure caused by environmental disturbances or internal forces.
Ecologists study ecosystems using controlled experimental systems, natural state studies, or both approaches.
A holistic ecosystem model attempts to quantify the composition, interaction, and dynamics of entire ecosystems, with a food web as an example.
Mesocosms are partitioned parts of natural ecosystems used for experiments, while microcosms are recreated ecosystems in laboratory environments.
Experimental systems like mesocosms and microcosms have limitations because altering natural ecosystems may change their dynamics.
Scientists use three basic types of ecosystem modeling: conceptual, analytical, and simulation models.
A conceptual model uses flow charts to show interactions of living and nonliving components and how disturbances affect ecosystems.
An analytical model uses simple mathematical formulas to predict effects of environmental disturbances on ecosystems.
A simulation model uses complex computer algorithms to holistically model ecosystems and predict effects of disturbances.
Conceptual models are useful for describing ecosystem structure and dynamics and showing relationships between organisms and their environment.
Analytical models have limited accuracy due to simplification of complex ecosystems, while simulation models better handle ecosystem complexities.

learning_objectives: Describe how organisms acquire energy in a food web and in associated food chains
Explain how the efficiency of energy transfers between trophic levels affects ecosystem structure and dynamics
Discuss trophic levels and how ecological pyramids are used to model them

knowledge_units: All living organisms require energy to drive metabolic pathways, such as building macromolecules from monomers.
Food webs illustrate the directional flow of energy through ecosystems, showing how energy is acquired, used, and transferred between organisms.
Energy is acquired by living organisms through photosynthesis, chemosynthesis, or consumption of other organisms.
Autotrophs are organisms that synthesize their own food and are critical for providing energy to ecosystems.
Photoautotrophs use sunlight to convert solar energy into chemical energy stored in ATP and organic molecules like glucose.
Chemoautotrophs use inorganic molecules, such as hydrogen sulfide, as an energy source in ecosystems without sunlight.
Productivity within an ecosystem is the percentage of energy entering the ecosystem that is incorporated into biomass in a particular trophic level.
Biomass is the total mass of living or previously living organisms within a trophic level per unit area at the time of measurement.
Gross primary productivity is the rate at which photosynthetic primary producers incorporate energy from the sun.
Net primary productivity is the energy remaining in primary producers after accounting for their respiration and heat loss, which is then available to primary consumers.
Energy is lost as metabolic heat during transfers between trophic levels due to the second law of thermodynamics.
The low efficiency of energy transfer between trophic levels limits the length of food chains in a food web.
Net consumer productivity (NPE) measures how efficiently a trophic level converts ingested energy into biomass for the next level.
Ectotherms generally have higher NPE than endotherms because endotherms use more energy for respiration and heat.
The low NPE of endotherms like cattle makes meat production energetically costly compared to plant-based foods.
Ecological pyramids visualize the relative amounts of parameters such as number of organisms, energy, and biomass across trophic levels.
Pyramids of numbers can be upright or inverted depending on the ecosystem, as seen in grasslands versus temperate forests.
Pyramids of biomass measure the amount of energy converted into living tissue at different trophic levels and can be upright or inverted.
An inverted pyramid of biomass, such as in the English Channel, results from a high turnover rate of primary producers like phytoplankton.
Pyramids of energy are always upright and represent the flow of energy through trophic levels in an ecosystem.
Pyramids of energy are the most consistent and representative models for studying energy flow through ecosystems.
Biomagnification is the increasing concentration of persistent, toxic substances in organisms at each trophic level, from primary producers to apex consumers.
DDT is an example of a persistent toxic substance that biomagnifies, causing adverse effects like eggshell fragility in birds.
PCBs and heavy metals like mercury also biomagnify, with concentrations increasing through trophic levels in aquatic ecosystems.
Biomagnification affects human health, as seen in EPA recommendations for pregnant women and young children to avoid high-mercury fish.

learning_objectives: Discuss the biogeochemical cycles of water, carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur
Explain how human activities have impacted these cycles and the potential consequences for Earth

knowledge_units: Matter is conserved and recycled through ecosystems, unlike energy which flows directionally.
The recycling of inorganic matter between living organisms and their environment is called a biogeochemical cycle.
The six most common elements in organic molecules—carbon, nitrogen, hydrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and sulfur—participate in biogeochemical cycles.
Geologic processes such as weathering, erosion, and plate subduction play a role in recycling materials.
Water is essential to all living processes and cycles through the hydrosphere in liquid, solid, and vapor forms.
Carbon is found in all organic macromolecules and is a constituent of fossil fuels.
Nitrogen is a major component of nucleic acids and proteins and is critical to agriculture.
Phosphorus is a major component of nucleic acids and a key ingredient in artificial fertilizers.
Sulfur is critical to the 3-D folding of proteins through disulfide binding.
The cycling of elements is interconnected, as water movement facilitates the leaching of nitrogen and phosphate into water bodies.
Water is the basis of all living processes on Earth.
Less than 1 percent of Earth's fresh water is easily accessible from lakes and rivers.
Many organisms depend on fresh surface water, and its scarcity can significantly affect ecosystem dynamics.
Water cycling is extremely important to ecosystem dynamics and has a major influence on climate.
Residence time is a measure of the average time a water molecule stays in a particular reservoir.
The water cycle is driven by the sun's energy, which causes evaporation and sublimation of water into the atmosphere.
Water vapor condenses into clouds and returns to Earth's surface as precipitation.
Precipitation can infiltrate the ground, flow as subsurface water, or become surface runoff that flows to oceans.
Carbon is the second most abundant element in living organisms and is present in all organic molecules.
The carbon cycle consists of two interconnected sub-cycles: a rapid biological cycle among living organisms and a long-term geologic cycle.
In the biological carbon cycle, autotrophs use atmospheric carbon dioxide to build high-energy compounds like glucose, releasing oxygen as a by-product.
Heterotrophs acquire carbon compounds by consuming autotrophs and break them down through respiration, requiring oxygen and releasing carbon dioxide.
The biogeochemical carbon cycle involves the slow movement of carbon through reservoirs such as the atmosphere, oceans, soil, and fossil fuels.
Carbon dioxide from the atmosphere dissolves in water to form carbonic acid, which ionizes to carbonate and bicarbonate ions, with most ocean carbon as bicarbonate ions.
Calcium carbonate from marine organisms forms limestone, which is the largest carbon reservoir on Earth.
Fossil fuels are nonrenewable carbon reservoirs formed from anaerobically decomposed plant remains over millions of years.
Volcanic eruptions and geothermal systems release carbon dioxide from carbon sediments into the atmosphere.
Human activities, such as burning fossil fuels and animal husbandry, have significantly increased atmospheric carbon dioxide levels since the Industrial Revolution.
Nitrogen enters the living world through nitrogen fixation, a process performed by free-living and symbiotic bacteria that convert atmospheric N₂ into organic nitrogen.
Cyanobacteria in aquatic ecosystems and Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules are key nitrogen fixers.
Organic nitrogen is a limiting nutrient for ecosystem processes like primary production and decomposition.
Nitrogen is returned to the atmosphere through a three-step process: ammonification, nitrification, and denitrification.
Ammonification converts nitrogenous waste into ammonium (NH₄⁺) by bacteria and fungi.
Nitrification converts ammonium to nitrites (NO₂⁻) and then to nitrates (NO₃⁻) by nitrifying bacteria.
Denitrification converts nitrates back into nitrogen gas (N₂) by bacteria, completing the cycle.
Phosphorus is an essential nutrient that is a major component of nucleic acids, phospholipids, and bones.
Phosphorus is often the limiting nutrient in aquatic ecosystems.
Phosphorus enters ecosystems through natural weathering of phosphate-containing rock and through human activities like fertilizer runoff.
Phosphate-containing ocean sediments originate from the bodies and excretions of ocean organisms and are moved to land by geological uplifting.
The movement of phosphate from the ocean to land is extremely slow, with an oceanic residence time of 20,000 to 100,000 years.
Excess phosphorus from fertilizer runoff and sewage causes eutrophication, leading to dead zones in aquatic ecosystems.
Dead zones are areas depleted of normal flora and fauna due to human activities like eutrophication, and their number has been increasing.

learning_objectives: Describe the geographic and ecological significance of Lake Victoria in the context of evolutionary biology.
Explain how cichlid fish in Lake Victoria demonstrate rapid adaptive radiation and speciation.
Analyze the impact of invasive species, such as the Nile Perch, on native biodiversity and extinction rates.

knowledge_units: Lake Victoria is an enormous and deep lake located in the Great Rift Valley in Africa.
Cichlid fish in Lake Victoria have evolved into over 500 distinct species, demonstrating rapid adaptive radiation.
The invasive Nile Perch has caused the extinction of hundreds of cichlid species in Lake Victoria.

learning_objectives: Define biodiversity in terms of species diversity and abundance
Describe biodiversity as the equilibrium of naturally fluctuating rates of extinction and speciation
Identify historical causes of high extinction rates in Earth’s history

knowledge_units: Biodiversity is traditionally measured by considering both the number of species and their relative abundance.
Adaptive radiation is a rapid branching of a phylogenetic clade into many closely related species that occupy different habitats and niches.
The introduction of the Nile perch to Lake Victoria contributed to the extinction of many cichlid species.
Human activities such as habitat destruction, introduction of exotic species, and overharvesting are major drivers of current species loss.
The current rate of species loss is comparable to past mass extinction events, but is primarily caused by human activity rather than cataclysmic natural events.
Biodiversity describes the number and kinds of species and their abundance in a given location or on the planet.
Genetic diversity defines the raw material for evolution and adaptation in a species.
A species' future potential for adaptation depends on the genetic diversity held in the genomes of its individuals.
Chemical diversity, a measure of genetic diversity, has potential benefit for humans as a source of pharmaceuticals.
Ecosystem diversity refers to the number of different ecosystems on the planet or within a given geographic area.
The loss of an ecosystem means the loss of interactions between species, unique coadaptation features, and biological productivity.
Endemic species are found in only one location and are particularly vulnerable to extinction.
Biogeographers suggest that latitude and age are hypotheses to explain biodiversity patterns on the planet.

learning_objectives: Identify chemical diversity benefits to humans
Identify biodiversity components that support human agriculture
Describe ecosystem services

knowledge_units: Accelerated biodiversity loss can lead to ecosystem collapse and increased costs for maintaining food production, clean air and water, and human health.
Agriculture began when early human societies settled and modified their environment, making it difficult to recognize dependence on undomesticated species.
The human species is embedded in and dependent on ecosystems, similar to all other species, despite technological advancements.
Humans have used plants for medicinal purposes for thousands of years.
Most plants produce secondary compounds that can function as medications.
Many modern pharmaceuticals, such as aspirin and antibiotics, are derived from plant, fungal, or bacterial compounds.
Animal venoms and poisons are being researched and developed into medicines for various diseases.
The discovery of new natural compounds for medicine is directly linked to biodiversity, as species loss reduces pharmaceutical opportunities.
Crop diversity arises from human breeding and selection to match diverse cultural and environmental demands.
Disease resistance is a chief benefit of crop biodiversity, and low diversity increases vulnerability to crop failure.
Wild species related to domesticated crops are essential sources of genetic diversity for crop improvement.
Seed banks, such as the Svalbard Global Seed Vault, help preserve crop diversity against loss.
Soil organisms provide ecosystem services by maintaining nutrient cycles and soil structure necessary for crop growth.
Plant pollination by species like bees is a key ecosystem service that supports agricultural productivity.
Natural enemies of pests, such as predators and parasites, contribute to pest control and are enhanced by landscape diversity.
Humans obtain food resources from wild populations, primarily fish populations.
For approximately 1 billion people worldwide, aquatic resources provide the main source of animal protein.
Fishery extinctions rarely lead to complete extinction of the harvested species but cause radical restructuring of the marine ecosystem.
The collapse of fisheries has dramatic and long-lasting effects on local populations that work in the fishery.
The loss of an inexpensive protein source increases the cost of living for populations that cannot afford to replace it.
Humans benefit psychologically from living in a biodiverse world.
Human evolutionary history has adapted humans to live in natural environments, and city environments can generate psychological stressors affecting health and well-being.
Research suggests natural landscapes have psychological regenerative benefits.
There is a moral argument that humans have a responsibility to inflict as little harm as possible on other species.

learning_objectives: Identify significant threats to biodiversity
Explain the effects of habitat loss, the introduction of exotic species, and hunting on biodiversity
Identify the early and predicted effects of climate change on biodiversity

knowledge_units: The core threat to biodiversity is the combination of human population growth and resource exploitation.
The three greatest proximate threats to biodiversity are habitat loss, overharvesting, and the introduction of exotic species.
Habitat loss and overharvesting are direct results of human population growth and resource use.
The introduction of exotic species results from increased mobility and trade.
Anthropogenic climate change is predicted to become a significant threat to biodiversity during this century.
Habitat loss occurs when humans eliminate ecosystems such as forests, deserts, grasslands, freshwater estuaries, or marine environments.
Species become extinct if their entire habitat within their range is removed.
Human destruction of habitats accelerated in the latter half of the twentieth century.
Forests are removed for timber and to plant palm oil plantations, which are used in food products, cosmetics, and biodiesel.
In the tropics, habitat loss leads to species extinction due to high levels of endemism, where species are unique to a defined geographic location.

learning_objectives: Identify new technologies and methods for describing biodiversity
Explain the legislative framework for conservation
Describe principles and challenges of conservation preserve design
Identify examples of the effects of habitat restoration
Discuss the role of zoos in biodiversity conservation

knowledge_units: Preserving biodiversity requires greater understanding of biodiversity itself.
Preserving biodiversity requires changes in human behavior and beliefs.
Preserving biodiversity requires various preservation strategies.
DNA barcoding uses rapid evolution in a mitochondrial gene (cytochrome c oxidase 1) to identify eukaryotic species, except for plants which use chloroplast genes.
Rapid mass sequencing machines and computer resources make DNA barcoding relatively inexpensive and quick for cataloging species.
DNA barcoding has identified significant numbers of undescribed species that were previously unrecognized as different from sibling species.
Computer databases provide information on named species and a framework for adding new species, but at current rates, cataloging all species would take about 500 years.
The IUCN maintains the Red List, an online listing of endangered species categorized by taxonomy, threat type, and other criteria, supported by scientific research.
The Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES) provides a legal framework to prevent listed species from being transported across national borders.
The Endangered Species Act (ESA) requires the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service to develop plans to protect listed species and restore them to sustainable numbers.
The Migratory Bird Treaty Act (MBTA) makes it illegal to disturb or kill over 800 protected bird species.
The Nature Conservancy purchases land to establish preserves for ecosystems as a conservation approach.
The Paris Climate Agreement aims to halt global temperature rise, which could benefit species negatively affected by climate change.
A preserve is an area of land set aside with varying degrees of protection for the organisms within its boundaries.
Preserves can be effective in the short term for protecting species and ecosystems but face challenges for long-term viability.
Determining how much land or marine habitat should be protected is challenging due to protected land allocation and biodiversity distribution.
The IUCN World Parks Congress estimated that 11.5 percent of Earth’s land surface was covered by preserves in 2003.
High-quality preserves include only about 50 percent of threatened amphibian species, indicating unequal representation.
The fundamental principle behind preserve design is based on the theory of island biogeography by MacArthur and Wilson.
Biodiversity on an island is a function of species migration, speciation, and extinction rates.
Islands farther from a mainland have lower migration rates and lower equilibrium species numbers.
Smaller islands have lower immigration rates, fewer niches for speciation, and higher extinction probabilities.
Conservation preserves can be viewed as 'islands' of habitat within 'an ocean' of non-habitat.
A preserve must be large enough to support a species, with critical size depending on the species' home range.
Larger preserves have more core habitat, more niches, and attract more species due to easier discovery and access.
Preserves perform better with buffer zones of suboptimal habitat around them.
One large preserve is better than several smaller ones of the same total area because it has more core habitat unaffected by edges.
Preserves shaped like a square or circle are better than those with many thin 'arms'.
Wildlife corridors between smaller preserves allow movement of individuals and genes, making them function more like a large preserve.
Policy, legislative, and enforcement specifications related to preserve uses can include timber extraction, regulated hunting, and human recreation.
Political pressures often influence policy decisions more than conservation considerations.
Strict wildlife protection policies have sometimes forced indigenous populations from ancestral lands within preserves.
Lack of enforcement can render preserve protections meaningless against illegal poaching and resource extraction.
Political and economic pressures typically make preserves smaller, making it difficult to set aside sufficiently large areas.
Climate change will cause problems with preserve locations as species migrate to higher latitudes.
Scientists are planning for global warming effects on preserves by predicting the need for new preserves.
Habitat restoration can improve biodiversity in degraded ecosystems but cannot restore extinct species.
Reintroducing wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995 led to ecosystem changes that increased biodiversity.
Wolves suppress elk and coyote populations, providing more resources for carrion eaters and allowing revegetation of riparian areas.
A keystone species is instrumental in maintaining diversity in an ecosystem.
Removing a keystone species may cause a collapse in diversity, while restoring one can restore biodiversity.
Dam removal restores naturally fluctuating water levels, leading to increased fish diversity and improved water quality.
In the Pacific Northwest, dam removal is expected to increase salmon populations, a keystone species that transports nutrients to inland ecosystems.
Dam removal has allowed the return of spawning anadromous fish species in regions like the Atlantic coast.
Zoos play a role in conservation through captive breeding programs and education.
Captive breeding programs for endangered species are generally inefficient and often prone to failure upon reintroduction to the wild.
Some captive breeding programs have succeeded, such as the American condor reintroduction and Whooping Crane reestablishment.
Zoo facilities are too limited for captive breeding programs for the number of species at risk.
Education is a potential positive impact of zoos on conservation, especially with reduced human-wildlife contact due to urbanization.

learning_objectives: Explain the historical significance of early mathematical artifacts like the Lebombo and Ishango Bones in tracking numerical information.
Identify the different sets of numbers used in algebra and their relationships.
Apply properties of operations to manipulate numbers in algebraic contexts.
Describe how prerequisite knowledge of number sets and operations supports further study in algebra.

knowledge_units: The Lebombo Bone and Ishango Bone are early mathematical artifacts that may have been used to track numerical information such as time or moon cycles.
Understanding relationships among numbers is necessary for performing calculations and making predictions.
This chapter reviews sets of numbers and properties of operations used to manipulate numbers, which serve as prerequisite knowledge for algebra.

learning_objectives: Classify a real number as a natural, whole, integer, rational, or irrational number.
Perform calculations using order of operations.
Use the following properties of real numbers: commutative, associative, distributive, inverse, and identity.
Evaluate algebraic expressions.
Simplify algebraic expressions.

knowledge_units: The number system evolved to include fractions, zero, and negative numbers to represent division, nothing, and loss or debt respectively.
Real numbers can be classified into categories such as natural, whole, integer, rational, and irrational numbers.
The properties of real numbers, including commutative, associative, distributive, inverse, and identity, are used in calculations and expressions.
Order of operations is a set of rules for performing calculations with real numbers.
Algebraic expressions can be evaluated and simplified using the properties of real numbers and order of operations.
Natural numbers are the set {1, 2, 3, ...} used for counting.
Whole numbers are the set of natural numbers plus zero: {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}.
Integers are the set {..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...} consisting of negative integers, zero, and positive integers.
Rational numbers are numbers that can be expressed as a fraction m/n where m and n are integers and n ≠ 0.
Every natural number, whole number, and integer is a rational number with a denominator of 1.
Rational numbers can be expressed as either terminating decimals or repeating decimals.
Any integer can be written as a rational number by placing it over 1 in a fraction.
The integer 7 can be expressed as the rational number 7/1.
The integer 0 can be expressed as the rational number 0/1.
The integer -8 can be expressed as the rational number -8/1.
A rational number can be expressed as a fraction and written as either a terminating or repeating decimal.
A terminating decimal is a decimal that ends after a finite number of digits.
A repeating decimal is a decimal in which a sequence of digits repeats indefinitely.
Irrational numbers cannot be written as fractions of two integers.
A number is irrational if it is not a rational number.
A rational number can be expressed as a fraction of two integers.
A rational number has a decimal representation that is either terminating or repeating.
An irrational number cannot be expressed as a fraction of two integers.
An irrational number has a decimal representation that is non-terminating and non-repeating.
The square root of a perfect square is a rational number.
The square root of a non-perfect square is an irrational number.
Every real number is either rational or irrational, but not both.
The set of real numbers consists of the union of rational and irrational numbers.
Real numbers can be classified into three subsets: negative real numbers, zero, and positive real numbers.
Zero is neither positive nor negative.
Each real number corresponds to a unique point on the number line, and each point on the number line corresponds to exactly one real number, establishing a one-to-one correspondence.
A negative number lies to the left of 0 on the number line.
A positive number lies to the right of 0 on the number line.
A rational number can be expressed as a fraction or a terminating/repeating decimal.
An irrational number cannot be expressed as a fraction and has a non-terminating, non-repeating decimal expansion.
The square root of a perfect square is a rational number.
A repeating decimal is a rational number.
The set of natural numbers is a subset of the set of whole numbers.
The set of whole numbers is a subset of the set of integers.
The set of integers is a subset of the set of rational numbers.
Rational numbers are numbers that can be written as a fraction m/n where n is not zero.
The set of irrational numbers is outside the set of rational numbers and includes numbers like pi and the square root of two.
All of these sets together make up the set of real numbers.
The set of natural numbers includes the numbers used for counting: {1, 2, 3, ...}.
The set of whole numbers is the set of natural numbers plus zero: {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}.
The set of integers adds the negative natural numbers to the set of whole numbers: {..., -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...}.
The set of rational numbers includes fractions written as {m/n | m and n are integers and n ≠ 0}.
The set of irrational numbers is the set of numbers that are not rational, are nonrepeating, and are nonterminating.
Natural numbers are the set of positive integers starting from 1.
Whole numbers include all natural numbers and zero.
Integers include all whole numbers and their negative counterparts.
Rational numbers can be expressed as a fraction of two integers where the denominator is not zero.
Irrational numbers cannot be expressed as a simple fraction and have non-repeating, non-terminating decimal expansions.
The order of operations is a sequence of rules for evaluating mathematical expressions to ensure consistent results.
Grouping symbols such as parentheses, brackets, braces, fraction bars, radicals, and absolute value bars indicate that operations within them should be performed first.
After simplifying expressions within grouping symbols, exponents and radicals are evaluated next.
Multiplication and division are performed from left to right, followed by addition and subtraction from left to right.
Operations in mathematical expressions must be evaluated in a systematic order.
The order of operations is summarized by the acronym PEMDAS, which stands for Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication and Division, and Addition and Subtraction.
Simplify mathematical expressions by first simplifying expressions within grouping symbols.
Simplify expressions containing exponents or radicals after handling grouping symbols.
Perform multiplication and division in order from left to right after simplifying exponents and radicals.
Perform addition and subtraction in order from left to right as the final step in simplification.
The order of operations must be followed to correctly evaluate mathematical expressions.
Grouping symbols such as parentheses, absolute value bars, and fraction bars indicate which operations should be performed first.
Exponents are evaluated before multiplication and division, which are performed before addition and subtraction.
The fraction bar acts as a grouping symbol, so the numerator and denominator are simplified separately before division.
The commutative property of addition states that numbers may be added in any order without affecting the sum.
The commutative property of multiplication states that numbers may be multiplied in any order without affecting the product.
Subtraction and division are not commutative operations.
The associative property of multiplication states that the grouping of numbers does not affect the product, expressed as a(bc) = (ab)c.
The associative property of addition states that the grouping of numbers does not affect the sum, expressed as a + (b + c) = (a + b) + c.
Subtraction and division are not associative operations.
The distributive property states that the product of a factor times a sum is the sum of the factor times each term in the sum.
Multiplication distributes over addition, but addition does not distribute over multiplication.
A special case of the distributive property occurs when a sum of terms is subtracted, which can be rewritten by adding the opposite.
To subtract a sum of terms, change the sign of each term and add the results.
The identity property of addition states that adding zero to any number results in the original number.
The identity property of multiplication states that multiplying any number by one results in the original number.
The additive identity is 0 and the multiplicative identity is 1.
These identity properties hold for all real numbers.
The inverse property of addition states that for every real number a, there exists an additive inverse -a such that a + (-a) = 0.
The inverse property of multiplication states that for every nonzero real number a, there exists a multiplicative inverse 1/a such that a · (1/a) = 1.
The commutative property states that the order of addition or multiplication does not change the result.
The associative property states that the grouping of numbers in addition or multiplication does not change the result.
The distributive property states that multiplication distributes over addition.
The identity property states that adding 0 or multiplying by 1 leaves a real number unchanged.
The inverse property states that every real number has an additive inverse that sums to 0, and every nonzero real number has a multiplicative inverse that multiplies to 1.
The distributive property allows rewriting a sum of products as a product of a sum, as shown in 3·6 + 3·4 = 3·(6 + 4).
The associative property of addition allows regrouping terms, as shown in (5 + 8) + (-8) = 5 + [8 + (-8)].
The inverse property of addition states that a number plus its opposite equals zero, as shown in 8 + (-8) = 0.
The identity property of addition states that adding zero to a number leaves it unchanged, as shown in 5 + 0 = 5.
An algebraic expression is a collection of constants and variables joined by addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
To evaluate an algebraic expression, replace each variable with its given value and simplify using the order of operations.
Constants are fixed numerical values in an algebraic expression.
Variables are symbols that represent unknown or changeable values in an algebraic expression.
To evaluate an algebraic expression, substitute the given value for the variable and perform the arithmetic operations.
Evaluating an expression at different values demonstrates how the expression's result changes based on the input.
To evaluate an algebraic expression, substitute the given values for the variables and perform the arithmetic operations.
When evaluating expressions, follow the order of operations, including handling exponents and roots before multiplication and addition.
Substituting a negative number for a variable requires careful use of parentheses to maintain the correct sign in the calculation.
Evaluating expressions with multiple variables involves substituting each given value into its corresponding variable and then simplifying the result.
An equation is a mathematical statement indicating that two expressions are equal.
A formula is an equation expressing a relationship between constant and variable quantities.
The area of a circle is given by the formula A = πr², where r is the radius.
The surface area of a right circular cylinder is given by the formula S = 2πr(r + h).
To evaluate an algebraic expression, substitute the given values for the variables and perform the arithmetic operations.
The properties of real numbers, such as the distributive property, can be used to simplify algebraic expressions.
The commutative property of addition allows rearranging terms to group like terms together.
The distributive property is used to eliminate parentheses by multiplying each term inside by the factor outside.
Simplifying an algebraic expression involves combining like terms after applying properties such as commutative and distributive.
The perimeter of a rectangle can be expressed as P = L + W + L + W.
The commutative property of addition allows rearranging terms to group like variables.
Combining like terms simplifies the perimeter expression to P = 2L + 2W.
The distributive property can be used to factor the expression to P = 2(L + W).
The square root of two is an irrational number because it does not terminate or repeat and cannot be written as a quotient of two integers.
The Associative Properties allow the regrouping of terms in addition or multiplication without changing the result.
The order of operations must be followed to correctly simplify numeric expressions.
Parentheses indicate which operations should be performed first in an expression.
Exponents are evaluated before multiplication, division, addition, and subtraction.
Multiplication and division are performed before addition and subtraction.
Operations at the same level (e.g., multiplication and division) are performed from left to right.
Solving an algebraic equation involves finding the value of the variable that makes the equation true.
Simplifying an algebraic expression involves combining like terms and applying the order of operations.
The distributive property is used to expand expressions like a(b + c) into ab + ac.
The order of operations (PEMDAS) must be followed when evaluating or simplifying expressions.
Inverse operations are used to isolate the variable when solving equations.
The order of operations must be applied when writing algebraic expressions for real-world scenarios.
The circumference of a quarter is an irrational number because it involves multiplying the diameter by π.
The commutative property of addition states that the order in which numbers are added does not change the sum.
An equation can be written to model a real-world situation involving quantities that change over time.
The inverse property of addition states that for any real number a, there exists a number -a such that a + (-a) = 0.
A graphing calculator can be used to solve algebraic equations for a variable.
Solutions obtained from a graphing calculator should be rounded to the nearest hundredth when specified.
A whole number that is not a natural number must be zero.
The multiplicative inverse of a rational number is also rational.
The product of a rational and an irrational number is always irrational.
The division of two whole numbers always results in a rational number.
The distributive property simplifies the expression 4 + 7(x - 1).

learning_objectives: Use the product rule of exponents.
Use the quotient rule of exponents.
Use the power rule of exponents.
Use the zero exponent rule of exponents.
Use the negative rule of exponents.
