knowledge_units: The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying exponential expressions with the same base, the exponents are added: a^m * a^n = a^(m+n).
The product rule of exponents can be used to simplify expressions that are a product of two numbers or expressions with the same base but different exponents.
The product rule of exponents states that for any real number a and natural numbers m and n, a^m multiplied by a^n equals a^(m+n).
For any real number[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and natural numbers[latex]\,m\,[/latex]and[latex]\,n,[/latex] the product rule of exponents states that [latex]{a}^{m}\cdot {a}^{n}={a}^{m+n}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying exponential expressions with the same base, you add the exponents.
The product rule can be extended to more than two factors by adding all the exponents together.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing exponential expressions with the same base, you subtract the exponents.
The quotient rule requires that the exponent in the numerator is greater than the exponent in the denominator for now.
The quotient rule of exponents states that for any real number a and natural numbers m and n where m > n, a^m divided by a^n equals a^(m-n).

For any real number[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and natural numbers[latex]\,m\,[/latex]and[latex]\,n,[/latex] such that[latex]\,m>n,[/latex] the quotient rule of exponents states that [latex]\frac{{a}^{m}}{{a}^{n}}={a}^{m-n}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing like bases, subtract the exponents: a^m / a^n = a^(m-n).
The power rule of exponents states that when raising an exponential expression to a power, multiply the exponents: (a^m)^n = a^(m*n).
The product rule involves adding exponents when multiplying like bases, while the power rule involves multiplying exponents when raising a power to a power.
The power rule of exponents states that for any real number a and positive integers m and n, (a^m)^n = a^(m·n).
For any real number[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and positive integers[latex]\,m\,[/latex]and[latex]\,n,[/latex]the power rule of exponents states that [latex]{\left({a}^{m}\right)}^{n}={a}^{m\cdot n}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The power rule of exponents states that (a^m)^n = a^(m*n).
The zero exponent rule states that a^0 = 1 for any nonzero real number a.
The expression 0^0 is undefined.
For any nonzero real number a, the zero exponent rule of exponents states that a^0 = 1.
For any nonzero real number[latex]\,a,[/latex] the zero exponent rule of exponents states that [latex]{a}^{0}=1[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The zero exponent rule states that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The quotient rule of exponents is used to simplify expressions where the numerator and denominator have the same base.
The negative exponent rule states that a factor with a negative exponent equals its reciprocal with a positive exponent.
A negative exponent can be made positive by moving the factor across the fraction bar.
The exponential expression a^n is defined for any integer n, including negative integers.
The product and quotient rules of exponents hold for any integer exponent.
The negative rule of exponents states that for any nonzero real number a and natural number n, a raised to the negative n equals 1 divided by a raised to the n.
For any nonzero real number[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and natural number[latex]\,n,[/latex] the negative rule of exponents states that [latex]{a}^{-n}=\frac{1}{{a}^{n}}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The negative exponent rule states that a base raised to a negative exponent is equal to the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
To simplify a quotient of exponential expressions with the same base, subtract the exponent in the denominator from the exponent in the numerator.
When simplifying exponential expressions, combine like bases in the numerator or denominator first before applying the quotient rule.
The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying exponential expressions with the same base, add the exponents.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing exponential expressions with the same base, subtract the exponents.
The zero exponent rule states that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The negative exponent rule states that a base raised to a negative exponent is equivalent to the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
The power of a product rule states that raising a product to a power is equivalent to raising each factor to that power and multiplying the results.
The power of a product rule of exponents states that for any real numbers a and b and any integer n, (ab)^n = a^n b^n.

For any real numbers[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and[latex]\,b\,[/latex]and any integer[latex]\,n,[/latex] the power of a product rule of exponents states that [latex]{\left(ab\right)}^{n}={a}^{n}{b}^{n}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The power of a product rule states that when raising a product to a power, each factor is raised to that power.
To apply the power of a product rule, distribute the exponent to each factor in the product.
The power of a quotient rule states that the power of a quotient is the quotient of the powers of the numerator and denominator.
When simplifying expressions with negative exponents, the final answer should be written with positive exponents.
The power of a quotient rule of exponents states that for any real numbers a and b and any integer n, (a/b)^n = a^n / b^n.

For any real numbers[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and[latex]\,b\,[/latex]and any integer[latex]\,n,[/latex] the power of a quotient rule of exponents states that [latex]{\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)}^{n}=\frac{{a}^{n}}{{b}^{n}}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
The power of a quotient rule states that raising a fraction to a power applies the exponent to both the numerator and the denominator.
When applying the power of a quotient rule, the exponent is multiplied with the exponent of each term in the numerator and denominator.
A negative exponent in the numerator can be rewritten as a positive exponent in the denominator before applying the power of a quotient rule.
Simplifying exponential expressions involves combining exponent rules to rewrite the expression with fewer terms and positive exponents.
The power of a product rule states that when raising a product to a power, each factor is raised to that power.
The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying like bases, add the exponents.
The power rule of exponents states that when raising a power to another power, multiply the exponents.
The negative exponent rule states that a negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
The zero exponent rule states that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing like bases, subtract the exponents.
The power of a quotient rule states that when raising a quotient to a power, both the numerator and denominator are raised to that power.
Scientific notation expresses numbers as a decimal between 1 and 10 multiplied by a power of 10.
To write a number in scientific notation, move the decimal point to create a number between 1 and 10 and multiply by 10 raised to the number of places moved.
In scientific notation, a positive exponent indicates a large number (decimal moved left) and a negative exponent indicates a small number (decimal moved right).
A number is written in scientific notation if it is in the form a × 10^n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an integer.
A number is written in scientific notation if it is written in the form[latex]\,a\,×\,{10}^{n},[/latex] where[latex]1 \le a \lt 10[/latex] and [latex] \,n\,[/latex]is an integer.
Model exponential growth and decay.

To convert a number greater than 1 to scientific notation, move the decimal point to the left until only one non-zero digit remains to the left of the decimal, and the exponent of 10 is positive and equals the number of places moved.

To convert a number less than 1 to scientific notation, move the decimal point to the right until only one non-zero digit remains to the left of the decimal, and the exponent of 10 is negative and equals the number of places moved.
To convert a number from scientific notation to standard notation, move the decimal point to the right if the exponent is positive and to the left if the exponent is negative.
The number of places to move the decimal point is equal to the absolute value of the exponent.
To multiply numbers in scientific notation, multiply the coefficients and add the exponents of the powers of 10.
To divide numbers in scientific notation, divide the coefficients and subtract the exponents of the powers of 10.
When multiplying or dividing numbers in scientific notation, the final result must be written in proper scientific notation with a coefficient between 1 and 10.
Scientific notation uses powers of 10 to simplify very large or very small numbers.
To divide numbers in scientific notation, divide the coefficients and subtract the exponents of the powers of 10.
The result of a division in scientific notation may need to be adjusted to proper scientific notation by shifting the decimal point and adjusting the exponent.
Scientific notation can be used to simplify calculations involving very large or very small numbers.
An exponent indicates how many times the base is multiplied by itself.
Scientific notation is a method for writing very large or very small numbers.
A negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the base raised to the corresponding positive exponent.
The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying like bases, add the exponents.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing like bases, subtract the exponents.
The power rule of exponents states that when raising a power to a power, multiply the exponents.
The zero exponent rule states that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The negative exponent rule states that a base raised to a negative exponent equals the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
Scientific notation expresses a number as a product of a coefficient between 1 and 10 and a power of 10.
The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying like bases, add the exponents.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing like bases, subtract the exponents.
The power rule of exponents states that when raising a power to a power, multiply the exponents.
The zero exponent rule states that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The negative exponent rule states that a negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
To find the power of a product, raise each factor to the power.
To find the power of a quotient, raise both the numerator and the denominator to the power.
Simplifying exponential expressions involves applying the rules of exponents to write the result with positive exponents.
Scientific notation is used to express very large or very small numbers in a compact form.
To convert a number from scientific notation to standard notation, move the decimal point to the right for positive exponents and to the left for negative exponents.
To convert a number from standard notation to scientific notation, write it as a product of a number between 1 and 10 and a power of 10.
For the following exercises, use a graphing calculator to simplify.

The product rule of exponents states that when multiplying like bases, you add the exponents.
The quotient rule of exponents states that when dividing like bases, you subtract the exponents.
The power rule of exponents states that when raising a power to another power, you multiply the exponents.
The zero exponent rule states that any non-zero base raised to the zero power equals one.
The negative exponent rule states that a negative exponent indicates the reciprocal of the base raised to the positive exponent.
To find the power of a product, raise each factor in the product to the power.
To find the power of a quotient, raise both the numerator and the denominator to the power.
Scientific notation expresses a number as a product of a coefficient and a power of ten, where the coefficient is between 1 and 10.

learning_objectives: Evaluate square roots.
Use the product rule to simplify square roots.
Use the quotient rule to simplify square roots.
Add and subtract square roots.
Rationalize denominators.
Use rational roots.

knowledge_units: The square root of a number is a value that, when multiplied by itself, gives the original number.
The principal square root is the nonnegative square root of a number.
The square root function is the inverse of the squaring function.
The principal square root of a is written as √a, where √ is the radical sign and a is the radicand.
The principal square root of a nonnegative number a is the nonnegative number that, when multiplied by itself, equals a.
The principal square root is written using a radical symbol over the radicand.
The principal square root of a nonnegative number is the nonnegative root, so √25 = 5, not ±5.
The order of operations requires adding the terms inside the radicand before taking the square root, so √(25+144) ≠ √25 + √144.
The product rule for square roots states that √(ab) = √a · √b for nonnegative a and b.
The product rule can be used to separate a square root of a product into a product of square roots or to combine multiple square roots into one.
The product rule for square roots states that for nonnegative numbers a and b, the square root of the product ab equals the product of the square roots of a and b.

If[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and[latex]\,b\,[/latex]are nonnegative, the square root of the product[latex]\,ab\,[/latex]is equal to the product of the square roots of[latex]\,a\,[/latex]and[latex]\,b.\,[/latex] [latex]\sqrt{ab}=\sqrt{a}\cdot \sqrt{b}[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
To simplify a square root radical expression using the product rule, factor any perfect squares from the radicand.
After factoring perfect squares, write the radical expression as a product of radical expressions.
The final step in simplifying using the product rule is to simplify the resulting radical expressions.
The product rule for square roots states that the square root of a product is equal to the product of the square roots of the factors.
To simplify a square root, factor the radicand to identify perfect square factors.
After factoring, rewrite the square root as a product of the square roots of the factors.
Simplify the square root of any perfect square factor to its integer or variable equivalent.
The product rule for radicals allows combining multiple radical expressions into a single radical expression.
To simplify a product of radicals, first express it as a single radical expression and then simplify.
The product rule for square roots allows rewriting the square root of a product as a product of square roots.
The quotient rule for square roots allows rewriting the square root of a quotient as a quotient of square roots.
The square root of a quotient equals the quotient of the square roots of the numerator and denominator, provided the denominator is not zero.
The quotient rule for square roots is expressed as the square root of a/b equals the square root of a divided by the square root of b.
The quotient rule for simplifying square roots involves writing the radical expression as a quotient of two radical expressions.
After writing the expression as a quotient, simplify the numerator and denominator separately.
The quotient rule for square roots states that the square root of a fraction equals the square root of the numerator divided by the square root of the denominator.
To simplify a square root of a fraction, apply the quotient rule to separate the numerator and denominator into individual square roots.
After applying the quotient rule, simplify the square root in the denominator if it is a perfect square.
The quotient rule for square roots states that the square root of a quotient is equal to the quotient of the square roots.
To simplify a radical expression with two square roots, combine them into a single radical and then simplify the fraction inside.
Radical expressions can be added or subtracted only when they have the same radicand and the same radical type.
Simplifying a radical expression may change its radicand, allowing it to be combined with other like radicals.
To add or subtract square roots, first simplify each radical expression.
After simplifying, add or subtract only expressions with equal radicands.
To add square roots, first simplify each radical so that they have the same radicand.
The product rule allows rewriting a square root of a product as the product of the square roots.
Square roots with the same radicand can be added by adding their coefficients.
To subtract square roots, rewrite each term so they have equal radicands.
When subtracting square roots with equal radicands, subtract the coefficients and keep the common radical.
Rationalizing the denominator removes radicals from the denominator of a fraction.
To rationalize a denominator with a single radical term, multiply the fraction by the radical over itself.
To rationalize a denominator that is a sum or difference of a rational and an irrational term, multiply by the conjugate of the denominator.
To rationalize a denominator containing a single square root radical, multiply both the numerator and denominator by that radical.
After multiplying by the radical in the denominator, simplify the resulting expression.
Rationalizing a denominator containing a single term involves multiplying the fraction by a form of one that eliminates the radical in the denominator.
To rationalize a denominator with a single square root, multiply the numerator and denominator by that square root.
After rationalizing, simplify the resulting expression by reducing the fraction if possible.
To rationalize a denominator containing a radical term and a constant, multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
After multiplying by the conjugate, use the distributive property to expand the expression.
Simplify the resulting expression to complete the rationalization process.
To rationalize a denominator containing two terms, multiply the fraction by the conjugate of the denominator over itself.
The conjugate of a binomial denominator is found by changing the sign between its two terms.
Rational roots include cube roots, 4th roots, and 5th roots, which are inverse functions of their respective power functions.
The nth root of a is a number that, when raised to the nth power, gives a.
The principal nth root of a is the number with the same sign as a that, when raised to the nth power, equals a.
The principal nth root of a is written as √[n]{a}, where n is the index of the radical.
The principal nth root of a real number a, written as √[n]{a}, is the number with the same sign as a that, when raised to the nth power, equals a.
The index of the radical is n.
The nth root of a number can be evaluated by finding a value that, when raised to the nth power, equals the radicand.
The product of two nth roots with the same index can be simplified by multiplying the radicands under a single radical.
A quotient of nth roots can be simplified by taking the nth root of the numerator and denominator separately.
To add or subtract nth roots, the radicands must be identical after simplification, allowing the coefficients to be combined.
Radical expressions can be written using rational exponents, where the index must be a positive integer.
If the index n is even, then the base a cannot be negative.
The expression a^(1/n) is equivalent to the nth root of a.
For a rational exponent m/n, the expression a^(m/n) is equivalent to (nth root of a)^m or the nth root of a^m.
All properties of exponents that apply to integer exponents also apply to rational exponents.
Rational exponents provide an alternative notation for expressing principal nth roots.
The general conversion between radical and rational exponent forms is given by a^(m/n) = (n√a)^m = n√(a^m).
To convert an expression with a rational exponent to radical form, determine the power from the numerator of the exponent and the root from the denominator.

A rational exponent a^(m/n) can be written as the nth root of a raised to the m power, or equivalently as the nth root of a^m.
When simplifying a rational exponent, it is generally easier to compute the root first and then raise the result to the power.
A radical expression can be written using a rational exponent where the numerator is the power and the denominator is the root.
The expression \(\frac{4}{\sqrt[7]{a^2}}\) can be rewritten as \(4a^{-\frac{2}{7}}\) using rational exponents.
The principal square root of a number a is the nonnegative number that when multiplied by itself equals a.
If a and b are nonnegative, the square root of the product ab is equal to the product of the square roots of a and b.
If a and b are nonnegative, the square root of the quotient a/b is equal to the quotient of the square roots of a and b.
Radical expressions can be added or subtracted if they have the same radicand and the same index.
To eliminate a square root radical from the denominator, multiply both the numerator and the denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
Radicals can be rewritten as rational exponents, and rational exponents can be rewritten as radicals.
The properties of exponents apply to rational exponents.
When a radical has no index, it is assumed to be a square root (index 2).
The principal square root is the nonnegative root of a number.

The square root of a perfect square is an integer.
The square root of a product can be simplified by taking the square root of each factor.
The square root of a quotient can be simplified by taking the square root of the numerator and denominator separately.
Square roots can be added or subtracted when they have the same radicand.
To rationalize a denominator, multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
Rational roots are roots that can be expressed as a fraction of integers.
The square root of a perfect square expression simplifies to the base raised to half the exponent.
The square root of a product can be simplified by taking the square root of each factor.
The square root of a quotient can be simplified by taking the square root of the numerator and denominator separately.
Square roots can be added or subtracted only when they have the same radicand.
To rationalize a denominator containing a square root, multiply the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
Rational exponents can be converted to radical form and simplified using the rules for square roots.
The Pythagorean Theorem can be used to find the length of a guy wire by evaluating the square root of the sum of the squares of the horizontal distance and the vertical height.

Simplifying expressions with square roots often requires applying the product and quotient rules.
Rationalizing denominators is a technique used to eliminate radicals from the denominator of a fraction.
Combining like terms and simplifying radicals are essential steps in adding and subtracting square root expressions.

learning_objectives: Identify the degree and leading coefficient of polynomials.
Add and subtract polynomials.
Multiply polynomials.

knowledge_units: A polynomial is a sum or difference of terms, each consisting of a variable raised to a nonnegative integer power.
A coefficient is a number multiplied by a variable raised to an exponent.
A term of a polynomial is a product of a coefficient and a variable raised to a power.
A constant is a term that does not contain a variable.
A monomial is a polynomial containing only one term.
A binomial is a polynomial containing two terms.
A trinomial is a polynomial containing three terms.
The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable that occurs in the polynomial.
The leading term is the term with the highest degree in a polynomial.
The leading coefficient is the coefficient of the leading term.
A polynomial is in standard form when its terms are written in descending order of powers.
A polynomial is an expression that can be written in the form a_n x^n + ... + a_2 x^2 + a_1 x + a_0.
Each real number a_i in a polynomial is called a coefficient.
The number a_0 that is not multiplied by a variable is called a constant.
Each product a_i x^i is a term of a polynomial.
The highest power of the variable that occurs in the polynomial is called the degree of a polynomial.
The leading term is the term with the highest power, and its coefficient is called the leading coefficient.
The degree of a polynomial is determined by the highest power of the variable.
The leading term of a polynomial is the term containing the highest power of the variable.
The leading coefficient is the coefficient of the leading term.
The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable in the polynomial.
The leading term of a polynomial is the term containing the highest degree.
The leading coefficient is the coefficient of the leading term.

To add or subtract polynomials, combine like terms.
After combining like terms, simplify and write the result in standard form.
To add polynomials, combine like terms by adding the coefficients of terms with the same variable and exponent.

The FOIL method is a shortcut for multiplying binomials.
Special products follow patterns that can be memorized to simplify polynomial multiplication.

When multiplying polynomials, the distributive property allows multiplying each term of the first polynomial by each term of the second.
After multiplying polynomial terms, add the products together and combine like terms to simplify the expression.
To multiply two polynomials, use the distributive property to multiply each term of the first polynomial by each term of the second.
After multiplying polynomials, combine like terms and simplify the expression.
The distributive property can be used to multiply polynomials by distributing each term of one polynomial to every term of the other polynomial.
After distributing, combine like terms to simplify the resulting polynomial expression.
A table can be used to organize the multiplication of each term from one polynomial by each term from the other polynomial.
The FOIL method is a shortcut for multiplying two binomials by multiplying the first, outer, inner, and last terms.
The FOIL method is derived from the distributive property, where each term of the first binomial is multiplied by each term of the second binomial.
The FOIL method is used to multiply two binomials by multiplying the first, outer, inner, and last terms.
After multiplying the terms of two binomials, the products are added and like terms are combined to simplify the expression.
The FOIL method is used to multiply two binomials by multiplying the First, Outer, Inner, and Last terms.
After applying FOIL, combine like terms to simplify the resulting polynomial.
A perfect square trinomial is the result of squaring a binomial.
The first term of a perfect square trinomial is the square of the first term of the binomial.
The last term of a perfect square trinomial is the square of the last term of the binomial.
The middle term of a perfect square trinomial is double the product of the two terms of the binomial.
The sign of the middle term in a perfect square trinomial matches the sign of the binomial.
A perfect square trinomial is the result of squaring a binomial.
The square of a binomial (x + a) is x^2 + 2ax + a^2.
To square a binomial, use the formula for perfect square trinomials.
The first step in squaring a binomial is to square the first term.
The second step is to square the last term of the binomial.
The middle term of the resulting trinomial is double the product of the two binomial terms.
After calculating the terms, add and simplify to complete the square.
Expanding a perfect square binomial involves squaring the first term, doubling the product of the two terms for the middle term, and squaring the last term.
The expansion of (3x - 8)^2 results in the trinomial 9x^2 - 48x + 64.
The difference of squares occurs when multiplying a binomial by another binomial with the same terms but opposite signs.
When multiplying binomials with opposite signs, the outer and inner terms cancel, resulting in the square of the first term minus the square of the last term.
The difference of squares is the product of two binomials with the same terms but opposite signs, resulting in the square of the first term minus the square of the last term.
The formula for the difference of squares is (a + b)(a - b) = a² - b².
There is no special form for the sum of squares because no two binomials multiply to equal a sum of squares.
The difference of squares is found by squaring the first term, squaring the last term, and subtracting the square of the last term from the square of the first term.
Given a binomial multiplied by a binomial with the same terms but the opposite sign, find the difference of squares.
Model exponential growth and decay.
The product of a sum and difference of the same two terms is the difference of their squares.
Polynomials can contain several variables, and the same rules for operations apply as with single-variable polynomials.
Multiplying polynomials with several variables involves using the distributive property and combining like terms.
To multiply polynomials containing several variables, use the distributive property to multiply each term in the first polynomial by each term in the second polynomial.
After multiplying, combine like terms to simplify the resulting polynomial.
The same rules for multiplying polynomials with one variable apply to polynomials with several variables.
The degree of a polynomial in standard form is the exponent of the leading term because the leading term has the highest exponent.
Multiplying two binomials with two variables can result in a trinomial, except for a difference of two squares which yields a binomial.
The four steps to multiply polynomials are: use the distributive property, multiply, combine like terms, and simplify.
A trinomial is not always a higher degree than a monomial because degree depends on the exponents, not the number of terms.
The degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable in the polynomial.
To add or subtract polynomials, combine like terms by adding or subtracting their coefficients.
To multiply polynomials, use the distributive property to multiply each term in the first polynomial by each term in the second polynomial.
When multiplying binomials, the product of the sum and difference of two terms is the difference of their squares: (a+b)(a-b) = a² - b².
When squaring a binomial, the result is the square of the first term plus twice the product of the terms plus the square of the last term: (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b².
Polynomials with several variables are multiplied by applying the distributive property to each term, treating each variable separately.
Multiplying binomials can be used to find the area of a plot of land in standard form.
Expanding a squared binomial and multiplying by a height can determine the volume of a silo.
Polynomial operations include addition, subtraction, and multiplication.
Multiplying polynomials involves applying the distributive property to combine like terms.
Operations with polynomials of several variables follow the same rules as single-variable polynomials.

learning_objectives: Factor the greatest common factor of a polynomial.
Factor a trinomial.
Factor by grouping.
Factor a perfect square trinomial.
Factor a difference of squares.
Factor the sum and difference of cubes.
Factor expressions using fractional or negative exponents.

knowledge_units: The greatest common factor (GCF) of a polynomial is the largest polynomial that divides evenly into each term.
When factoring a polynomial, the first step is to check for a greatest common factor (GCF).
To find the GCF of a polynomial, look for the GCF of the coefficients and then the GCF of the variables.
Factoring a polynomial expression can simplify it into a product of simpler expressions.
The greatest common factor (GCF) of polynomials is the largest polynomial that divides evenly into the polynomials.
Model exponential growth and decay.
To factor out the greatest common factor of a polynomial, first identify the GCF of the coefficients and the GCF of the variables.
The factored expression is written as the product of the GCF and the sum of the terms needed to multiply by to obtain each original term.
To factor a polynomial, first find the greatest common factor (GCF) of all terms.
The GCF of a polynomial is the product of the GCF of the coefficients and the GCF of the variables with their lowest exponents.
After factoring out the GCF, the polynomial is expressed as the product of the GCF and the sum of the remaining terms.
Trinomials of the form x^2 + bx + c can be factored by finding two numbers whose product is c and whose sum is b.
A trinomial of the form x^2 + bx + c can be factored as (x + p)(x + q) where pq = c and p + q = b.
A trinomial of the form[latex]\,{x}^{2}+bx+c\,[/latex]can be written in factored form as[latex]\,\left(x+p\right)\left(x+q\right)\,[/latex]where[latex]\,pq=c\,[/latex]and[latex]\,p+q=b.[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
To factor a trinomial of the form x^2 + bx + c, list the factors of c.
Find a pair of factors p and q of c such that their sum equals b.
Write the factored expression as (x + p)(x + q).
To factor a trinomial with leading coefficient 1, find two numbers whose product equals the constant term and whose sum equals the coefficient of the linear term.
The factored form of a trinomial with leading coefficient 1 is the product of two binomials (x + p)(x + q) where p and q are the numbers found.
Not every trinomial can be factored into a product of binomials; such polynomials are called prime.
The order of the factors in a factored polynomial does not matter because multiplication is commutative.
Factoring by grouping is used for trinomials with leading coefficients other than 1 by splitting the middle term and factoring in pairs.
To factor a trinomial of the form ax^2 + bx + c by grouping, find two numbers whose product is ac and whose sum is b.
The two numbers are used to split the middle term into two terms, allowing the expression to be factored in groups.
After grouping, factor out the greatest common factor from each group and then from the entire expression.
To factor a trinomial of the form ax^2 + bx + c by grouping, first find factors p and q of ac that sum to b.
Rewrite the trinomial as ax^2 + px + qx + c to prepare for factoring by grouping.
Factor by grouping involves pulling out the greatest common factor from the first two terms and the last two terms separately.
After grouping, factor out the greatest common factor of the entire expression to complete the factorization.
To factor a trinomial by grouping, first find two numbers whose product is ac and whose sum is b.
The trinomial is rewritten as four terms using the two numbers found, then factored by grouping.
A perfect square trinomial is a trinomial that can be written as the square of a binomial.
The square of a binomial (a + b)² equals a² + 2ab + b².
The square of a binomial (a - b)² equals a² - 2ab + b².
A perfect square trinomial can be written as the square of a binomial.
The general form of a perfect square trinomial is a^2 + 2ab + b^2 = (a + b)^2.
A perfect square trinomial can be factored into the square of a binomial.
To factor a perfect square trinomial, first confirm that the first and last terms are perfect squares.
The middle term of a perfect square trinomial must be twice the product of the square roots of the first and last terms.
The factored form of a perfect square trinomial is written as the square of a binomial.
A perfect square trinomial is a trinomial where the first and last terms are perfect squares and the middle term is twice the product of their square roots.
A difference of squares is a binomial that can be factored into the product of two binomials with the same terms but opposite signs.
A difference of squares can be rewritten as two factors containing the same terms but opposite signs.
The difference of squares formula is a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b).
To factor a difference of squares, first confirm that both terms are perfect squares.
The factored form of a difference of squares is (a + b)(a - b).
A difference of squares can be factored into the product of two binomials: a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b).
A sum of squares cannot be factored.
A sum of cubes can be factored into a binomial and a trinomial: a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2).
A difference of cubes can be factored into a binomial and a trinomial: a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2).
The acronym SOAP (Same, Opposite, Always Positive) helps remember the signs when factoring the sum or difference of cubes.
The sum of two cubes can be factored as (a + b)(a² - ab + b²).
The difference of two cubes can be factored as (a - b)(a² + ab + b²).
To factor a sum of cubes, write it as (a + b)(a² - ab + b²).
To factor a difference of cubes, write it as (a - b)(a² + ab + b²).
A sum of cubes can be factored using the formula a^3 + b^3 = (a + b)(a^2 - ab + b^2).
To factor a sum of cubes, identify the cube roots of each term and apply the sum of cubes formula.
The trinomial factor resulting from a sum of cubes factorization cannot be factored further.
A difference of cubes can be factored using the formula a³ - b³ = (a - b)(a² + ab + b²).
The trinomial factor resulting from factoring a difference of cubes cannot be factored further.
Expressions with fractional or negative exponents can be factored by extracting the greatest common factor (GCF) with the lowest exponent.
When factoring an expression with fractional or negative exponents, factor out the term with the lowest exponent as the greatest common factor.
After factoring out the greatest common factor, simplify the remaining expression by combining like terms.
The first step in any factoring problem should be to check for a greatest common factor.
Polynomials containing fractional and negative exponents can be factored by pulling out a greatest common factor.
A polynomial can be factorable even if its terms do not have a greatest common factor.
Factoring by grouping involves dividing a term into the sum of two terms, factoring each portion separately, and then factoring out the greatest common factor of the entire expression.
The greatest common factor of a polynomial is the largest expression that divides each term evenly.
Factoring by grouping involves splitting a polynomial into groups and factoring each group separately to find a common binomial factor.

The difference of cubes factors into a binomial and a trinomial using the formula a^3 - b^3 = (a - b)(a^2 + ab + b^2).
Expressions with fractional or negative exponents can be factored by identifying a common factor with the smallest exponent.

learning_objectives: Simplify rational expressions.

knowledge_units: A rational expression is the quotient of two polynomial expressions.

After multiplying rational expressions, cancel any common factors to simplify the product.

Factor all numerators and denominators completely before simplifying.

After rewriting with a common denominator, the numerators are added or subtracted.
The resulting rational expression is simplified.

Each rational expression is multiplied by a form of 1 to rewrite it with the LCD as the denominator.
After rewriting with a common denominator, add the numerators and keep the common denominator.
Multiplying a rational expression by a fraction equal to 1 does not change its value.
To subtract rational expressions, factor the denominators and find the least common denominator (LCD).
Multiply each fraction by a form of 1 to rewrite it with the LCD before subtracting.
Apply the distributive property to combine the numerators over the common denominator.
Simplify the resulting expression by factoring and canceling common factors.

A complex rational expression contains rational expressions in its numerator, denominator, or both.
To simplify a complex rational expression, rewrite the numerator and denominator as single rational expressions and then divide.
To simplify a complex rational expression, first combine the expressions in the numerator into a single rational expression by adding or subtracting.
Next, combine the expressions in the denominator into a single rational expression by adding or subtracting.
Rewrite the complex rational expression as the numerator divided by the denominator.
Rewrite the division as multiplication by the reciprocal of the denominator.
Multiply the expressions and simplify the result.

Rewrite the division of two rational expressions as multiplication by the reciprocal of the divisor.
Multiplying rational expressions involves multiplying the numerators and multiplying the denominators.
Adding or subtracting rational expressions requires finding a common denominator.
Factoring the numerator and denominator of a rational expression allows you to cancel common factors to simplify it.
The least common denominator (LCD) is used to combine rational expressions when adding or subtracting them.
Finding the LCD is only necessary for adding or subtracting rational expressions, not for multiplication or division.
Simplifying a rational expression involves factoring the numerator and denominator and canceling common factors.
To multiply rational expressions, multiply the numerators together and the denominators together, then simplify the result.
Dividing rational expressions requires multiplying by the reciprocal of the divisor and then simplifying.

Dividing rational expressions is used to find the number of sod pieces needed by dividing the yard area by the sod area.

Multiplying rational expressions involves multiplying the numerators together and the denominators together, then simplifying.
Dividing rational expressions is equivalent to multiplying by the reciprocal of the divisor, followed by simplification.

learning_objectives: Explain the concept of territorial possession as used in the context of NFL football and relate it to mathematical points on a coordinate plane.
Identify the coordinates of points A and B in a real-world scenario and describe their significance in determining a feasible route.
Apply the concept of equations and inequalities to model decision-making processes, such as choosing the most feasible path between two points.

knowledge_units: Territorial possession in NFL football refers to the ball's position on the field, which is governed by referees using chains.
The ball's position can be represented as point A with coordinates (x1, y1) on a coordinate plane.
The quarterback must decide the most feasible route from point A to point B, the end zone, with coordinates (x2, y2).

learning_objectives: Solve equations in one variable.
Solve a rational equation.

knowledge_units: A linear equation in one variable is an equation of a straight line where the variable has a power of 1.
Linear equations in one variable can be classified as identity, conditional, or inconsistent.
An identity equation is true for all values of the variable, and its solution set is all real numbers.
A conditional equation is true for only some values of the variable, and its solution set consists of specific numbers.
An inconsistent equation results in a false statement and has no solution.
Solving linear equations in one variable involves using fundamental properties of equality and basic algebraic operations.
A linear equation in one variable can be written in the form ax + b = 0, where a and b are real numbers and a ≠ 0.
A linear equation in one variable can be written in the form [latex]ax+b=0[/latex] where a and b are real numbers,[latex]\,a\ne 0.[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.
To solve a linear equation in one variable, use algebra to isolate the unknown variable.
When manipulating an equation, you may add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides by the same number or expression, provided you do not divide by zero.
Apply the distributive property as needed to simplify expressions.
Isolate the variable on one side of the equation to solve it.
When the variable is multiplied by a coefficient, multiply both sides by the reciprocal of the coefficient to solve for the variable.
An equation in one variable can be solved by performing algebraic operations to isolate the variable.
To solve an equation, one can subtract the same value from both sides to simplify the equation.
To solve an equation, one can multiply or divide both sides by the same nonzero value to isolate the variable.
To solve an equation with variables on both sides, apply algebraic properties such as the distributive property and combining like terms to isolate the variable.
A rational equation is an equation that contains at least one rational expression, which is a ratio of two polynomials.
To solve a rational equation, multiply both sides by the least common denominator (LCD) to eliminate denominators and simplify the equation.
To solve a rational equation, multiply both sides by the least common denominator (LCD) of all fractions in the equation.
The LCD must contain each denominator as a factor, and each denominator must divide evenly into the LCD.
When finding the LCD, treat binomial denominators as single factors and factor all denominators first.
For a rational equation in the form of a proportion (one fraction equals another), cross-multiplication can be used to solve without finding the LCD.
Any solution that makes a denominator in the original equation equal to zero must be excluded.
A rational equation contains at least one rational expression where the variable appears in at least one of the denominators.
Model exponential growth and decay.
To solve a rational equation, first factor all denominators and exclude values that make any denominator zero.
After finding the least common denominator (LCD), multiply the entire equation by the LCD to eliminate denominators.
Solve the resulting equation and check all solutions in the original equation to ensure they do not produce a zero denominator.
To solve a rational equation, identify the least common denominator (LCD) of all fractions in the equation.
Multiplying both sides of a rational equation by the LCD eliminates the denominators and simplifies the equation.
Values that make any denominator zero must be excluded from the solution set of a rational equation.
To solve a rational equation, first find the common denominator of all terms.
The common denominator is the smallest expression divisible by each denominator in the equation.
Values that make any denominator zero are excluded from the solution set.
Multiplying both sides of the equation by the common denominator eliminates the fractions.
After clearing the denominators, solve the resulting linear equation for the variable.
To solve a rational equation, identify the least common denominator (LCD) of all fractions in the equation.
Multiplying both sides of a rational equation by the LCD eliminates the denominators and simplifies the equation.

After solving a rational equation, verify that the solution is not an excluded value to ensure it is valid.
To solve a rational equation, factor the denominators and identify the least common denominator (LCD).
Multiplying both sides of a rational equation by the LCD eliminates the denominators.
Excluded values are values that make any denominator in the original equation equal to zero.
Solutions to a rational equation must be checked in the original equation to ensure they do not result in division by zero.

When solving a rational equation, values that make any denominator zero must be excluded from the solution set.
Solving an equation for x involves isolating the variable on one side of the equation.
A rational equation contains at least one rational expression with a variable in the denominator.
When solving a rational equation, values that make any denominator zero must be excluded from the solution set.
To solve a rational equation, one can multiply both sides by the least common denominator to eliminate the fractions.
After solving a rational equation, the solution must be checked against the excluded values to ensure it is valid.

learning_objectives: Set up a linear equation to solve a real-world application.
Use a formula to solve a real-world application.

knowledge_units: Many real-world applications can be modeled by linear equations.
To set up a linear equation for a real-world application, determine known quantities and define the unknown quantity as a variable.
A variable cost depends on the unknown quantity and is represented by a term like 0.10x.
A fixed cost is independent of the variable and is added or subtracted as a constant term.
Common verbal expressions can be directly translated into mathematical expressions for modeling equations.
To model a linear equation for a real-world problem, identify known quantities and assign a variable to represent the unknown quantity.
If there is more than one unknown quantity, express the second unknown in terms of the first.
Write an equation by interpreting the problem's words as mathematical operations.
Solve the equation and ensure the solution can be explained in words, including units of measure.
A linear equation can be set up to solve for unknown quantities in a real-world problem.
When one number exceeds another by a given amount, the second number can be expressed as the first number plus that amount.
The word 'is' in a problem statement typically translates to an equal sign in the equation.
Solving the linear equation yields the values of the unknown numbers.

To solve a real-world application, identify known and unknown quantities and set up equations based on the given relationships.

To represent the remaining amount after investing x dollars from a total of $2,000, use the expression 2,000 - x.

knowledge_units: The set of complex numbers fills voids in the set of real numbers.
Complex numbers are a new set of numbers that extend the real numbers.
The imaginary unit i is defined as the square root of -1.
The square root of any negative number can be expressed as a multiple of i.
A complex number is the sum of a real number and an imaginary number.
A complex number is written in standard form as a + bi, where a is the real part and b is the imaginary part.
Squaring an imaginary number produces a negative real number.
A complex number is a number of the form a + bi, where i = √(-1).
In the complex number a + bi, a is the real part and b is the imaginary part.
If b = 0, the complex number a + bi is a real number.
If a = 0 and b ≠ 0, the complex number is a pure imaginary number.
To express an imaginary number in standard complex form, rewrite the square root of a negative number as the product of the square root of the positive number and i.
The imaginary unit i is defined as the square root of -1.
The standard form of a complex number is a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.
An imaginary number can be expressed in standard form as a real part plus an imaginary part.
The square root of a negative number can be simplified by factoring out the square root of -1, which is i.
The standard form of a complex number is a + bi, where a and b are real numbers.
Complex numbers cannot be plotted on a real number line but can be represented graphically on the complex plane.
The complex plane is a coordinate system where the horizontal axis represents the real component and the vertical axis represents the imaginary component.
A complex number is represented as an ordered pair (a, b) on the complex plane, where a is the real part and b is the imaginary part.
In the complex plane, the horizontal axis is the real axis and the vertical axis is the imaginary axis.
In the complex plane, the horizontal axis is the real axis, and the vertical axis is the imaginary axis, [latex]\, 3 \,[/latex].
Model exponential growth and decay.

The imaginary part of a complex number is plotted parallel to the vertical axis.
A complex number is plotted on the complex plane using its real part as the horizontal coordinate and its imaginary part as the vertical coordinate.
To add or subtract complex numbers, combine the real parts and combine the imaginary parts separately.
The sum of two complex numbers (a+bi) and (c+di) is (a+c)+(b+d)i.
The difference of two complex numbers (a+bi) and (c+di) is (a-c)+(b-d)i.
To add or subtract complex numbers, first identify the real and imaginary parts of each number.
When adding or subtracting complex numbers, combine the real parts separately from the imaginary parts.
To add or subtract complex numbers, combine the real parts and combine the imaginary parts separately.
Multiplying complex numbers is similar to multiplying binomials, with separate handling of real and imaginary parts.
Multiplying a complex number by a real number involves distributing the real number to both the real and imaginary parts of the complex number.
Let's begin by multiplying a complex number by a real number.
Model exponential growth and decay.
