As long as inflation is positive, real GDP is less than nominal GDP in years after the base year.
As long as inflation is positive, real GDP is greater than nominal GDP in years before the base year.
The GDP deflator can be calculated as (Nominal GDP / Real GDP) × 100.
Real GDP growth rate approximates the nominal GDP growth rate minus the inflation rate.

learning_objectives: Explain recessions, depressions, peaks, and troughs
Evaluate the importance of tracking real GDP over time

knowledge_units: Real GDP growth is reported as an annualized rate by multiplying the quarterly growth rate by four.
Real GDP is highly correlated with other measures of economic activity, such as employment.
Recessions and depressions lead to layoffs and job losses, causing financial and personal hardship.
A recession is defined as the period from a peak to a trough in the business cycle.
An economic expansion is defined as the period from a trough to a peak in the business cycle.
The business cycle describes the economy's movement from peak to trough and trough to peak.
The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) tracks business cycles for the U.S. economy.
The speeding up and slowing down of GDP growth represents the business cycle.
A recession occurs when GDP declines significantly.
A depression is a longer and deeper decline than a recession.
Recessions begin at the business cycle's peak and end at the trough.

learning_objectives: Explain how we can use GDP to compare the economic welfare of different nations
Calculate the conversion of GDP to a common currency by using exchange rates
Calculate GDP per capita using population data

knowledge_units: GDP is commonly used as a measure of economic welfare or standard of living in a nation.
Comparing GDP between countries requires converting to a common currency because each country measures GDP in its own currency.
To compare standards of living across countries, GDP must be divided by population to account for differences in the number of people.
To compare GDP across countries with different currencies, conversion to a common currency using an exchange rate is necessary.
Exchange rates can be expressed as the units of one currency needed for a single unit of another currency.
Market exchange rates fluctuate daily based on supply and demand in foreign exchange markets.
Purchasing power parity (PPP) equivalent exchange rates provide a longer-run measure for cross-country GDP comparisons.
Economists typically use PPP-equivalent exchange rates for comparing GDP across nations.
Converting GDP to a common currency involves dividing the domestic GDP by the exchange rate.
GDP per capita is calculated by dividing a country's GDP by its population.
GDP per capita provides a better measure of a nation's economic welfare than total GDP alone.
To compare GDP across countries, we must first convert GDP to a common currency using exchange rates.
Countries with large populations may have large total GDPs, but GDP per capita reveals the average economic output per person.
High-income nations typically have GDP per capita in the range of $20,000 to $50,000.
Middle-income countries have GDP per capita in the range of $6,000 to $12,000.
Low-income countries often have GDP per capita of less than $2,000 per year.
GDP per capita gives only a rough idea of differences in living standards across countries.

learning_objectives: Discuss how productivity influences the standard of living
Explain the limitations of GDP as a measure of the standard of living
Analyze the relationship between GDP data and fluctuations in the standard of living

knowledge_units: GDP per capita captures some aspects of the standard of living, as evidenced by migration patterns from low to high GDP per capita countries.
Standard of living is a broader concept than GDP because it includes all elements affecting well-being, not just market transactions.
GDP does not cover all factors relevant to the standard of living, such as non-market elements that affect well-being.
GDP measures economic activity but not all activity, making it a limited indicator of well-being and prosperity.
GDP does not capture unpaid work or leisure time, so equal GDP between countries may not reflect equal prosperity if work hours differ.
GDP includes spending on environmental protection, healthcare, and education but does not measure actual levels of environmental cleanliness, health, or learning.
GDP counts market-exchanged production but excludes non-market production, such as household tasks done by individuals.
GDP per capita is an average that does not reveal inequality in how income is distributed across society.
GDP does not account for the variety of goods and services available to consumers.
GDP does not reflect the availability of new technologies or products that can affect the standard of living.
A rise in GDP may not indicate improved well-being if it results from negative events like natural disasters or increased spending on security due to crime.
GDP per capita does not fully capture the broader concept of standard of living.
A rise in GDP could theoretically coincide with a falling standard of living if factors like health and environmental quality worsen.

The rise in GDP can understate the actual rise in the standard of living because it does not capture improvements in leisure, health, environmental cleanliness, technological possibilities, and product variety.
The rise in GDP can overstate the true rise in the standard of living because it ignores negative factors like higher crime, traffic congestion, and income inequality.
The shift of services from the non-market to the market economy can cause GDP to overstate the rise in the standard of living.
GDP measures production well and indicates when a country is materially better or worse off in terms of jobs and incomes.
GDP per capita is a reasonable, rough-and-ready measure of the standard of living.

GDP does not directly account for leisure, environmental quality, levels of health and education, activities conducted outside the market, changes in income inequality, increases in variety, increases in technology, or the value society places on certain types of output.
A significantly higher GDP per capita often occurs with improvements in everyday life along dimensions like education, health, and environmental protection.

learning_objectives: The Relatively Recent Arrival of Economic Growth
Labor Productivity and Economic Growth
Components of Economic Growth
Economic Convergence

knowledge_units: The picture shows the average daily calorie consumption for an individual from various countries.
The United States has the highest intake at 3,770 calories.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
The average worker in the early 1600s earned wages that could afford 2,500 food calories per day.
Between 1800 and 1875, economic growth allowed western European workers to purchase 5,000 food calories per day.
By 2012, a low-skilled worker in an affluent Western European/North American country could afford 2.4 million food calories per day.
Dramatic improvements in a nation's standard of living are possible, as shown by South Korea's rapid economic growth from the 1960s to the early twenty-first century.
South Korea's GDP per capita increased by more than 6% per year over four decades, transforming it into a high-income country.
Other East Asian nations like Thailand, Indonesia, and China have also experienced rapid and sustained economic growth.
The U.S. economy has transformed from a primarily rural and agricultural economy to one based on services, manufacturing, and technology since the Civil War.

learning_objectives: Explain the conditions that have allowed for modern economic growth in the last two centuries
Analyze the influence of public policies on an economy's long-run economic growth

knowledge_units: Modern economic growth refers to the rapid and sustained economic growth experienced in the last two centuries.
Before the last two centuries, the average person's standard of living had not changed much for centuries.
The Industrial Revolution, beginning in Great Britain, involved the widespread use of power-driven machinery and led to significant economic and social changes.
The Industrial Revolution created a self-reinforcing cycle where new inventions and investments generated profits, funding further investment and inventions.
Institutions, such as laws and traditions, play a critical role in a country's economic growth.
The Industrial Revolution led to increasing inequality among nations, with some economies growing rapidly while others remained near subsistence levels.
Catching up in economic growth is possible, as demonstrated by countries like Japan, Brazil, South Korea, and China in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries.
Economic growth depends on adherence to the rule of law and protection of property rights and contractual rights by a country's government.
Laws must be clear, public, fair, enforced, and equally applicable to all members of society for markets to work effectively.
Property rights are the rights of individuals and firms to own property and use it as they see fit.
Contractual rights are based on property rights and allow individuals to enter into agreements regarding the use of their property with legal recourse for noncompliance.
Without a legal system that enforces contracts, people would be unlikely to enter into agreements due to the risk of non-payment, slowing economic growth.
The World Bank ranks countries' legal systems based on effective protection of property rights and rule-based governance, with low rankings associated with lower GDP per capita.
Countries lacking a clear system of property rights and an independent court system free from corruption have lagged in economic growth.

learning_objectives: Identify the role of labor productivity in promoting economic growth
Analyze the sources of economic growth using the aggregate production function
Measure an economy’s rate of productivity growth
Evaluate the power of sustained growth

knowledge_units: Sustained long-term economic growth comes from increases in worker productivity.
Labor productivity is the value that each employed person creates per unit of their input.
Human capital, the accumulated knowledge, skills, and expertise of workers, is a determinant of labor productivity.
Technological change, combining invention and innovation, is a determinant of labor productivity.
Economies of scale, the cost advantages from industry size, is a determinant of labor productivity.

An aggregate production function describes the technical relationship by which economic inputs like labor, machinery, and raw materials are turned into outputs like goods and services for the entire economy.
A microeconomic production function describes a firm's or industry's inputs and outputs, while an aggregate production function applies to the entire economy.
The aggregate production function relates inputs like workforce, human capital, physical capital, and technology to output such as GDP.
Human capital per person, physical capital per person, and technology per person are inputs that produce GDP per capita.
An economy's rate of productivity growth is closely linked to the growth rate of its GDP per capita, but the two are not identical.
Over the long term, the only way that GDP per capita can grow continually is if the productivity of the average worker rises or if there are complementary increases in capital.
A common measure of U.S. productivity per worker is dollar value per hour the worker contributes to the employer’s output.
This measure excludes government workers because their output is not sold in the market and so their productivity is hard to measure.
The 'new economy' hypothesis suggests that advances in communications and information technology in the 1990s could generate higher average productivity growth for decades.
Pessimists argue that a few years of stronger productivity growth does not prove that higher productivity will last for the long term.
Productivity growth is closely linked to the average level of wages because firms' willingness to pay workers depends on the value of the output those workers produce.
In the long run, productivity per hour is the most important determinant of the average wage level in any economy.
The OECD tracks data on the annual growth rate of real GDP per hour worked to compare economies' productivity growth.
Sustained economic growth is the most important factor for improving people's standard of living.
Small differences in growth rates, when compounded over long periods, lead to enormous differences in GDP per capita.
The formula for calculating future GDP is GDP at starting date × (1 + growth rate of GDP)^years = GDP at end date.
Compound growth rates behave similarly to compound interest rates, where growth is applied to a base that includes past growth.
The rate of productivity growth is the primary determinant of an economy's long-term economic growth and higher wages.
An aggregate production function specifies how inputs like human capital, physical capital, and technology lead to GDP per capita output.

learning_objectives: Discuss the components of economic growth, including physical capital, human capital, and technology
Explain capital deepening and its significance
Analyze the methods employed in economic growth accounting studies
Identify factors that contribute to a healthy climate for economic growth

knowledge_units: Small differences in annual economic growth rates lead to large differences in GDP per capita over time.
Physical capital includes plant, equipment, and infrastructure, and it can increase output through greater quantity or improved quality.
Human capital refers to the skills and knowledge that make workers productive, and investing in it yields higher future productivity.
Technology includes inventions, innovations, new organizational methods, and institutions that increase output quantity and quality.
GDP per capita is used to compare living standards across countries by accounting for population size differences.
A per capita production function divides inputs by population to show how human capital, physical capital, and technology per person affect GDP per capita.
Capital deepening refers to an increase in the level of capital per person in a society.
Capital deepening can apply to both human capital per worker and physical capital per worker.
Human capital deepening in the U.S. economy is primarily driven by increases in education and training rather than work experience.
Physical capital per worker in the United States has increased over time, as shown by rising real dollar amounts.
Economic growth can be fostered by investing in human capital, physical capital, and technology, as demonstrated by South Korea's development.
Growth accounting studies use an aggregate production function to estimate how much of per capita economic growth can be attributed to growth in physical capital and human capital.
The part of economic growth unexplained by measured inputs of physical and human capital is attributed to growth in technology.
Technology is typically the most important contributor to U.S. economic growth, often explaining more than half of the growth.
Building human capital is at least as important as investment in physical capital for economic growth.
Human capital, physical capital, and technology work together to drive economic growth.
In low-income countries, girls who receive more education tend to have fewer, healthier, and better-educated children.
Education for girls in low-income countries is an investment in economic growth with benefits beyond the current generation.
A healthy economic climate requires a market orientation that provides incentives for individuals and firms to increase human and physical capital.
Markets that allow personal and business rewards encourage overall macroeconomic growth through individual and firm decisions.
A market orientation typically includes openness to international trade.
Government plays a role in correcting market failures and guiding markets toward beneficial outcomes.
Government can facilitate capital deepening and technology through policies in education, savings and investment, infrastructure, special economic zones, and scientific research.

A healthy climate for growth in GDP per capita includes human capital deepening, physical capital deepening, and technological gains operating in a market-oriented economy with supportive government policies.

learning_objectives: Explain economic convergence
Analyze various arguments for and against economic convergence
Evaluate the speed of economic convergence between high-income countries and the rest of the world

knowledge_units: Economic convergence is a pattern where low-income and middle-income economies grow faster than high-income economies.
Countries in the 'fast growth club' averaged GDP growth of at least 5% per year in both the 1990s and 2010s, potentially converging with high-income countries.
Countries in the 'slow growth club' averaged GDP growth of 2% per year or less in both time periods.
Low-income countries tend to have faster GDP growth than middle-income countries, which in turn grow faster than high-income countries, supporting the convergence pattern.
The persistence of economic convergence into the future is a controversial question among economists.
Diminishing marginal returns to human and physical capital suggest that low-income countries can achieve larger marginal gains from capital deepening than high-income countries.
Low-income countries may find it easier to improve their technologies by adopting existing innovations, a concept known as 'the advantages of backwardness.'.
Countries can learn from the experiences of faster-growing economies and may develop market-friendly institutions as they enjoy higher living standards.
If economic growth depended only on capital deepening, diminishing marginal returns would slow growth over time.
Technology can offset diminishing marginal returns from capital deepening by shifting the aggregate production function upward.
Most growing economies simultaneously deepen capital and improve technology, allowing sustained increases in GDP per capita.
Technological improvements have not shown diminishing returns over the last two centuries because new ideas can be applied at low marginal cost.
The ability of low-income countries to copy and adapt technology depends on supportive economic, educational, and public policy institutions.
Economic convergence between high-income and lower-income countries will proceed slowly even under optimistic growth scenarios.
Small differences in annual economic growth rates accumulate into large differences in GDP per capita over time.
High-income countries have built their standard of living advantages over decades or more than a century.
Modern economic growth is associated with increased calorie consumption due to rising incomes.
Technological progress combined with physical and human capital deepening drives modern economic growth and convergence.
Food shortages and famine are often caused by failures in government macroeconomic policy rather than insufficient food production.
As incomes rise, individuals tend to demand more calories from fats and protein and fewer from carbohydrates.
Convergence occurs when lower-income countries catch up to higher-income countries in GDP per capita.
New investment in physical and human capital can yield larger marginal gains in lower-income countries than in higher-income countries.
Higher-income countries experience diminishing returns to investments in human and physical capital.
Continuous technological innovation in high-income economies can counterbalance diminishing returns to capital investment.

learning_objectives: How Economists Define and Compute Unemployment Rate
Patterns of Unemployment
What Causes Changes in Unemployment over the Short Run
What Causes Changes in Unemployment over the Long Run

knowledge_units: A photograph shows industrial robots palletizing food products in a factory.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
The U.S. unemployment rate increased from 4.4% to 14.8% between March and April 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
The COVID-19 pandemic caused labor force participation to decline and remain below pre-pandemic levels as of early 2022.
Unemployment imposes financial stress and personal hardships on individuals and their families.
High unemployment represents an economic cost to society because it means unused labor resources and lost potential output.
This chapter will explain how economists define and compute the unemployment rate.

learning_objectives: Calculate the labor force participation rate and the unemployment rate
Explain hidden unemployment and what it means to be in or out of the labor force
Evaluate the collection and interpretation of unemployment data

knowledge_units: The unemployment rate is reported as a percentage of the labor force.
Small changes in the unemployment rate represent large numbers of people because the labor force is large.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics is the official source for U.S. unemployment data.
The labor force consists of the employed and the unemployed.
To be classified as unemployed, a person must be without a job, currently available to work, and actively looking for work in the previous four weeks.
A person who does not have a job but is not currently available to work or has not actively looked for work in the last four weeks is counted as out of the labor force.
People who are out of the labor force include those not working and not looking for work due to reasons such as retirement, looking after children, or discouragement from job search.
The labor force participation rate is calculated by dividing the number of people in the labor force by the total adult population and multiplying by 100.
The unemployment rate is calculated by dividing the number of unemployed people by the total labor force and multiplying by 100.
The unemployment rate represents the percentage of adults in the labor force who do not have jobs, not the percentage of the total adult population without jobs.
Hidden unemployment includes individuals who are employed part-time or temporarily but are seeking full-time permanent employment.
Underemployed individuals, such as those working in jobs that do not utilize their skills or education, are counted as employed but represent hidden unemployment.
Discouraged workers who have stopped looking for employment are no longer counted as unemployed and fall under hidden unemployment.
The labor force participation rate is the percentage of adults who are either employed or unemployed and looking for a job.
The labor force participation rate is calculated by dividing the number of people in the labor force by the total adult population and multiplying by 100.
The U.S. labor force participation rate peaked in the late 1990s and has generally declined since, with notable drops during the Great Recession and the COVID-19 pandemic.
The establishment payroll survey is based on a survey of about 147,000 businesses and government agencies to estimate payroll employment.
The payroll survey generates estimates for all employees, average weekly hours worked, and average hourly, weekly, and overtime earnings.
A criticism of the establishment payroll survey is that it does not count the self-employed.
The establishment payroll survey does not distinguish between new, minimum wage, part-time, or temporary jobs and full-time jobs with higher pay.
The U.S. unemployment rate is based on the Current Population Survey (CPS), which has been conducted monthly since 1940.
The CPS survey is designed to be representative by dividing the country into 3,137 areas and selecting 729 of them for sampling.
The survey interviews about 60,000 households monthly, using a rotating sample where households are interviewed for four months, out for eight months, then interviewed again for four months.
The CPS provides detailed unemployment statistics by state, industry, urban/rural areas, gender, age, race/ethnicity, and education level.
The CPS measures the percentage of the labor force that is unemployed, while the establishment payroll survey (EPS) measures net job changes.
Discouraged workers who have stopped looking for jobs are counted as out of the labor force, not as unemployed.
Some individuals counted as unemployed may not be actively seeking work and could more accurately be classified as out of the labor force.
Some individuals may report being unemployed while actually working in unreported jobs.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics publishes various surveys to develop a more nuanced view of the labor market beyond the unemployment rate.
Even imperfect measures like the unemployment rate can be informative when interpreted knowledgeably.

learning_objectives: Explain historical patterns of unemployment in the U.S.
Identify trends of unemployment based on demographics
Evaluate global unemployment rates

knowledge_units: Let’s look at how unemployment rates have changed over time and how various groups of people are affected by unemployment differently.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
U.S. unemployment rates fluctuate over time, reaching roughly 10% during deep recessions in the early 1980s, 2007–2009, and 2021.
Unemployment rates were historically low in the late 1990s and mid-2000s, staying below 5% for extended periods.
The unemployment rate never falls to zero and rarely stays below 3% for long.
Rises and falls in unemployment correlate with economic cycles, though unemployment tends to lag changes in economic activity, especially during upswings after recessions.
There is no significant long-term upward or downward trend in U.S. unemployment rates despite population growth and increased labor force participation.
Unemployment is not distributed evenly across the U.S. population by gender, age, and race/ethnicity.
Female unemployment rates were generally higher than male rates until the early 1990s.
Younger workers tend to have higher unemployment rates than middle-aged workers due to more frequent job transitions.
Elderly workers have extremely low unemployment rates because those without jobs often exit the labor force through retirement.
The unemployment rate for African-Americans is substantially higher than for other racial or ethnic groups, reflecting patterns of discrimination.
Gaps in unemployment rates between White, Black, and Hispanic people diminished in the 1990s.
Those with less education typically suffer higher unemployment because additional education offers better labor market connections and higher demand.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics categorizes unemployment by reason, including new entrants, re-entrants, job leavers, and job losers (temporary and non-temporary).
Unemployment duration is measured in categories such as under 5 weeks, 5 to 14 weeks, 15 to 26 weeks, and over 27 weeks.
The U.S. unemployment rate typically appears lower than the average rate among other high-income countries.
Cross-country comparisons of unemployment rates require caution due to differences in definitions, measurement tools, and labor market structures.
Japan's low reported unemployment rate may not accurately reflect its labor market health because workers often exit the labor force after job loss and firms retain underemployed workers.
Comparing unemployment rates between high-income and low-income countries is difficult due to differences in statistical capabilities and the nature of labor market participation.
In many low-income countries, most workers are not formally employed, making unemployment manifest differently than in high-income countries.
Unemployed workers in high-income countries have access to government support programs, which are often minimal or absent in low-income countries.

learning_objectives: Analyze cyclical unemployment
Explain the relationship between sticky wages and employment using various economic arguments
Apply supply and demand models to unemployment and wages

knowledge_units: Unemployment varies across times and places.
The causes of changes in unemployment differ between the short run and the long run.
Cyclical unemployment is the variation in unemployment caused by the economy moving through the business cycle, from expansion to recession or vice versa.
In the short run, the labor supply curve is assumed to be relatively stable, meaning the quantity of hours people are willing to work for a given wage does not change much.

A primary determinant of labor demand is firms' perception of the macro economy; if firms believe the economy is expanding, the labor demand curve shifts right, and if they perceive a recession, it shifts left.
In a competitive and flexible labor market model, unemployment is a puzzle because the market should move toward an equilibrium where labor supplied equals labor demanded.
Involuntary unemployment occurs when individuals are willing to work at wages similar to those of employed peers, but employers are not hiring, indicating a lack of available jobs.
Wages may be sticky downward due to economic laws and institutions such as minimum wage laws and union contracts.
Implicit contracts between employers and employees act as insurance, leading firms to hesitate cutting wages to avoid worker betrayal and reduced effort.
Efficiency wage theory suggests employers pay above-market wages to motivate workers and reduce turnover costs.
The adverse selection of wage cuts argument states that wage cuts cause the best workers to leave, so firms prefer layoffs over across-the-board wage reductions.
The insider-outsider model argues that cutting wages alienates insiders who are essential for firm productivity and smooth operations.
The relative wage coordination argument indicates workers resist wage cuts because they fear others will not experience similar cuts, worsening their relative position.
When wages are inflexible and unlikely to fall, short-run or long-run unemployment can result.

learning_objectives: Explain frictional and structural unemployment
Assess relationships between the natural rate of employment and potential real GDP, productivity, and public policy
Identify recent patterns in the natural rate of employment
Propose ways to combat unemployment

knowledge_units: The natural rate of unemployment is the level of unemployment that persists even when the economy is healthy.
Cyclical unemployment explains why unemployment rises during recessions and falls during expansions.
The unemployment rate is never zero, even during strong economic growth.
The natural rate of unemployment is the rate resulting from economic, social, and political factors when the economy is neither booming nor in recession.

The natural rate is influenced by factors such as companies expanding and contracting workforces, social and economic forces affecting the labor market, and public policies affecting work eagerness or hiring willingness.
Frictional unemployment occurs as workers move between jobs due to the time needed to find and match with suitable employment.
Frictional unemployment is not inherently negative because it allows for better job matching, which can increase productivity.
The level of frictional unemployment depends on the ease of learning about job opportunities and workers' willingness to relocate.
A population with a higher proportion of young workers tends to have higher frictional unemployment due to greater job mobility.
Structural unemployment occurs when individuals lack skills valued by the labor market due to shifts in demand or insufficient skill acquisition.
Technology can cause structural unemployment by displacing lower-skilled workers while creating demand for higher-skilled workers.
Education and retraining are key to minimizing structural unemployment, as individuals with degrees can adapt to changing skill demands.
The natural unemployment rate is the unemployment rate that exists when the economy is at full employment.
Full employment occurs when the actual unemployment rate equals the natural unemployment rate.
At full employment, real GDP equals potential real GDP.
When the economy is below full employment, the unemployment rate exceeds the natural rate and real GDP is less than potential.
When the economy is above full employment, the unemployment rate is below the natural rate and real GDP exceeds potential.
Operating above potential real GDP is only sustainable in the short term.
Unexpected shifts in productivity can affect the natural rate of unemployment.
Wages in an economy are ultimately determined by workers' productivity over time.
Adjustments of wages to productivity levels are not immediate due to infrequent wage reviews and difficulty measuring individual productivity.
When productivity changes unexpectedly, it can create a gap between labor supply and demand, temporarily raising the natural rate of unemployment.
The U.S. economy in the 1970s experienced unexpectedly low productivity growth, leading to higher unemployment as wages adjusted slowly.
Over time, wages eventually adjust to reflect productivity levels, causing the natural rate of unemployment to return to normal.
Public policy can affect the natural rate of unemployment by influencing the opportunity cost of being unemployed for workers.
The duration of unemployment benefits matters more than their generosity in affecting the incentive to seek work.
Government assistance for job search or retraining can encourage people to return to work sooner.
Unemployment insurance is a joint federal–state program funded by a federal tax on employers.
Most states limit unemployment benefits to 26 weeks, with possible extensions during high unemployment.
An individual does not have to collect unemployment benefits to be classified as unemployed.
Government regulations, such as bureaucratic red tape, can discourage businesses from hiring and contribute to a higher natural rate of unemployment.
Laws that restrict business operations, like zoning or Sunday closing laws, can create mismatches between willing workers and employers, increasing unemployment.
Difficulties in firing workers may lead businesses to hire fewer workers than needed.
High minimum wages may discourage businesses from hiring low-skill workers.
The natural rate of unemployment can change over time due to underlying economic, social, and political factors.
The internet and social networking sites have reduced frictional unemployment by making job searches easier.
The growth of the temporary worker industry has reduced the natural rate of unemployment by providing jobs and serving as a clearinghouse for permanent positions.
The aging of the baby boom generation has reduced the natural rate of unemployment because older workers experience lower unemployment than younger workers.
The natural rate of unemployment was lower in the 1990s and early 2000s compared to the 1980s due to these combined factors.
The natural rate of unemployment in Europe has been higher than in the United States since the 1970s due to different underlying conditions in labor supply and demand.
Generous welfare and unemployment benefits in Europe can contribute to a higher natural rate of unemployment.
Laws requiring lengthy notice periods and substantial severance packages for layoffs discourage both firing and hiring, increasing the natural rate of unemployment.
The prevalence of labor market regulations in Europe, compared to the United States, leads to higher unemployment rates even during economic growth.
The remedy for unemployment depends on diagnosing its type, such as cyclical unemployment caused by recessions.
Governments can combat cyclical unemployment by stimulating overall buying power to encourage firms to hire.
The natural rate of unemployment is influenced by factors beyond government control, such as population age structure and productivity shifts.
Government policies can affect the natural rate of unemployment by influencing information and incentives for workers and employers.
Governments may need to reassess labor market programs to avoid unduly discouraging labor supply or demand.
The natural rate of unemployment includes frictional unemployment from job changes and structural unemployment from permanent shifts in job skills.

learning_objectives: Tracking Inflation
How to Measure Changes in the Cost of Living
How the U.S. and Other Countries Experience Inflation
The Confusion Over Inflation
Indexing and Its Limitations

knowledge_units: The image shows a photograph of Zimbabwean currency.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
Inflation is a general and ongoing rise in the level of prices in an entire economy.
Hyperinflation is an extreme form of inflation where prices rise at an extraordinarily high rate.
In Zimbabwe between 2008 and 2009, hyperinflation reached a rate of 79.6 billion percent, causing prices to double daily.
During hyperinflation, people rush to spend currency because it rapidly loses value.
Hyperinflation can lead to government printing money to pay bills, price controls, shortages, and black markets.
Inflation is distinct from a relative price change, which involves the price of one good rising while another falls.
Inflation implies an ongoing rise in prices, not a one-time shift in equilibrium.
This chapter explains how to combine prices of individual goods and services to create a measure of overall inflation.

learning_objectives: Calculate the annual rate of inflation
Explain and use index numbers and base years when simplifying the total quantity spent over a year for products
Calculate inflation rates using index numbers

knowledge_units: Inflation is a general rise in the level of all prices in an economy.
The inflation rate is the percentage change in the price level.
Economists combine prices of a variety of goods and services into a single price level to measure inflation.
Inflation affects not only goods and services but also wages and income levels.
Economists calculate the price level using a basket of goods and services that represents typical purchases.
A weighted average of prices is used to construct an overall price level, with weights based on actual quantities purchased.
Changes in prices of goods that account for a larger share of income have a greater impact on the overall price level.
The annual rate of inflation is calculated as the percentage change in the total cost of purchasing a basket of goods between time periods.
The percentage change formula is (Level in new year – Level in previous year) / Level in previous year × 100.
Economists use index numbers to simplify the interpretation of price levels for complex baskets of goods.
A base year is arbitrarily chosen as the starting point for an index number and is assigned a value of 100.
Index numbers are calculated by dividing the dollar amount spent in a given year by the dollar amount spent in the base year and multiplying by 100.
Index numbers have no units because the dollar signs cancel out during calculation.
The inflation rate can be calculated using the percentage change formula applied to index numbers.
Index numbers allow for easier comparison of inflation rates over time than raw dollar amounts.
The inflation rate is not derived by simply subtracting index numbers but by calculating the percentage change between them.
The choice of a base year is arbitrary and does not affect the calculated inflation rate between periods.

learning_objectives: Use the Consumer Price Index (CPI) to calculate U.S. inflation rates
Identify several ways the Bureau of Labor Statistics avoids biases in the Consumer Price Index (CPI)
Differentiate among the Consumer Price Index (CPI), the Producer Price Index (PPI), the International Price Index, the Employment Cost Index, and the GDP deflator.

knowledge_units: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is the most commonly cited measure of inflation in the United States.
The CPI is calculated based on the prices in a fixed basket of goods and services representing the purchases of an average family of four.

Substitution bias occurs because a fixed basket does not account for consumers substituting away from goods whose relative prices have risen, causing the CPI to overstate the true cost of living increase.

Quality/new goods bias occurs because a fixed basket does not account for improvements in product quality or the introduction of new goods, causing the CPI to overstate the true cost of living increase.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) determines the CPI basket using the Consumer Expenditure Survey, which divides spending into eight major groups and over 200 item categories.
The CPI basket consists of about 80,000 specific products, with about one-quarter of the sample rotated each year.
Data collectors visit or call about 23,000 stores monthly to collect prices for the CPI.
The CPI is calculated by combining prices of individual products using weights based on quantities purchased, allowing for factors like substitution and quality improvements.
A core inflation index is derived from the CPI by excluding volatile variables like energy and food prices to better show underlying inflation trends.
The core inflation index helps guide monetary policy by measuring persistent underlying inflation rather than transitory price changes.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics uses alternative mathematical methods to allow for substitution between goods when calculating the CPI.
The BLS updates the basket of goods behind the CPI more frequently to include new and improved goods more rapidly.
For certain products, the BLS conducts studies to measure quality improvements, such as adjusting computer prices for changes in speed, memory, and screen size.
Adjustments for quality improvements are imperfect and often controversial among economists.
Since the early 2000s, substitution and quality/new goods biases have been somewhat reduced, so the CPI probably overstates true inflation by about 0.5% per year.
Over a decade or two, even a small annual overstatement compounds to a more significant amount.
The CPI tracks prices from physical locations and does not include lower prices from online sites like Amazon.

A tradeoff exists between simplicity and interpretation when measuring inflation: a fixed basket is straightforward but suffers from biases, while an evolving basket reduces biases but increases complexity.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) represents an average U.S. household's consumption and may not capture inflation experienced by specific demographic or geographic groups.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics publishes experimental price indices tailored for specific groups, geographic areas, or categories of goods.
The Producer Price Index (PPI) measures prices paid for supplies and inputs by producers of goods and services.
The GDP deflator is a price index that includes all GDP components and uses a basket that is not fixed but recalculated based on the current year's GDP.
The Employment Cost Index measures wage inflation in the labor market.
The International Price Index is based on the prices of exported or imported merchandise.
The GDP deflator is considered the most accurate measure of overall inflation because it includes prices of all domestically produced goods and services.
The CPI is the most accurate measure of inflation impacting households, as it only includes prices of products purchased by households.
The 'best' measure of inflation depends on the intended use of the data.

learning_objectives: Identify patterns of inflation for the United States using data from the Consumer Price Index
Identify patterns of inflation on an international level

knowledge_units: In the U.S. economy over the last three decades, inflation has been relatively low, with the Consumer Price Index typically rising 2% to 4% per year.
Over the twentieth century, the U.S. experienced several periods of double-digit inflation, but nothing close to hyperinflation.
Inflation as measured by the Consumer Price Index reflects the annual percentage change in the cost of a basket of goods and services for the average consumer.
The first two waves of inflation in the U.S. occurred right after World War I and World War II.
Two periods of severe deflation occurred in the early twentieth century: one following the 1920-21 recession and another during the Great Depression of the 1930s.
From 1900 to about 1960, major inflations and deflations nearly balanced, resulting in an average annual inflation rate of about 1%.
A third wave of more severe inflation occurred in the 1970s and ended in the early 1980s.
Recessions or depressions often correspond with lower inflation rates, as seen in the 1920-21 recession, the Great Depression, the 1980-82 recession, and the 2008-2009 Great Recession.
High unemployment during recessions reduces total demand for goods, pulling the price level down.
Rapid economic growth, such as after wartime or during the 1960s, often brings lower unemployment but higher inflation.
Many industrialized countries experienced relatively high inflation rates in the 1970s.
Inflation rates in the United States and Europe declined in the 1980s and have largely remained low since.
From the early 1990s to 2020, inflation was consistently below 5 percent in the United States, Japan, Germany, and the United Kingdom.
Brazil experienced extreme inflation, with rates spiking to 3,000 percent in 1990.
Russia's inflation rate was near 1,000 percent in 1993 but declined to 50 percent by 1995.
China's inflation rate remained very low, between 0 and 3 percent, from 1998 to 2020.
In the early 2000s, the problem of inflation diminished for most countries compared to previous decades.
Zimbabwe experienced hyperinflation in the early 2000s, with currency becoming nearly worthless.

learning_objectives: Explain how inflation can cause redistributions of purchasing power
Identify ways inflation can blur the perception of supply and demand
Explain the economic benefits and challenges of inflation

knowledge_units: Economists generally oppose high inflation but with less intensity than non-economists.
Surveys show that a majority of the U.S. population considers preventing high inflation a highly important national priority.
Professional economists are less likely to fully agree that preventing high inflation is a highly important national priority compared to the general public.
If all prices, wages, and interest rates adjust automatically and immediately with inflation, no one's purchasing power, profits, or real loan payments would change.
Inflation can cause unintended redistributions of purchasing power when other economic variables do not move exactly in sync with inflation.
Inflation can blur price signals when other economic variables do not adjust for inflation immediately.
Inflation can cause difficulties in long-term planning when other economic variables adjust for inflation only after a time lag.
Inflation can cause redistributions of purchasing power that hurt some people and help others.
People holding cash or financial assets with nominal returns that do not keep up with inflation lose purchasing power.
Taxes on nominal interest income can worsen the negative effects of inflation on real returns.
Wage increases may lag behind inflation, causing workers to lose purchasing power in the short term.
Borrowers with fixed interest rates can benefit from inflation because they repay loans with dollars that have less purchasing power.
Lenders with fixed interest rates are hurt by inflation because they receive repayment in dollars worth less than expected.
Inflation can make economic rewards and penalties seem arbitrary and unfair, undermining public acceptance of market outcomes.
Inflation blurs price signals, making it harder to perceive information about supply and demand conditions.
High inflation can lead to practical difficulties, such as stores not posting prices directly on items due to frequent changes.
Uncertain price levels and changes make it harder for businesses and individuals to react to economic signals.
High and variable inflation weakens incentives to adjust in response to price changes, leading to slower and more erratic market adjustments toward equilibrium.
Inflation makes long-term planning difficult because future inflation rates are unknown.
Businesses can profit from inflation by delaying payments and accelerating collections, but this diverts focus from improving products and services.
High inflation rewards businesses skilled at profiting from inflation rather than those excelling in productivity or innovation.
Variable inflation may lead businesses to adopt shorter-term strategies.
Moderate inflation (less than 3% per year) may not prevent healthy economic growth, but evidence on its impact on productivity is controversial.
U.S. productivity growth slowed when inflation increased in the 1970s and improved when inflation decreased in the 1980s.
The impact of inflation varies significantly depending on whether it is creeping, galloping, or hyperinflation.
Low inflation is preferable to deflation, which occurs during severe recessions.
Moderate inflation may help the economy by making wages in labor markets more flexible, potentially aiding necessary real wage declines.
Unexpected inflation tends to hurt those with fixed wages or interest payments, while it can benefit debtors who repay with less valuable dollars.
High rates of inflation can muddle price signals in the short term and complicate long-term savings and investment decisions.

learning_objectives: Explain the relationship between indexing and inflation
Identify three ways the government can control inflation through macroeconomic policy

knowledge_units: Indexing refers to the automatic adjustment of prices, wages, or interest rates according to inflation.
An indexed payment increases based on the index number that measures inflation.
Indexing reduces the negative effects of inflation by aligning different parts of the economy, such as prices and wages, with inflation.
Cost-of-living adjustments (COLAs) are a form of indexing that automatically increase wages to keep up with inflation.
Adjustable-rate mortgages (ARMs) are loans where the interest rate varies with the inflation rate, protecting lenders against inflation risk.
Long-term business contracts may include automatic price adjustments for inflation, effectively setting a real price rather than a nominal price.
Indexing government programs to inflation prevents bracket creep by automatically adjusting income tax brackets upward with inflation.
Social Security benefits are indexed to rise annually with the Consumer Price Index.
The Social Security tax base is adjusted upward each year according to the rate of inflation.
Indexed bonds guarantee a real rate of interest above the inflation rate, protecting investors from inflation risk.
Indexing can reduce concerns about inflation by protecting individuals, firms, and government programs from arbitrary redistributions and other effects of inflation.
Indexing is always partial, as not all employers, companies, interest rates, or financial instruments are fully indexed against inflation.
Partial inflation indexing may reduce political opposition to inflation because those who are indexed have less reason to care about inflation increases.
In a partially indexed economy, financially savvy businesses and investors may seek inflation protection, while the financially unsophisticated and small businesses may be more affected by inflation.
Inflation is caused by too many dollars chasing too few goods.
Government spending during wars can lead to inflation due to high demand and limited supply.
After wartime price controls end, pent-up buying power can drive up inflation.
Slowdowns in economic activity are typically associated with reduced inflation or deflation.
To avoid inflation, purchasing power in the economy must grow at roughly the same rate as the production of goods.
Macroeconomic policies involving taxes, spending, and regulation of interest rates and credit can influence inflation levels.
A payment is indexed if it is automatically adjusted for inflation.
Examples of indexing in the private sector include wage contracts with cost-of-living adjustments (COLAs) and adjustable-rate mortgages (ARMs).
Examples of indexing in the public sector include tax brackets and Social Security payments.

chapter_title: The International Trade and Capital Flows

learning_objectives: Measuring Trade Balances
Trade Balances in Historical and International Context
Trade Balances and Flows of Financial Capital
The National Saving and Investment Identity
The Pros and Cons of Trade Deficits and Surpluses
The Difference between Level of Trade and the Trade Balance

knowledge_units: A photograph shows many different currencies from around the world, including the U.S. (dollars), UK (pounds), and Europe (euros).
Asses the complexity of international trade.
The balance of trade is the gap between a nation's dollar value of exports and its dollar value of imports.
If exports exceed imports, the economy has a trade surplus.
If imports exceed exports, the economy has a trade deficit.
A trade deficit does not necessarily indicate poor economic performance, as seen in the United States where deficits can worsen during economic strength.
A trade surplus does not necessarily indicate good economic performance, as seen in the Democratic Republic of the Congo where high poverty persists despite a surplus.
Large trade deficits can trigger financial crises when foreign investors become pessimistic and move their money out of the country.
Financial crises triggered by unbalanced trade can lead to deep recessions with significant drops in real GDP.
International flows of goods and services are intimately connected to international flows of financial capital.

learning_objectives: Explain merchandise trade balance, current account balance, and unilateral transfers
Identify components of the U.S. current account balance
Calculate the merchandise trade balance and current account balance using import and export data for a country

knowledge_units: The merchandise trade balance measures the solid or physical items transported between countries.
In most high-income economies, services comprise more than half of a country's total production.
Technological advances have enabled international trade in services such as finance, software, and management consulting.
Economists typically rely on broader measures like the current account balance, which includes other international flows of income and foreign aid.
The current account balance includes exports and imports of goods and services, income from investments, and unilateral transfers.
The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) compiles U.S. trade statistics using data from the Census Bureau, surveys, and cross-checks with other sources.
The U.S. runs a surplus in trade in services, which has grown in importance relative to goods trade.
Income payments in the current account balance refer to money received by U.S. investors on foreign investments and payments to foreign investors on U.S. investments.
Unilateral transfers are payments made abroad without receiving a direct good or service, such as foreign aid or charitable donations.
Unilateral transfers are treated as imports in the current account balance because they represent money flowing out of the country.
The merchandise trade balance is calculated as the difference between exports of goods and imports of goods.
The current account balance is calculated by summing the balances of goods, services, income payments, and unilateral transfers.

learning_objectives: Analyze graphs of the current account balance and the merchandise trade balance
Identify patterns in U.S. trade surpluses and deficits
Compare the U.S. trade surpluses and deficits to other countries' trade surpluses and deficits

knowledge_units: The current account balance and merchandise trade balance can be analyzed in nominal dollar terms or as a share of GDP.
Expressing trade balances as a share of GDP factors out inflation and real economic growth.
The U.S. has consistently run trade deficits in recent years, while Japan and many European nations have run trade surpluses.
Most countries have trade surpluses or deficits that are less than 5% of GDP.
The United States experienced large trade surpluses in the early 1980s, a tiny trade surplus in 1991, and even larger trade deficits in the late 1990s and early 2000s.
A trade deficit necessarily means a net inflow of financial capital from abroad.
A trade surplus necessarily means a net outflow of financial capital from an economy to other countries.

learning_objectives: Explain the connection between trade balances and financial capital flows
Calculate comparative advantage
Explain balanced trade in terms of investment and capital flows

knowledge_units: Trade surpluses and deficits can be good or bad depending on circumstances.
International flows of goods and services are connected with international flows of financial capital.
The connection between trade balances and financial capital flows can be illustrated using a parable and a circular flow diagram.
In a two-person economy, voluntary trade ensures that each person's exports equal their imports, resulting in balanced trade.
A trade imbalance arises when one party provides goods or services now in exchange for future repayment, creating a trade surplus for the lender and a trade deficit for the borrower.
A trade surplus is equivalent to an outflow of financial capital, while a trade deficit is equivalent to an inflow of financial capital.
Comparative advantage is determined by lower opportunity cost, not absolute advantage in production efficiency.
The balance of trade is closely connected to international flows of financial capital, leading economists to describe it as the balance of payments.
Each category of the current account balance corresponds to a flow of payments between a country and the rest of the world.
Flows of goods and services appear in the current account, while flows of funds appear in the financial account.
Investment income is recorded in the current account, while the underlying investments are recorded in the financial account.
International flows of financial capital include private investments such as real estate, companies, stocks, and bonds, not just government debt.
A current account deficit indicates a country is a net borrower from the rest of the world.
A current account surplus indicates a country is a net lender to the rest of the world.

learning_objectives: Explain the determinants of trade and current account balance
Identify and calculate supply and demand for financial capital
Explain how a nation's own level of domestic saving and investment determines a nation's balance of trade
Predict the rising and falling of trade deficits based on a nation's saving and investment identity

knowledge_units: Trade balances are closely connected to international flows of savings and investments.
Macroeconomic analysis views trade balances in the context of overall levels of savings and financial investment in the economy.
The national saving and investment identity states that the supply of financial capital must equal the demand for financial capital.
The supply of financial capital comes from domestic savings (S) and the inflow of foreign capital, which equals the trade deficit (M – X).
The demand for financial capital comes from private investment (I) and government borrowing when spending exceeds taxes (G – T).
The national savings and investment identity is expressed as S + (M – X) = I + (G – T).
A government budget deficit (G > T) makes the government a demander of financial capital, while a budget surplus (T > G) makes it a supplier.
A trade deficit (M > X) represents an inflow of foreign capital that increases the supply of financial capital, while a trade surplus (X > M) represents an outflow.
Domestic savings always contribute to the supply of financial capital, and domestic investment always contributes to the demand for financial capital.
A nation's own levels of domestic saving and investment determine its balance of trade.
A trade deficit occurs when domestic investment exceeds the sum of private domestic saving and government saving.
A trade surplus occurs when the sum of private domestic saving and government saving exceeds domestic investment.
The balance of trade is a fundamentally macroeconomic phenomenon determined by national saving and investment, not by sectoral performance or trade policies.
The national saving and investment identity states that domestic investment minus private domestic savings minus public domestic savings equals the trade deficit.
If domestic investment rises while private and public savings remain unchanged, the trade deficit must increase.
If private domestic savings rises while domestic investment and public savings remain unchanged, the trade deficit will decline.
If the government budget deficit increases while domestic investment and private savings remain unchanged, the trade deficit will increase.
The saving and investment identity can be used to calculate the change in private domestic savings needed to reduce a trade deficit.
A recession tends to make a trade deficit smaller or a trade surplus larger.
A period of strong economic growth tends to make a trade deficit larger or a trade surplus smaller.
During a recession, a country may purchase fewer imports, which can reduce its trade deficit.
A rapidly growing domestic economy often accompanies a trade deficit or a much lower trade surplus.
A slowing or recessionary domestic economy often accompanies a trade surplus or a much lower trade deficit.
When a trade deficit rises, it necessarily means a greater net inflow of foreign financial capital.
The national saving and investment identity states that the total quantity of financial capital supplied must equal the total quantity demanded.
The inflow of foreign financial capital can be absorbed by reduced private savings, higher domestic investment, or greater government borrowing.

learning_objectives: Identify three ways in which borrowing money or running a trade deficit can result in a healthy economy
Identify three ways in which borrowing money or running a trade deficit can result in a weaker economy

knowledge_units: Flows of international trade are equivalent to flows of international financial capital.
Borrowing can be economically sound when used for investments that generate long-run payoffs.
A national economy can benefit from borrowing abroad if the funds are invested to raise economic growth.
The United States in the nineteenth century borrowed heavily from abroad and invested in projects like railroads, leading to strong economic growth.
South Korea ran trade deficits in the 1970s while investing heavily, leading to rapid economic growth and later trade surpluses.
Borrowing can lead to trouble if funds are not invested in ways that increase productivity.
Rapid outflows of foreign capital can destabilize banking systems and cause recessions, as seen in East Asia in the late 1990s.
A trade surplus does not guarantee robust economic health, as shown by Japan's slow growth since the 1990s.
Trade surpluses do not guarantee economic health.
Trade deficits do not guarantee economic weakness.
The outcome of trade deficits or surpluses depends on whether a government wisely invests the corresponding flows of financial capital.

learning_objectives: Identify three factors that influence a country's level of trade
Differentiate between balance of trade and level of trade

knowledge_units: A nation's level of trade is measured by its exports of goods and services as a share of its GDP.
The balance of trade indicates whether a country is running a trade surplus or trade deficit.
A country can have a high level of trade while also having a near-balance between exports and imports.
A country can have a low level of trade but a large trade deficit.
Three factors that strongly influence a nation's level of trade are the size of its economy, its geographic location, and its history of trade.
Large economies tend to have lower levels of trade because they can trade more internally.
Nations that are neighbors tend to trade more due to lower transportation and communication costs.
A country's level of trade indicates the degree of its economy's globalization.
Trade deficits and trade surpluses can be either good or bad signs for an economy depending on the circumstances.
A trade deficit means a country is attracting foreign financial capital.
A trade surplus means a country is shipping financial capital abroad.
The level of trade is measured by the percentage of exports out of GDP.
The level of trade depends on a country's history of trade, its geography, and the size of its economy.
The balance of trade is the dollar difference between a country's exports and imports.
A country's borrowing can lead to long-term economic growth if invested in productivity-boosting investments.

learning_objectives: Macroeconomic Perspectives on Demand and Supply
Building a Model of Aggregate Supply and Aggregate Demand
Shifts in Aggregate Supply
Shifts in Aggregate Demand
How the AS–AD Model Incorporates Growth, Unemployment, and Inflation
Keynes’ Law and Say’s Law in the AS–AD Model

knowledge_units: This photograph shows a new house under construction.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
The U.S. housing market grew between 1990 and 2006, with homeownership rates rising from 64% to over 69%.
The housing bubble and financial market crisis were major contributors to the Great Recession, which led to unemployment rates over 10% and falling GDP.
The aggregate demand–aggregate supply model is introduced to understand why economies expand and contract over time.
The AS–AD model analyzes the relationships between economic growth, unemployment, and inflation.
Shifts in aggregate demand or aggregate supply affect macroeconomic equilibrium.

learning_objectives: Explain Say’s Law and understand why it primarily applies in the long run
Explain Keynes’ Law and understand why it primarily applies in the short run

knowledge_units: Macroeconomists are divided into two groups: those who argue supply is the most important determinant of the macroeconomy and those who argue demand is the most important factor.
Asses the complexity of international trade.
Say's Law states that supply creates its own demand.
The intuition behind Say's Law is that production generates income, which creates equivalent demand elsewhere in the economy.
Modern economists who emphasize supply's role in determining macroeconomic size are called neoclassical economists.
Say's Law is a good approximation for the long run, as productive capacity and total demand grow at roughly the same pace over years or decades.
Over shorter time horizons, recessions can occur where firms face a lack of demand for their products.
Keynes' law states that demand creates its own supply.
Keynes argued that economic downturns like the Great Depression were caused by a lack of aggregate demand, not a reduction in the economy's supply capacity.
Keynes' law applies primarily in the short run, when firms may experience insufficient demand or excess demand.
In the long run, the economy's output is constrained by its supply capacity, including labor, capital, and technology.
Say's Law holds that supply creates its own demand and applies more accurately in the long run.
Keynes' Law holds that demand creates its own supply and applies more accurately in the short run.
An economic approach focused only on supply or only on demand is only partially successful; both must be considered.
The tradeoffs and connections between macroeconomic goals may differ between the short run and the long run due to the differing applicability of Say's Law and Keynes' Law.

learning_objectives: Explain the aggregate supply curve and how it relates to real GDP and potential GDP
Explain the aggregate demand curve and how it is influenced by price levels
Interpret the aggregate demand/aggregate supply model
Identify the point of equilibrium in the aggregate demand/aggregate supply model
Define short run aggregate supply and long run aggregate supply

knowledge_units: The aggregate demand/aggregate supply model shows what determines total supply and total demand for the economy and how they interact.
The aggregate demand/aggregate supply model is used to explain aggregate supply, aggregate demand, and the equilibrium between them.
Aggregate supply (AS) refers to the total quantity of output (real GDP) firms will produce and sell.
The aggregate supply curve shows the total quantity of output firms will produce and sell at each price level.
Potential GDP is the amount of real GDP an economy can produce by fully employing its existing levels of labor, physical capital, and technology.
When output is far below potential GDP, a small increase in the price level can lead to a large increase in aggregate supply due to high unemployment and idle factories.
As output approaches potential GDP, the aggregate supply curve becomes steeper because firms face increasing limits on production capacity.
The aggregate supply curve can cross potential GDP in the short run because firms can temporarily produce beyond full employment through unsustainable efforts.
At the far right of the aggregate supply curve, higher prices cannot increase output because all inputs are fully employed.
Potential GDP is also called full-employment GDP because it corresponds to the natural rate of unemployment.
Aggregate demand (AD) refers to the total spending on domestic goods and services in an economy.
Aggregate demand includes consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports.
The aggregate demand curve shows the total spending on domestic goods and services at each price level.
The aggregate demand curve slopes downward because increases in the price level lead to lower total spending.
The interest rate effect explains that higher prices increase demand for money and credit, raising interest rates, which reduces consumption and investment spending.
The foreign price effect explains that a higher domestic price level makes U.S. goods relatively more expensive, reducing exports and increasing imports, thus lowering net exports.
In the AD/AS model, equilibrium occurs where the aggregate demand curve intersects the aggregate supply curve.
If equilibrium occurs in the flat range of the aggregate supply curve, the economy is not close to potential GDP and experiences unemployment with a stable price level.
If equilibrium occurs in the steep range of the aggregate supply curve, the economy is close to potential GDP and experiences inflationary pressures with low unemployment.
The intersection of the aggregate supply and aggregate demand curves determines the equilibrium level of real GDP and the equilibrium price level.
At a relatively low price level, firms have little incentive to produce, while consumers would purchase a large quantity of output.
As the price level rises, aggregate supply rises and aggregate demand falls until the equilibrium point is reached.
The vertical axis of an aggregate supply and demand diagram represents the overall price level, not the price of a single good.
The horizontal axis of an aggregate supply and demand diagram measures real GDP, not the quantity of a specific good.
The reasons for the slopes of the aggregate supply and demand curves differ from those of microeconomic supply and demand curves.
The short run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve shows a positive relationship between the price level and real GDP in the short run.
Aggregate supply slopes upward because a higher output price level with fixed input prices creates profit opportunities that encourage more production.
In the long run, aggregate supply is represented by a vertical line at potential GDP, which is the maximum output the economy can produce with its existing resources and technology.
The aggregate demand (AD) curve slopes downward due to the wealth effect, the interest rate effect, and the foreign price effect.
The wealth effect means a higher price level reduces real wealth, which decreases consumption.
The interest rate effect means a higher price level increases money demand, raising interest rates and reducing investment spending.
The foreign price effect means a higher domestic price level makes exports relatively more expensive and imports relatively cheaper, reducing net exports.

learning_objectives: Explain how productivity growth changes the aggregate supply curve
Explain how changes in input prices change the aggregate supply curve

knowledge_units: A rightward shift of the aggregate supply curve means producers supply a greater quantity of real GDP at every price level.
A leftward shift of the aggregate supply curve means producers supply a lower quantity of real GDP at every price level.
Productivity growth and changes in input prices are two important factors that can cause shifts in the aggregate supply curve.
Productivity growth is the most important factor shifting the aggregate supply curve in the long run.
Productivity is defined as the amount of output produced with a given quantity of labor.
A higher level of productivity shifts the aggregate supply curve to the right because firms can produce more output at every price level.
Increased productivity raises the level of potential GDP, causing a rightward shift in the long-run aggregate supply curve.
Increases in the prices of widely used inputs like labor and energy shift the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve to the left.
A leftward shift in SRAS due to higher input prices leads to reduced GDP, higher unemployment, and a higher price level.
Declines in the prices of key inputs like oil shift the SRAS curve to the right, encouraging more production at each price level.
A rightward shift in SRAS due to lower input prices can lead to economic expansion, lower unemployment, and reduced inflation.
Changes in input prices generally shift only the SRAS curve, not the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve.
An unexpected early freeze destroying agricultural crops shifts the aggregate supply curve to the left due to fewer agricultural products available at any given price.

Shocks to the labor market, such as an overseas war requiring workers to cease production, shift both short-run and long-run aggregate supply curves to the left because fewer workers are available to produce goods.
A pandemic like COVID-19 reduces the supply of workers through illness and safety concerns, causing a leftward shift in the short-run aggregate supply curve and shortages in goods and services.
The aggregate supply curve shifts right as productivity increases.
The aggregate supply curve shifts left as the price of key inputs rises and shifts right if the price of key inputs falls.
A leftward shift in the aggregate supply curve leads to stagflation, characterized by lower output, higher unemployment, and higher inflation.
A rightward shift in the aggregate supply curve can result in lower inflation, higher output, and lower unemployment.

learning_objectives: Explain how imports influence aggregate demand
Identify ways in which business confidence and consumer confidence can affect aggregate demand
Explain how government policy can change aggregate demand
Evaluate why economists disagree on the topic of tax cuts

knowledge_units: Aggregate demand is composed of consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports (exports minus imports).
A rightward shift of the aggregate demand curve indicates an increase in at least one component of aggregate demand, leading to higher total spending at every price level.
A leftward shift of the aggregate demand curve indicates a decrease in at least one component of aggregate demand, leading to lower total spending at every price level.
Imports are subtracted in the aggregate demand formula because aggregate demand measures demand for domestically produced goods and services.
Changes in consumer or firm behavior and changes in government tax or spending policy are two broad categories that can cause shifts in the aggregate demand curve.
Consumer confidence influences consumption spending, with higher confidence leading to increased consumption.
Business confidence influences investment spending, with higher confidence leading to increased investment.
A rise in consumer or business confidence shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right, increasing output and the price level.
A decline in consumer or business confidence shifts the aggregate demand curve to the left, decreasing output and the price level.
Consumer and business confidence often reflect macroeconomic conditions but can also change due to non-economic factors like political events or public statements.
Higher government spending shifts the aggregate demand curve to the right, while lower government spending shifts it to the left.
Tax cuts for individuals tend to increase consumption demand, shifting the aggregate demand curve to the right.
Tax policy can increase investment demand by offering lower tax rates for corporations or tax reductions for specific investments.
During a recession, tax cuts can shift aggregate demand to the right, increasing real GDP and reducing unemployment.
When the economy is near full employment, tax cuts may shift aggregate demand too far right, causing inflationary pressures with little gain in GDP.
The effect of tax cuts on the economy depends on whether the economy is in a recession or near full employment.
Government spending and tax rate changes are fiscal policy tools that can shift aggregate demand.
Higher interest rates tend to reduce consumption and investment demand, shifting aggregate demand to the left.
Lower interest rates stimulate consumption and investment demand, shifting aggregate demand to the right.
A rightward shift in aggregate demand leads to higher real GDP and upward pressure on the price level.
A leftward shift in aggregate demand leads to lower real GDP and a lower price level.

learning_objectives: Use the aggregate demand/aggregate supply model to show periods of economic growth and recession
Explain how unemployment and inflation impact the aggregate demand/aggregate supply model
Evaluate the importance of the aggregate demand/aggregate supply model

knowledge_units: The AD/AS model shows interlocking relationships between the macroeconomic goals of growth, unemployment, and low inflation.
The AD/AS framework accommodates both the Keynesian focus on aggregate demand in the short run and the classical focus on aggregate supply in the long run.
The AD/AS model illustrates how the three macroeconomic goals of economic growth, low unemployment, and low inflation are achieved.
Long-run economic growth due to productivity increases is represented by a gradual rightward shift of the aggregate supply curve in the AD/AS diagram.
A recession is illustrated in the AD/AS diagram when the equilibrium level of real GDP is substantially below potential GDP.
During periods of economic growth, the equilibrium in the AD/AS diagram is typically close to potential GDP.
Short-run variations in unemployment, known as cyclical unemployment, are caused by the business cycle as the economy expands and contracts.
The natural rate of unemployment is the baseline level of unemployment that occurs year-in and year-out, determined by how well market and government institutions match workers and employers.
