Most real-world choices involve marginal analysis, which compares the benefits and costs of choosing a little more or a little less of a good.
Utility is the satisfaction or benefit that people derive from consuming goods and services.
The law of diminishing marginal utility states that as a person consumes more of a good, the additional (marginal) utility from each additional unit declines.
The law of diminishing marginal utility explains why people rarely make all-or-nothing choices and instead seek a combination of goods.
Sunk costs are costs incurred in the past that cannot be recovered and should not affect current decisions.
The budget constraint framework assumes decisions are forward-looking and ignore sunk costs.
When making a decision, one should focus on the marginal costs and benefits of current and future options rather than past expenditures.
Firms and individuals often struggle with sunk costs because ignoring them means admitting earlier errors in judgment.
The budget constraint model with two goods can be extended to many goods by drawing multiple budget constraints for different pairs of goods.
In advanced economics, mathematical equations with many goods, quantities, and prices can represent the overall budget constraint.
The two-good budget constraint graph illustrates that every choice has an opportunity cost, a principle that applies to real-world decisions.

learning_objectives: Interpret production possibilities frontier graphs
Contrast a budget constraint and a production possibilities frontier
Explain the relationship between a production possibilities frontier and the principle of increasing opportunity cost
Contrast productive efficiency and allocative efficiency
Define comparative advantage

knowledge_units: Society faces constraints due to limited resources, which limits the quantities of goods and services it can produce.
The production possibilities frontier (PPF) illustrates the tradeoff between two goods, showing that producing more of one requires giving up some of the other.
The opportunity cost of producing more of one good is the amount of the other good that must be given up, as shown by the slope of the PPF.
A budget constraint reflects a consumer's purchasing perspective and is a straight line due to relative prices, while a PPF is curved due to increasing opportunity cost.
The PPF can be depicted without specific numbers to represent concepts like opportunity cost and productive efficiency given limited resources.
The production possibilities frontier is curved due to the principle of increasing opportunity cost.
When resources are specialized, transferring them from one production process to another results in imperfect transfers.
As additional resources are added to a good, the initial gains are large with minimal opportunity cost, but opportunity costs gradually increase.
The curvature of the PPF illustrates that the opportunity cost of producing more of one good increases as more resources are already devoted to that good.
The principle of increasing opportunity cost explains the outward-bending shape of the production possibilities frontier.

Productive efficiency means that, given available inputs and technology, it is impossible to produce more of one good without decreasing the quantity produced of another good when all resources are utilized.
All points on the production possibilities frontier display productive efficiency.
Any choice inside the production possibilities frontier is productively inefficient because it is possible to produce more of at least one good without sacrificing another.
Allocative efficiency means that the particular mix of goods a society produces represents the combination that society most desires.
Only one of the productively efficient choices will be the allocatively efficient choice for society as a whole.
Determining allocative efficiency requires more information than what is provided by a production possibilities frontier, such as market data on consumer demand.
A society can expand consumption of all goods by improving productive efficiency to produce on the production possibilities frontier.
Economic growth from increased resources shifts the production possibilities frontier outward, allowing more of all goods.
In the short term, society must choose tradeoffs because efficiency improvements and growth are gradual.
Increases in production of one good typically require offsetting decreases in production of another good.
The slope of a production possibilities frontier gives the opportunity cost of producing one more unit of the good on the x-axis in terms of the good on the y-axis.
Countries tend to have different opportunity costs of producing a specific good due to differences in climates, geography, technology, or skills.
A country has a comparative advantage in producing a good when it can produce that good at a lower opportunity cost than another country.
In the example, Brazil has a comparative advantage in sugar cane because it has a lower opportunity cost of producing sugar cane in terms of wheat than the U.S.
In the example, the U.S. has a comparative advantage in wheat because it has a lower opportunity cost of producing wheat in terms of sugar cane than Brazil.
Orthodox economics argues that countries specialize in producing goods for which they have a comparative advantage and trade for other goods.

learning_objectives: Analyze arguments against orthodox economic approaches to decision-making
Interpret a tradeoff diagram
Contrast normative statements and positive statements

knowledge_units: Arguments against orthodox economic approaches to decision-making typically fall into two categories: that people do not act as the economic model assumes, and that even if they did, they should not.
Understanding the orthodox economic approach to decision-making is distinct from feeling comfortable applying it.
The orthodox economic approach to decision-making assumes more information and careful deliberation than most individuals actually possess.
The orthodox economic approach can still be a useful way to analyze tradeoffs in economic decisions, even if people do not consciously use its tools.
The fact that individuals can make effective economic decisions through practice and experience does not invalidate the analytical tools of orthodox economics.
Orthodox economics describes economic behavior rather than prescribing moral conduct.
Positive statements attempt to describe the world objectively, while normative statements describe it subjectively.
The tradeoff diagram illustrates three basic themes: scarcity, tradeoffs, and economic efficiency.
Scarcity means it is not feasible to have unlimited amounts of both goods.
Tradeoffs require giving up some of one good to gain more of another.
Economic efficiency means getting the most benefit from scarce resources.
Choices on a production possibilities frontier display productive efficiency.
The invisible hand suggests that broader social good can emerge from selfish individual actions.
Acquisitive self-interest is the orthodox economic starting point for analyzing many economic decisions.
Intertemporal choices involve decisions where costs and benefits occur at different times.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: Multiple Definitions of Economics
Diversity of Paradigms in Economics
A Critical Examination of the Orthodox (Neoclassical) Definition of Economics
The Presentation of an Alternative Definition

knowledge_units: Definitions are important because they provide clarity and direction for understanding a discipline.
The discipline of economics has more than one definition, which depends on the paradigmatic perspective of the economist.
A paradigm represents a school of thought that frames the types of questions, phenomena, and methods of study in a discipline.
Economics, like other social sciences, has multiple paradigms, each associated with different definitions.

learning_objectives: Define the term ideology
Describe the relationship between ideology and paradigms
Identify some of the differences between competing economic paradigms

knowledge_units: Ideology refers to the ideas, beliefs, subjective values, and worldviews that influence how a person perceives social circumstances.
Multiple theoretical perspectives in economics lead to ideological differences.
Conservative economists believe markets work well without outside interference and favor limited government involvement.
Progressive economists believe free markets generate inequality and instability, requiring government intervention.
Radical economists believe market economies are class-based, exploit labor, and need to be replaced.
Conservative economists address negative externalities by assigning property rights to allow markets to function.
Progressive economists address negative externalities through government taxes and regulations to account for social costs.
Radical economists argue that externalities are pervasive and require an alternative to market allocation.
Conservative economists view the free market as the ideal institution for resource allocation with limited government.
Progressive economists advocate for government institutions to correct market failures like pollution and unemployment.
Radical economists support alternative institutions, such as worker cooperatives, to challenge the current economic system.
Conservative economists attribute income inequality to productivity differences and oppose efforts to reduce it.
Progressive economists attribute income inequality to social circumstances and favor government programs for equality of opportunity.
Radical economists view income inequality as a byproduct of a class-based system and support programs that cannot eliminate inequality within capitalism.
Conservative economists believe free trade reduces global poverty through specialization and production expansion.
Progressive economists advocate for government intervention in trade with policies like infant industry and fair labor standards.
Radical economists argue that international trade worsens class tensions and cannot end global poverty.

learning_objectives: Discuss the orthodox definition of economics
Understand the organization of the orthodox definition of economics
Describe the paradigmatic direction of orthodox economics

knowledge_units: Deconstructing an idea is a valuable process for enriching understanding.
The orthodox definition of economics, originating from Lionel Robbins, defines economics as the study of human behavior as a relationship between ends and scarce means with alternative uses.
Contemporary orthodox economics often defines economics as the study of allocating limited resources to satisfy unlimited wants.
Orthodox economics defines resources as those things needed for production, focusing nearly exclusively on Land, Labor, and Capital.
In orthodox economics, resources are assumed to be limited or scarce.
In the orthodox definition, wants are conditioned by the availability of resources and refer to products (goods and services) produced by resources.
Orthodox economics assumes people want products because they derive satisfaction or utility from consuming them.
Orthodox economics assumes wants are unlimited, meaning people have an endless desire for utility or happiness derived from products.
The orthodox definition implies that if wants are unlimited, then resources on a finite planet must be scarce.
The orthodox definition suggests people have an insatiable desire to acquire products, which is interpreted as inherent greed.
The orthodox definition implies that if products bring happiness and people have an endless desire for products, then people have an endless desire to be happy.

learning_objectives: Discuss critiques of the orthodox definition of economics
Understand the differences between facts and beliefs
Describe the differences between needs and wants
Understand the interconnection between facts and values
Compare the differences between scarcity and surplus
Compare the differences between and individual and social approach to economics
Identify the relationship between unlimited wants and environmental degradation

knowledge_units: The orthodox definition of economics as limited resources and unlimited wants is critiqued by heterodox economists for being potentially misleading about actual economic activity.
Heterodox economists offer four critiques of the orthodox definition and its implications from an alternative economic perspective.
A fact is something immutable, whereas a belief is something a person or people think, whether supported by evidence or not.
Karl Polanyi criticized the orthodox scarcity story as a belief assumed within the mind of the orthodox economist, not a fact.
The concept of unlimited wants for material items is not an immutable fact present in all times and places among all human beings.
Anthropological studies, such as those by Marshall Sahlins, document that hunter-gatherer societies can be affluent by having basic needs and wants easily satisfied.
Happiness Economics research finds that people focused on monetary gain and material benefits tend to be less happy than those focused on non-material factors like interpersonal relationships.
Orthodox economics insists on separating facts and values, known as positive economics.
The orthodox fact/value dichotomy is misleading because theorists apply pre-analytical values to their analysis.
The use of assumptions implies value-based decision making by the theorist.
Orthodox economics claims to be a fact-based science but actually utilizes value-based (normative) propositions.
Orthodox economists employ subjective values, such as the desire to maximize acquisition, to frame their definition of economics.
Stating something as 'is' rather than 'believed' presents subjective values as absolute facts.
An honest presentation of the orthodox definition would acknowledge its subjective, normative nature.
The orthodox definition of economics is circular, positing endless want as the cause of scarcity.
Endless want does not imply insufficient resources to meet all basic human needs.
Homelessness results from misallocated resources or maldistribution of production, not from a scarcity of resources.
Global food production is sufficient to adequately nourish every person, yet chronic undernourishment persists due to poverty.
Reorienting economics away from scarcity reveals a story of surplus and abundance, where resources often meet all material needs and many wants.
A need is something necessary for sustaining basic physical and psychological health.
Maslow's hierarchy of needs provides a framework for understanding needs, but the concept of need evolves with social and technological circumstances.
A want is something desired but not necessary for an individual to live and function in society.
The orthodox definition of economics focuses on wants rather than needs.
Orthodox economists may not differentiate between needs and wants, or they may assume that basic needs are already being met.
The orthodox merger of needs and wants can produce misleading insights because basic needs must be met before other wants become relevant.
If basic needs are not being met, orthodox economics may not provide direction toward studying or addressing human suffering.
Economics is categorized as a social science that seeks to understand human interactions on a social level.
Orthodox economics prioritizes the individual over the social element, focusing on want fulfillment and utility maximization.
Utility is subjective and measured through individual consumption choices, making orthodox economics the study of individual decision-making.
Methodological individualism is an approach that examines individual decision-making as the central focus of orthodox economics.
In orthodox economics, social outcomes are derived as a byproduct of aggregating individual maximizing behavior.
Non-orthodox economists critique the absence of a social perspective in orthodox economics, which ignores how societal norms and institutions influence individual behavior.
Non-acquisitive or non-self-interested individual behavior falls outside the scope of orthodox economics but is part of actual human economic interactions.
Enlightened self-interest is the idea that acting to benefit others can be in one's own self-interest.
Orthodox economics cannot consistently apply its definition of economics to enlightened self-interest because charitable giving is product dissipation, not product acquisition.

If all actions provide utility and people are utility maximizers, then orthodox economics reduces to observing behavior and claiming it must be in the person's best interest, which lacks empirical and predictive power.
Behavioral economics uses experiments and psychology to understand deviations from rational, acquisitive behavior due to non-economic factors like religious beliefs or cultural norms.
Behavioral economics still under-emphasizes social considerations by starting from the isolated maximizing individual and searching for deviations, rather than studying social institutions directly.
The Earth is a finite biosphere with limited natural resources, representing a circumstance of scarcity.
Orthodox economics emphasizes endless economic growth driven by unlimited wants and consumption.
Endless economic growth in a finite biosphere creates a conundrum due to resource limits and waste accumulation.
Human economic activity generates waste byproducts that pollute the environment, which has limited absorption capacity.
Planetary boundaries, such as climate change and ocean acidification, must not be exceeded for safe human existence.
Orthodox economics addresses environmental issues through the concept of externalities, where economic activities impose unaccounted costs on third parties.
Orthodox solutions to negative externalities include government regulation and taxation to mitigate pollution.
Critics argue that taxing all negative externalities is impractical and may require excessive bureaucracy.
Orthodox solutions to externalities conflict with the core tenet of endless economic growth and consumption.
Ecological economics reorients the relationship by placing the economy subordinate to the natural environment, prioritizing sustainability over endless growth.

learning_objectives: Describe an alternative definition of economics
Understand the basis for framing an alternative definition of economics

knowledge_units: Alternative definitions of economics can simplify economic activity to its basic form, such as studying how humans work together with nature to fulfill material needs and wants.
Alternative definitions of economics can focus on essential characteristics, such as studying how humans organize production, distribution, and consumption.
Alternative definitions of economics can emphasize interdisciplinary facets, such as studying how cultural norms, social institutions, and political structures influence economic behavior.
Alternative definitions avoid assuming scarcity, allowing for diverse economic organizations and ways to meet societal needs.
Alternative definitions avoid unprovable assumptions about individual behavior, emphasizing social elements of economic organization.
Alternative definitions avoid predicating endless want and endless growth, allowing for economic structures within natural biosphere limits.
An alternative definition of economics is the study of social provisioning, rooted in social, political, natural, and cultural processes.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: Ontology
Epistemology
Axiology

knowledge_units: Meta-theory refers to the underlying set of instructions or codes that structure how an academic discipline operates.
Orthodox economics is defined as the study of the allocation of scarce resources among competing ends.
Heterodox economics is defined as the study of the social provisioning process rooted in social, political, natural, and cultural processes.
Heterodox economics challenges the validity of orthodox economics' meta-theory and proposes alternative strategies for studying the economy.
Ontology is the theory of reality or existence, addressing what it means to claim that something exists.
Epistemology is the theory of knowledge or truth, addressing what it means to claim we know something and how such claims can be justified.
Axiology is the theory of value, investigating why we consider some things more important or of greater interest than others.
Meta-theoretical paradigms organize the foundations and procedures of scientific investigation.
Abstraction, such as through modeling, analogies, and metaphors, helps gain knowledge about the physical observable world.
The answers to questions about reality, knowledge, and value comprise our meta-theoretical foundations or paradigms.
Orthodox economics is historically grounded in the Positivist meta-theoretical paradigm, which applies the Empiricist paradigm of natural sciences to social science.
Heterodox economics is primarily grounded in the Realist paradigm, which addresses flaws in both Empiricist and Positivist paradigms without succumbing to relativism.
A commitment to a specific conceptualization of reality directly informs how we approach studying that reality and discuss value.

learning_objectives: Define and describe three ontological relationships between structures and agents
Develop examples of subjects that are best understood using each of the three ontological commitments described
Utilize the following terms: reductionism, duality, aggregation, reflexivity, and embedded, to differentiate orthodox from heterodox economics

knowledge_units: Ontology is the study of what is real and provides the foundational coding for science.
Social ontology describes the relationships between structures and agents in social reality.
In the first ontological relationship, structures define or determine agents, as exemplified by the feudal order.
The second ontological relationship is reductionism, where structures are the aggregation of agent choices, and the whole is equal to the sum of its parts.
Duality in reductionism posits that humans can observe and understand the world from an outside perspective.
Aggregation means adding up individual parts to understand the whole, as seen in orthodox economics modeling individual choices.
The third ontological relationship is reflexivity, where structures and agents have a bi-directional influence, making the whole greater than the sum of its parts.
Reflexivity challenges the concept of duality by questioning whether scientists can be separate from the social world they study.
Heterodox economics views the economy as embedded in social, cultural, political, and environmental contexts.
Orthodox economics proceeds from an aggregation ontology, seeking to study the economy in isolation from other contexts.

learning_objectives: Compare and contrast how approaching economics from different ontological positions affects heterodox and orthodox perspectives
Describe how ontology informs the selection of methodology for scientific study
Define Positivist paradigm and discuss its influence on orthodox economics
Describe examples of orthodox and heterodox methodologies

knowledge_units: Ontological foundations influence the development of scientific knowledge and methodologies.
The determinist ontological paradigm historically derived knowledge through religious interpretation rather than defined epistemology.
Thomas Kuhn's concept of paradigm shifts describes how anomalies in existing knowledge systems encourage the development of new methodologies.
Isaac Newton's work on gravity and calculus exemplified the application of mathematical tools to understand physical relationships.
The universality of Newton's law of gravity spurred a new paradigm of scientific inquiry based on empirical analysis and the scientific method.
The scientific method displaced religion and mythology as primary explanations of reality, enabling discovery by anyone.
The ontology that the whole is equal to the sum of its parts underlies the revolution in science and its broader societal impacts.
The success of natural science methodologies led to their proposed application in explaining social systems.
The Positivist paradigm argues for the use of physical science principles like rates of change and velocities to address social science inquiries.
Neoclassical economics is grounded in an aggregation ontology where the economy is the sum of its parts.
Orthodox economics is the study of the allocation of scarce resources among competing ends.
The law of demand states that when prices go up people consume less and vice versa.
Constrained optimization is the process of finding the maximum or minimum of an objective function with respect to unknown variables in the presence of a constraint.
The objective function in consumer theory is the utility function, which describes how consumers derive satisfaction from consumption.
Consumer preferences are assumed to be complete, transitive, and to satisfy 'more is better'.
Consumers are constrained by their income and the prices they face in the market.
The budget constraint assumes the consumer uses all income to maximize utility.
The solution to the consumer optimization problem is a point in the Cartesian plane.
The demand curve is derived by solving the optimization problem for varying prices, holding other factors constant.
Market demand is obtained by aggregating individual demand curves, assuming identical consumer behavior.
Orthodox economics uses mathematics from natural sciences, but its appropriateness for modeling human activities is questioned.
Einstein's quote highlights ontological differences between natural and social sciences, not just epistemological ones.
Social sciences study a reflexive ontology where the whole is greater than the sum of its parts, focusing on relationships between agents and structures.
Oncology has shifted from reductionist approaches to embracing reflexive understandings that consider environmental and social contexts.
Cancer treatment now considers environmental factors and preventive measures, not just cellular-level interventions.
The study of cancer exemplifies a reflexive ontology by blurring the division between natural and social phenomena.
Economists can learn from oncology by pursuing methodologies that understand relationships between economic agents and structures.
Heterodox economists advance from a reflexive ontology, focusing on institutions as socially constructed units of production.
Heterodox economics studies social provisioning rooted in social, political, natural, and cultural processes.
Orthodox economics emphasizes scarcity, while heterodox economics emphasizes the social provisioning process without assuming scarcity as an underlying human condition.
Heterodox economics does not assume competitive ends are the primary means for resource allocation, allowing for coordinated cooperation in institutions.
Heterodox economics includes social, political, environmental, and cultural processes as fundamental elements for understanding the economy.
Orthodox economics attempts to separate the economy from politics, culture, and environment through controlled models and assumptions.
Heterodox economists use diverse methodological tools, including matrix algebra, network analysis, spatial analysis, historical studies, case studies, ecological models, and critical legal studies.
Heterodox economists reject Newtonian physics as a valid model for the economy but accept mathematics and other natural science methods as productive tools.
Orthodox economics grounds universal claims like the Law of Demand in assumptions of self-interest as a natural force, similar to gravity.
Understanding underlying assumptions in economic arguments is important to avoid hindering innovation that could address issues like poverty or inflation.
Orthodox economics adopts tools from natural sciences like constrained optimization and equilibrium analysis based on the assumption that economic phenomena are predictable and reducible.
Heterodox economists argue that human economic behavior is not reducible to universal laws and requires methodologies that account for social, political, environmental, and cultural contexts.
The choice of axiology, or theory of value, is dependent on an individual's ontological and epistemological commitments.

learning_objectives: Describe how ontology, epistemology, and axiology are related
Compare and contrast use value, exchange value and the labor theory of value
Match the different theories of value to key contributors to the history of economic thought
Apply Marx’s model of the working day, to describe the relationship between exploitation and surplus value

knowledge_units: Axiology is the study of the nature, types, and criteria of values and of value judgments.
Orthodox economists largely do not recognize axiology as a primary concern because they equate value with price.
The exchange theory of value and the labor theory of value are two principal approaches to value theory in the history of political economy.
The selection of an ontological foundation is deeply connected to the epistemological study of reality and the criteria of values that structure economics.
The diamonds and water paradox introduces the exchange theory of value in orthodox texts.
The marginalist revolution establishes neoclassical economics as the dominant school of thought and allows economics to be viewed as a natural science.
Heterodox economics uses the labor theory of value and the surplus approach as alternatives to the price equals value narrative.
Heterodox economics offers an alternative meta-theoretical grounding if orthodox economics is not fit for purpose.
Adam Smith distinguished between value in use (utility) and value in exchange (purchasing power).
The diamond-water paradox highlights that goods with high use value (like water) can have low exchange value, while goods with low use value (like diamonds) can have high exchange value.
Neoclassical economists resolved the paradox by introducing marginal analysis, which examines the value of an additional unit of a good.
Marginal utility is the satisfaction gained from consuming one more unit of a good.
In marginal analysis, the value of a good is determined by its scarcity at the margin, explaining why diamonds are expensive and water is cheap.
Neoclassical economics uses marginal analysis to argue that value emerges from market exchanges, separating economic analysis from value judgments and ethics.
The surplus approach critiques the exchange theory of value by emphasizing social, political, and cultural processes in economic analysis.
Wage negotiations reveal power imbalances between employers and workers, challenging the assumption of equitable exchange.
Marshall recognized that employers might exploit workers by paying unfair wages, necessitating collective bargaining through unions.
Surplus production occurs when output exceeds what is necessary for subsistence, raising questions about its distribution.
The distribution of surplus is shaped by institutional structures like property rights, taxes, and corporate governance.
The labor theory of value posits that labor is the source of surplus or profit, as argued by Smith, Ricardo, and Marx.
Marx's model of the working day divides it into necessary labor time and surplus labor time.
Necessary labor time is the portion of the working day required to produce output covering workers' wages.
Surplus labor time is the portion of the working day where workers produce beyond their wages, generating profit for capitalists.
If labor is the source of value, then profits arise from the exploitation of labor.
Heterodox economics studies the social provisioning process, integrating social, political, cultural, and environmental contexts.
Value is a socially constructed process, not a 'value-free' scientific concept, requiring interdisciplinary study.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: Measuring the Size of the Economy: Gross Domestic Product
Adjusting Nominal Values to Real Values
Tracking Real GDP over Time
Comparing GDP among Countries
How Well GDP Measures the Well-Being of Society

knowledge_units: Macroeconomics focuses on the economy as a whole, addressing questions like what causes recessions and why some countries grow faster than others.
Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is a measure developed to track the total production of an entire economy.
Macroeconomics involves aggregating the economic activity of all households and businesses to understand overall demand and supply.
Decisions that are rational at the microeconomic level can have unintended or counterproductive results at the macroeconomic level.
The study of macroeconomics is structured around macroeconomic goals, analytical frameworks, and government policy tools.
Economic growth is measured by the percentage change in real gross domestic product.
Unemployment is measured by the unemployment rate, which is the percentage of people in the labor force without a job.
Inflation is a sustained increase in the overall level of prices, measured by the consumer price index.
Macroeconomics uses the theories of aggregate demand (AD) and aggregate supply (AS).
The Neoclassical and Keynesian perspectives each provide their own version of AD and AS models.
Studying both perspectives helps understand what drives the macroeconomy.
National governments use monetary policy to manage the money supply and interest rates.
National governments use fiscal policy to change government spending and taxes.

learning_objectives: Identify the components of GDP on the demand side and on the supply side
Evaluate how economists measure gross domestic product (GDP)
Contrast and calculate GDP, net exports, and net national product

knowledge_units: Gross domestic product (GDP) is the value of all final goods and services produced within a country in a given year.
GDP is measured by summing the quantity of each good and service produced multiplied by its price.
GDP can be measured either by the total dollar value of what consumers purchase or by the total dollar value of what the country produces.
GDP can be measured by summing the four components of demand: consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports.
Consumption expenditure by households is the largest component of GDP, typically accounting for about two-thirds of total GDP.
Investment expenditure refers to purchases of plant, equipment, and software primarily by businesses and is important for job creation but is volatile.
Government expenditure in GDP includes only government purchases of goods and services, not transfer payments like Social Security.
Net exports are calculated as the value of exports minus the value of imports, representing the trade balance.
A trade surplus occurs when a country's exports exceed its imports, while a trade deficit occurs when imports exceed exports.
The formula for GDP measured by demand is GDP = C + I + G + (X - M), where C is consumption, I is investment, G is government spending, and (X - M) is net exports.
GDP measured by what is produced is exactly the same as GDP measured by the components of demand.
The five categories of what a country produces are durable goods, nondurable goods, services, structures, and the change in inventories.
Services constitute the largest share of GDP and have been growing over time.
Durable goods and nondurable goods are roughly equal shares of the goods category within GDP.
The change in inventories reflects goods produced but not yet sold, and its level tends to decline when business is better than expected and rise when business is worse than expected.
GDP can be measured by summing all income earned from production, known as the national income approach.
The total value of a nation's output equals the total value of a nation's income.

Gross national product (GNP) measures the total output produced by a country's citizens and firms, regardless of location, while gross domestic product (GDP) measures output within a country's geographic borders.
Net national product (NNP) is calculated by subtracting depreciation from gross national product (GNP).
Depreciation is the process by which physical capital ages and loses value over time.
GDP can be calculated using the expenditure approach as the sum of consumption, investment, government purchases, and net exports.
Net exports are calculated as exports minus imports.
NNP can be calculated by adjusting GDP for net income flows from abroad and subtracting depreciation.

learning_objectives: Contrast nominal GDP and real GDP
Explain GDP deflator
Calculate real GDP based on nominal GDP values

knowledge_units: Nominal value measures an economic statistic using actual prices at the time.
Real value measures an economic statistic after adjusting for inflation.
The real value of an economic statistic is generally more important than the nominal value.
Nominal GDP is measured using the actual market prices prevailing in each stated year.
The GDP deflator is a price index measuring the average prices of all goods and services included in the economy.
Real GDP is calculated by dividing nominal GDP by the GDP deflator divided by 100.
The base year is the year whose prices are used to compute real GDP, and in that year nominal and real GDP are equal.
As long as inflation is positive, real GDP is less than nominal GDP in any year after the base year.
As long as inflation is positive, real GDP is greater than nominal GDP in any year before the base year.
The real GDP growth rate can be approximated by subtracting the inflation rate from the nominal GDP growth rate.

learning_objectives: Explain recessions, depressions, peaks, and troughs
Evaluate the importance of tracking real GDP over time

knowledge_units: Real GDP growth is reported as an annualized rate by multiplying the quarterly growth rate by four.
Real GDP is highly correlated with employment, so when real GDP rises, employment also rises.
Recessions cause firms to lay off workers, leading to financial and personal costs for those affected.
A peak is the highest point of the economy before a recession begins.
A trough is the lowest point of a recession before a recovery begins.
A recession lasts from peak to trough, and an economic expansion runs from trough to peak.
The business cycle refers to the economy's movement from peak to trough and trough to peak.
The National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) tracks business cycles for the U.S. economy.

learning_objectives: Explain how we can use GDP to compare the economic welfare of different nations
Calculate the conversion of GDP to a common currency by using exchange rates
Calculate GDP per capita using population data

knowledge_units: GDP is commonly used as a measure of economic welfare or standard of living in a nation.
Comparing GDP between countries requires converting to a common currency because each country measures GDP in its own currency.
To compare standards of living across countries, GDP must be divided by population to account for differences in the number of people.
To compare GDP across countries with different currencies, it is necessary to convert to a common currency using an exchange rate.
An exchange rate is the value of one currency in terms of another currency.
Market exchange rates vary daily based on supply and demand in foreign exchange markets.
Purchasing power parity (PPP) equivalent exchange rates provide a longer-run measure for cross-country GDP comparisons.
Economists typically use PPP-equivalent exchange rates for comparing GDP across countries.
To convert GDP from one currency to another, divide the GDP in the original currency by the exchange rate.
GDP per capita is calculated by dividing a country's GDP by its population.
GDP per capita provides a measure of economic welfare on a per-person basis.
Rankings by GDP can differ from rankings by GDP per capita, as seen when comparing India and Germany.
China has a large GDP but a much lower GDP per capita than the United States due to its larger population.
GDP per capita can be used to classify countries into high-income, middle-income, and low-income groups based on typical ranges.

learning_objectives: Discuss how productivity influences the standard of living
Explain the limitations of GDP as a measure of the standard of living
Analyze the relationship between GDP data and fluctuations in the standard of living

knowledge_units: GDP per capita captures some aspects of the standard of living, as evidenced by migration patterns from low to high GDP per capita countries.
Standard of living is a broader concept than GDP because it includes all elements affecting well-being, not just market transactions.
GDP does not cover all factors relevant to the standard of living, such as non-market elements that affect well-being.
GDP does not account for leisure time, so a larger GDP may reflect longer working hours rather than higher productivity.
GDP includes spending on environmental protection, healthcare, and education but does not measure actual outcomes like environmental cleanliness, health, or literacy.
GDP only includes market transactions, so non-market production such as household work is excluded.
GDP per capita is an average and does not reflect income inequality within a society.
GDP does not measure the variety of goods and services available to consumers.
GDP does not account for technological advancements or new products that improve the standard of living.
A rise in GDP may not indicate an improved standard of living if it results from negative events like natural disasters or increased spending on security due to crime.
GDP per capita does not fully capture the broader concept of standard of living.
A rise in GDP can theoretically occur while the standard of living falls if factors like health and environmental cleanliness worsen.
The rise in GDP understates the actual rise in the standard of living because it does not capture improvements in leisure, health, environment, technology, and product variety.

The rise in GDP overstates the true rise in the standard of living because it ignores increases in crime, traffic congestion, income inequality, and the shift of non-market services to the market economy.
GDP measures production well and indicates when a country is materially better or worse off in terms of jobs and incomes.
GDP per capita is a reasonable, rough-and-ready measure of the standard of living.
No single number can capture all the elements of the standard of living.
GDP is the broadest measure of a nation's economic activity.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: The Relatively Recent Arrival of Economic Growth
Labor Productivity and Economic Growth
Components of Economic Growth
Economic Convergence

knowledge_units: Economic growth refers to the sustained increase in a nation's standard of living over time.
The standard of living can be measured by the amount of goods and services, such as food calories, that average wages can purchase.
Rapid economic growth, as seen in Western Europe between 1800 and 1875, can dramatically increase living standards within a few generations.
Many countries, particularly in Western Europe, North America, and East Asia, have achieved high levels of economic growth that allow for adequate food provision.
Economic growth is a primary concern for all countries, with high-income nations seeking to maintain growth and middle-income nations aiming to catch up.
A significant portion of the world's population remains in extreme poverty, highlighting the importance of economic growth for human welfare.
Dramatic improvements in a nation's standard of living are possible, as demonstrated by South Korea's transformation from a poor economy to a high-income country.
Sustained economic growth, as experienced by South Korea and other East Asian nations, can elevate a country to high-income status within a few decades.
The United States has undergone long-term economic growth, transforming from a rural agricultural economy to one based on services, manufacturing, and technology.

learning_objectives: Explain the conditions that have allowed for modern economic growth in the last two centuries
Analyze the influence of public policies on an economy’s long-run economic growth

knowledge_units: Modern economic growth refers to the rapid and sustained economic growth experienced in the last two centuries.
Before the last two centuries, the average person's standard of living had not changed much for centuries.
The Industrial Revolution involved the widespread use of power-driven machinery and began in Great Britain in the early 1800s.
The Industrial Revolution created a self-reinforcing cycle where new inventions and investments generated profits that funded further investment and inventions.
Institutions, such as laws and traditions, play a critical role in a country's economic growth.
The Industrial Revolution led to increasing inequality among nations, with some economies growing rapidly while others remained near subsistence levels.
Catching up in economic growth is possible, as demonstrated by rapid growth in countries like Japan, Brazil, and several East Asian economies in the twentieth century.
Economic growth in the last two centuries has significantly improved the human condition in terms of nutrition, health, education, and political democracy.
Economic growth depends on adherence to the rule of law and protection of property rights and contractual rights by a government.
Property rights are the rights of individuals and firms to own and use property as they see fit.
Contractual rights are based on property rights and allow individuals to enter into agreements with recourse through the legal system for noncompliance.
Without a legal system that enforces contracts, people would be unlikely to enter into agreements, slowing economic growth.
The World Bank ranks countries' legal systems based on effective protection of property rights and rule-based governance.
Countries lacking a clear system of property rights and an independent court system have lagged in economic growth.

learning_objectives: Identify the role of labor productivity in promoting economic growth
Analyze the sources of economic growth using the aggregate production function
Measure an economy’s rate of productivity growth
Evaluate the power of sustained growth

knowledge_units: Sustained long-term economic growth comes from increases in worker productivity.
Labor productivity is the value that each employed person creates per unit of his or her input.
Human capital is the accumulated knowledge, skills, and expertise that the average worker in an economy possesses.
Technological change is a combination of invention (advances in knowledge) and innovation (putting those advances to use in a new product or service).
Economies of scale are the cost advantages that industries obtain due to size.

The aggregate production function describes the technical relationship by which economic inputs like labor, machinery, and raw materials are turned into outputs like goods and services for the entire economy.
The inputs in an aggregate production function typically include workforce, human capital, physical capital, and technology.
An economy's rate of productivity growth is closely linked to the growth rate of its GDP per capita, but the two are not identical.
Over the long term, the only way that GDP per capita can grow continually is if the productivity of the average worker rises or if there are complementary increases in capital.
A common measure of U.S. productivity per worker is the dollar value per hour the worker contributes to the employer’s output.
The measure of U.S. productivity per worker excludes government workers and farming due to measurement difficulties or their small economic share.
The 'new economy' controversy centers on whether advances in communications and information technology in the 1990s led to a permanent increase in U.S. productivity growth.
Optimists argue that the 'new economy' would generate higher average productivity growth for decades to come.
Pessimists argue that a few years of stronger productivity growth does not prove that higher productivity will last for the long term.
Productivity growth is closely linked to the average level of wages because firms' willingness to pay workers depends on the value of the output those workers produce.
In the long run, productivity per hour is the most important determinant of the average wage level in any economy.
The OECD tracks data on the annual growth rate of real GDP per hour worked to compare economies' productivity growth.
Sustained economic growth is crucial for improving people's standard of living.
Small differences in growth rates, when compounded over long periods, lead to large differences in GDP per capita.
The formula for calculating future GDP is GDP at starting date multiplied by (1 + growth rate) raised to the power of years.
Higher growth rates, such as 5% or 8% per year, can rapidly transform an economy's standard of living.
Compound economic growth rates work similarly to compound interest rates, where growth builds on past growth.

learning_objectives: Discuss the components of economic growth, including physical capital, human capital, and technology
Explain capital deepening and its significance
Analyze the methods employed in economic growth accounting studies
Identify factors that contribute to a healthy climate for economic growth

knowledge_units: Small differences in annual economic growth rates lead to large differences in GDP per capita over time.
Physical capital includes plant, equipment, and infrastructure, and it can increase output through greater quantity or improved quality.
Human capital refers to the skills and knowledge that make workers productive.
Technology includes inventions, innovations, new organizational methods, and institutions that increase output quantity and quality.
GDP per capita is used to compare living standards across countries with different population sizes.
Per capita income growth requires that the rate of income growth exceeds population growth.
Capital deepening refers to an increase in the level of capital per person in a society.
Capital deepening can apply to both additional human capital per worker and additional physical capital per worker.
Human capital deepening in the U.S. economy focuses more on additional education and training than on increased average work experience.
Physical capital deepening in the U.S. economy is shown by the average worker having access to significantly more physical capital over time.
Economic growth requires investments in labor productivity through human capital, technology, and physical capital.
South Korea's economic growth was fostered by investments in human capital, physical capital, and technology.
Growth accounting studies use an aggregate production function to estimate how much of per capita economic growth can be attributed to growth in physical capital and human capital.
The part of economic growth unexplained by measured inputs of physical and human capital is attributed to growth in technology.
Technology is typically the most important contributor to U.S. economic growth, often explaining more than half of the growth.
Building human capital is at least as important as investment in physical capital for economic growth.

Human capital, physical capital, and technology work together to drive economic growth, as workers with higher education and skills often drive technological innovations that require new physical capital investment.
In low-income countries, helping girls close the education gap with boys is especially important for economic growth because educated women tend to have fewer, healthier, better-educated children.
A healthy economic climate requires a market orientation at the microeconomic level to provide incentives for increasing human and physical capital.
Markets that allow personal and business rewards encourage individuals to acquire human capital and firms to invest in physical capital and technology.
Government plays a role in correcting market failures and guiding markets toward outcomes that benefit society.

Government policies such as education requirements, low capital gains taxes, infrastructure investment, special economic zones, and scientific research support can facilitate capital deepening and technology.
A healthy climate for economic growth includes human capital deepening, physical capital deepening, and technological gains operating in a market-oriented economy with supportive government policies.

learning_objectives: Explain economic convergence
Analyze various arguments for and against economic convergence
Evaluate the speed of economic convergence between high-income countries and the rest of the world

knowledge_units: Economic convergence is a pattern where low-income and middle-income economies grow faster than high-income economies.
Countries in the 'fast growth club' averaged GDP growth of at least 5% per year from 1990 to 2008, potentially converging with high-income countries.
Countries in the 'slow growth club' averaged GDP growth of 2% per year or less from 1990 to 2008.
Low-income countries have faster GDP growth than middle-income countries, which in turn have faster growth than high-income countries.
The persistence of economic convergence into the future is a controversial question among economists.
The law of diminishing returns suggests that as an economy increases its human and physical capital, the marginal gains to economic growth will diminish.
Low-income countries tend to have lower levels of human and physical capital, so investment in capital deepening should have a larger marginal effect than in high-income countries.
Diminishing returns implies that low-income economies could converge to the levels achieved by high-income countries.
Low-income countries may find it easier to improve their technologies by applying already invented and well-understood technology, a phenomenon called 'the advantages of backwardness.'.
Countries that have observed the experience of faster-growing economies may learn from it and build market-friendly institutions to support a higher standard of living.
Diminishing marginal returns of capital deepening limit sustained economic growth without technological progress.
Technological improvements can offset diminishing returns from capital deepening, allowing continued growth.
Technological ideas do not seem to run into diminishing returns because they can be widely applied at low marginal cost.
The ability of low-income countries to copy and adapt technology depends on supportive economic, educational, and public policy institutions.
Economic convergence between high-income and low-income countries will proceed slowly even with rapid growth in poorer countries.
Small differences in annual growth rates accumulate into large differences in GDP per capita over time.
High-income countries have built their standard of living advantages over decades or more than a century.
Modern economic growth is driven by increases in inputs such as technology, physical capital, and human capital.
Technological progress combined with capital deepening creates growth and convergence.
Food distribution problems are often caused by failures in government macroeconomic policy rather than insufficient food production.
Macroeconomic policies promoting stable inflation, full employment, education of women, and property rights help eliminate starvation and improve food distribution.
As incomes rise, individuals tend to consume more calories from fats and protein and fewer from carbohydrates.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: How Economists Define and Compute Unemployment Rate
Patterns of Unemployment
What Causes Changes in Unemployment over the Short Run
What Causes Changes in Unemployment over the Long Run

knowledge_units: Unemployment is a lagging indicator of business activity, meaning it begins to increase after a recession starts and declines after a recovery begins.
The decline in the unemployment rate after a recession can be slow due to a combination of slow job growth and ongoing increases in the population and labor force.
Recent recessions have been characterized by longer periods of recovery than their predecessors.
Unemployment imposes significant human costs, including financial stress, lifestyle adjustments, and potential impacts on self-worth, family relationships, and health.
Unemployment imposes economic costs on society because it represents unused economic resources, and its opportunity cost is the output that unemployed workers could have produced.

learning_objectives: Calculate the labor force participation rate and the unemployment rate
Explain hidden unemployment and what it means to be in or out of the labor force
Evaluate the collection and interpretation of unemployment data

knowledge_units: The unemployment rate is reported as a percentage of the labor force.
Small changes in the unemployment rate represent large numbers of people because the labor force is large.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics is responsible for tracking and reporting unemployment data.
The adult population is divided into three categories: employed, unemployed, and out of the labor force.
To be classified as unemployed, a person must be without a job, currently available to work, and actively looking for work in the previous four weeks.
A person who is not working and not actively looking for work is classified as out of the labor force.
The labor force consists of the employed plus the unemployed.
The unemployment rate is the percentage of the labor force that is unemployed, calculated as (unemployed people / total labor force) × 100.
The labor force participation rate is the percentage of the adult population that is in the labor force, calculated as (people in the labor force / total adult population) × 100.
The adult population is divided into those in the labor force (employed or unemployed and looking for work) and those out of the labor force.
The unemployment rate is not the percentage of the total adult population without jobs, but rather the percentage of the labor force without jobs.
Hidden unemployment includes individuals who are employed part-time or temporarily but seek full-time permanent employment.
Underemployment refers to individuals working in jobs that do not fully utilize their skills or training.
Discouraged workers who have stopped seeking employment are not counted as unemployed but are part of hidden unemployment.
The labor force participation rate is the percentage of adults who are either employed or unemployed and actively looking for a job.
The labor force participation rate is calculated by dividing the number of people in the labor force by the total adult population and multiplying by 100.
The U.S. labor force participation rate historically peaked in the late 1990s and has generally declined since, with a sharp drop during the COVID-19 pandemic.
The establishment payroll survey is based on a survey of about 147,000 businesses and government agencies to estimate payroll employment.
The payroll survey generates estimates for all employees, average weekly hours worked, and average hourly, weekly, and overtime earnings.
A criticism of the establishment payroll survey is that it does not count the self-employed.
The establishment payroll survey does not distinguish between new, minimum wage, part-time, or temporary jobs and full-time jobs with higher pay.
The U.S. unemployment rate is based on the Current Population Survey (CPS), which is conducted monthly by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics.
The CPS survey is designed to be representative of the country by dividing it into areas, districts, and clusters, and interviewing about 60,000 households each month.
The CPS collects data on various demographic and economic factors, such as state, industry, gender, age, race, education, and reasons for unemployment.
The Current Population Survey (CPS) measures the percentage of the labor force that is unemployed, while the establishment payroll survey (EPS) measures net job changes.
Discouraged workers who have stopped looking for jobs are counted as out of the labor force, not as unemployed.
Some individuals counted as unemployed may not be actively seeking work and could be more accurately classified as out of the labor force.
Some individuals may report being unemployed while actually working in unreported jobs.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics publishes additional surveys to provide a more nuanced view of the labor market beyond the unemployment rate.
Economic statistics like the unemployment rate are imperfect but can still be informative when interpreted knowledgeably.

learning_objectives: Explain historical patterns of unemployment in the U.S.
Identify trends of unemployment based on demographics
Evaluate global unemployment rates

knowledge_units: Let’s look at how unemployment rates have changed over time and how various groups of people are affected by unemployment differently.
Explain historical patterns of unemployment in the U.S.
U.S. unemployment rates fluctuate over time, reaching roughly 10% during deep recessions in the early 1980s and 2007–2009.
Unemployment rates were below 5% during the late 1990s and mid-2000s, which was low by historical standards.
The unemployment rate never falls to zero and almost never gets below 3% for extended periods.
Unemployment tends to lag changes in economic activity, especially during upswings following a recession.
There is no significant long-term upward or downward trend in U.S. unemployment rates despite population growth and increased labor force participation.
Unemployment rates are highest for the very young and decrease with age.
Younger workers have higher unemployment due to more frequent job transitions as part of the job-matching process.
Middle-aged workers tend to have lower unemployment because they feel greater responsibility to maintain employment.
Elderly workers have extremely low unemployment rates because those without jobs often retire and exit the labor force.
Those with less education typically experience higher unemployment because education provides better labor market connections and higher demand.
Unemployment can be categorized by reason, including new entrants, re-entrants, job leavers, and job losers (temporary and non-temporary).
Unemployment can be measured by the length of time individuals have been unemployed, ranging from under 5 weeks to over 27 weeks.
The U.S. unemployment rate typically appears slightly better than the average of other high-income countries.
Cross-country comparisons of unemployment rates require caution due to differences in definitions, measurement tools, and labor market structures.
Japan's low reported unemployment rate may not accurately reflect its labor market health due to workers exiting the labor force and firms retaining underemployed workers.
Comparing unemployment rates between high-income and low-income countries is difficult due to differences in statistical capabilities and labor market participation patterns.
In many low-income countries, most workers are not formally employed, and unemployment manifests differently than in high-income countries with social safety nets.

learning_objectives: Explain cyclical, frictional, and structural unemployment
Assess relationships between the natural rate of employment and potential real GDP, productivity, and public policy
Identify recent patterns in the natural rate of employment
Propose ways to combat unemployment

knowledge_units: Cyclical unemployment is the variation in unemployment caused by the business cycle as the economy moves between expansion and recession.
The natural rate of unemployment is the level of unemployment that persists even when the economy is healthy and growing.
Unemployment rates are never zero, even during strong economic growth, as seen in the U.S. where the rate rarely dips below 4%.
Some economies, like those in Italy, France, and Germany, have persistently high unemployment rates through both good and bad economic periods.
The natural rate of unemployment is the unemployment rate resulting from economic, social, and political factors when the economy is neither booming nor in recession.
The natural rate of unemployment is influenced by the usual pattern of companies expanding and contracting their workforces in a dynamic economy.
Social and economic forces that affect the labor market influence the natural rate of unemployment.
Public policies that affect the eagerness of people to work or the willingness of businesses to hire influence the natural rate of unemployment.
Frictional unemployment occurs as workers move between jobs due to the time needed to find suitable employment.
Frictional unemployment is not inherently negative because it allows for better job matching between employers and employees.
The level of frictional unemployment depends on the ease of learning about job opportunities and workers' willingness to relocate.
Frictional unemployment tends to be higher among younger workers who are exploring job options and lower among prime-age and older workers.
A society with a higher proportion of young workers will generally experience a higher natural rate of unemployment due to increased frictional unemployment.
Structural unemployment occurs when individuals lack skills valued by the labor market due to shifts in demand or insufficient skill acquisition.
Technology can cause structural unemployment by displacing lower-skilled workers while creating demand for higher-skilled workers.
Education and retraining are key to minimizing structural unemployment, as individuals with degrees can adapt to changing skill demands.
The natural unemployment rate is the unemployment rate that exists when the economy is at full employment.
When the economy is at full employment, real GDP equals potential real GDP.
If the economy is below full employment, the unemployment rate exceeds the natural rate and real GDP is less than potential.
If the economy is above full employment, the unemployment rate is below the natural rate and real GDP exceeds potential, but this can only be sustained temporarily.
Unexpected shifts in productivity can affect the natural rate of unemployment.
Wages are determined by worker productivity over time, but adjustments are not immediate due to infrequent wage reviews and difficulty measuring individual productivity.
When productivity is unexpectedly low, unemployment tends to be higher, and when unexpectedly high, unemployment tends to be lower, until wages adjust.
Public policies that provide generous unemployment benefits can lower the opportunity cost of unemployment and reduce the eagerness of unemployed workers to find new jobs.
The duration of unemployment benefits matters more than their generosity in influencing the natural rate of unemployment.
Government assistance for job search or retraining can encourage unemployed people to return to work sooner.
Unemployment insurance is a joint federal–state program funded by a federal tax on employers, with states setting benefit levels and duration.
An individual does not have to collect unemployment benefits to be classified as unemployed.
Government regulations that create bureaucratic barriers to starting or expanding businesses can discourage hiring and increase the natural rate of unemployment.
Laws that restrict business operations, such as zoning or Sunday closing laws, can create barriers between willing workers and employers, contributing to a higher natural rate of unemployment.
Government policies that make it difficult to fire workers may discourage businesses from hiring more than strictly necessary.
High minimum wages may discourage businesses from hiring low-skill workers.
Government support for powerful unions can push up wages for union workers but may discourage businesses from hiring those workers.
The natural rate of unemployment can change over time due to underlying economic, social, and political factors.
The internet and social networking sites have reduced frictional unemployment by making job searches easier.
The growth of the temporary worker industry has helped reduce the natural rate of unemployment by providing jobs and serving as a clearinghouse for permanent positions.
The aging of the baby boom generation has reduced the natural rate of unemployment because older workers experience lower unemployment than younger workers.
The natural rate of unemployment was lower in the 1990s and early 2000s compared to the 1980s due to combined factors like internet job searches, temp work growth, and workforce aging.
The natural rate of unemployment in many European economies has been higher than in the U.S. since the 1970s due to different labor market conditions.
Generous welfare and unemployment benefits, along with rules imposing additional costs on businesses when hiring, contribute to a higher natural rate of unemployment in Europe.
Laws requiring extended notice periods and substantial severance packages before layoffs discourage both firing and hiring, increasing the natural rate of unemployment.
The prevalence of labor market regulations in Europe, compared to the United States, explains the persistently higher unemployment rates even during economic growth.
Cyclical unemployment is a short-term problem caused by recessions, and the preferred solution is to avoid or minimize recessions through policies that stimulate overall buying power.
The natural rate of unemployment is influenced by factors beyond government control, such as population age structure and productivity shifts, but government policy can affect its persistence.
Government policies affecting workers or employers should consider their impact on information and incentives for job matching, such as job search assistance and labor market regulations.
Unemployment is a lagging indicator of business activity due to the costs employers incur in recruitment, hiring, and training workers.
Firms tend to retain workers initially during a downturn to avoid the costs of rehiring and retraining if demand recovers.
Jobless recoveries following recent recessions are linked to job polarization, which is the disappearance of middle-skill routine occupations due to technological progress.
Job polarization results from technological advances in robotics, computing, and information technology, reducing demand for labor in routine tasks.

metadata.book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

book_title: Principles of Economics: Scarcity and Social Provisioning (3rd Ed.)

learning_objectives: Tracking Inflation
How to Measure Changes in the Cost of Living
How the U.S. and Other Countries Experience Inflation
The Confusion Over Inflation
Indexing and Its Limitations

knowledge_units: Inflation is a general and ongoing rise in the level of prices in an entire economy.
Hyperinflation is an extreme form of inflation where prices rise at an extremely high rate, such as doubling daily.
Inflation differs from a relative price change, which involves the price of one good rising while another falls.
Inflation implies an ongoing rise in prices, not a one-time shift to a new equilibrium.
This chapter shows how to combine prices of individual goods and services to create a measure of overall inflation.
Inflation statistics are used to adjust other economic variables, such as GDP, to distinguish real increases from price rises.
Inflation has consequences for people and firms in their roles as lenders, borrowers, wage-earners, taxpayers, and consumers.

learning_objectives: Calculate the annual rate of inflation
Explain and use index numbers and base years when simplifying the total quantity spent over a year for products
Calculate inflation rates using index numbers

knowledge_units: Inflation refers to a general rise in the level of all prices in an economy.
The inflation rate is calculated as the percentage change in the price level.
Economists combine prices of various goods and services into a single price level to measure inflation.
Inflation affects not only goods and services but also wages and income levels.
Economists calculate the price level using a basket of goods and services that represents typical purchases.
A weighted average of prices is used to construct an overall price level, where weights are based on actual quantities purchased.
Changes in prices of goods with larger budget shares have a greater impact on the overall price level.
The annual rate of inflation is calculated as the percentage change in the total cost of purchasing a basket of goods between time periods.
The percentage change formula is (new level minus previous level) divided by previous level, multiplied by 100.
Economists report price levels as index numbers to simplify interpretation of complex baskets of goods.
The base year is arbitrarily chosen and assigned an index number of 100.
Index numbers are calculated by dividing the dollar amount for a year by the base year amount and multiplying by 100.
Index numbers have no units because dollar signs cancel out in the calculation.
Inflation rates can be calculated using the percentage change formula on index numbers.
Index numbers allow easier eyeballing of inflation rates compared to absolute dollar amounts.
The inflation rate is not derived by subtracting index numbers but by the percentage-change calculation.
Index numbers mirror proportions in data and transform it for easier use.
The choice of base year is arbitrary and does not affect calculated inflation rates.

learning_objectives: Use the Consumer Price Index (CPI) to calculate U.S. inflation rates
Identify several ways the Bureau of Labor Statistics avoids biases in the Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Differentiate among the Consumer Price Index (CPI), the Producer Price Index (PPI), the International Price Index, the Employment Cost Index, the GDP deflator, and the Personal Consumption Expenditure price index.

knowledge_units: The Consumer Price Index (CPI) is the most commonly cited measure of inflation in the United States.
The CPI is calculated based on the prices in a fixed basket of goods and services representing the purchases of an average family of four.
The change in the total cost of a fixed basket of goods over time is not the same as the change in the cost of living.
Substitution bias occurs because a fixed basket does not account for consumers substituting away from goods whose relative prices have risen, overstating the true cost of living increase.
Quality/new goods bias occurs because a fixed basket does not account for improvements in quality or the introduction of new goods, overstating the true cost of living increase.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) uses the Consumer Expenditure Survey to determine a representative basket of goods for the CPI.
The CPI basket includes about 80,000 specific products across over 200 broad-item categories.
The BLS collects price data monthly from about 23,000 stores in 87 urban areas.
The CPI is calculated by combining prices of individual products using weights based on quantities purchased, allowing for substitution and quality improvements.
The eight major categories in the CPI are food and beverages, housing, apparel, transportation, medical care, recreation, education and communication, and other goods and services.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) measures prices as they affect everyday household spending.
Economists calculate a core inflation index by taking the CPI and excluding volatile economic variables like energy and food prices.
The core inflation index provides a better guide to monetary policy by measuring persistent underlying inflation rather than transitory influences.
The CPI helps households understand their overall cost of living, while the core inflation index is preferred for government policy decisions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics uses alternative mathematical methods and more frequent updates to the CPI basket to allow for substitution between goods and to include new and improved goods more rapidly.
The BLS conducts studies to measure quality improvements, such as adjusting computer prices for changes in speed, memory, and screen size, though these adjustments are imperfect and controversial.
Since the early 2000s, adjustments have reduced substitution and quality/new goods biases, so the CPI overstates true inflation by about 0.5% per year, which compounds significantly over decades.
A tradeoff exists between simplicity and accuracy: a fixed basket is straightforward but suffers from biases, while an evolving basket reduces biases but increases calculation complexity.
The Consumer Price Index (CPI) represents an average hypothetical U.S. household's consumption and may not capture inflation experienced by specific demographic or geographic groups.
The Bureau of Labor Statistics publishes experimental price indices for specific groups, geographic areas, or categories of goods to address limitations of the CPI.
The Producer Price Index (PPI) measures prices paid for supplies and inputs by producers and can be broken down by industry, commodity, or stage of processing.
The International Price Index is based on prices of exported or imported merchandise.
The Employment Cost Index measures wage inflation in the labor market.
The GDP deflator is a price index that includes all GDP components (consumption, investment, government, and net exports).
The Personal Consumption Expenditure (PCE) price index is the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation measure and uses baskets that are re-calculated annually based on current GDP composition.
The GDP deflator is the most accurate measure of overall inflation but includes prices of goods not purchased by households, making it less suitable for measuring cost of living.
The CPI or PCE price index are better measures of inflation as it impacts households because they only include prices of products purchased by households.

learning_objectives: Identify patterns of inflation for the United States using data from the Consumer Price Index
Identify patterns of inflation on an international level

knowledge_units: In the last three decades, inflation in the U.S. economy has been relatively low, with the Consumer Price Index typically rising 2% to 4% per year.
Over the twentieth century, there have been several periods where inflation caused the price level to rise at double-digit rates, but nothing has come close to hyperinflation.
Inflation in the U.S. reached its highest levels in 1974 and 1980.
Deflation is a period when the buying power of money in terms of goods and services increases.
